title: Dielectric Characterization of Hard Dental Tissues at Microwave Frequencies: Experimental Validation of First-Order Debye Behavior
authors: Farzaneh Afkhami, Syed Akbar Raza Naqvi, Ove A. Peters, Amin Abbosh
publish: IEEE Journal of Electromagnetics, RF and Microwaves in Medicine and Biology
abstract: Accurate dielectric characterization of dental hard tissue is a prerequisite for the development and validation of microwave-based dental diagnostic approaches. Existing studies are limited by small sample sizes or reliance on simulated models, resulting in a lack of reliable experimental data on hard dental tissues. This study reports broadband dielectric measurements of hard dental tissues using 14 bovine teeth, which are widely used as an alternative for human teeth in dental research due to their comparable properties. The characterization was performed over the 1–14 GHz range using an open-ended coaxial probe. Careful sample preparation was applied to ensure accuracy and reproducibility. The results show that the dielectric response of hard dental tissues differs from that of water-rich biological tissues due to their mineral-rich and collagen-based composition. Most importantly, the frequency-dependent behavior of these tissues is accurately described by the first-order Debye model. The frequency-dependent dielectric model reported in this study provides a physically consistent baseline for electromagnetic forward simulations, near-field sensor design optimization, and algorithm development for such sensors.

title: MSCAC-TCAM: An Interpretable Fault Diagnosis Method for Rolling Bearings Based on Multisensor Data Fusion
authors: Yongyong Hui, Yali Sun, Gaolei Mao, Xiaoqiang Zhao
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: In industrial systems, timely and accurate fault diagnosis of rolling bearings is critical to ensuring safe and stable operation of equipment. However, in scenarios with varying noise and varying loads, key fault features in bearing vibration signals are difficult to extract sufficiently, resulting in degraded diagnostic performance and insufficient reliability. To address these issues, an interpretable fault diagnosis method for rolling bearings based on multisensor data fusion is designed, referred to as MSCAC-TCAM. First, a multisensor data adaptive fusion module (MSAF) is designed to perform two-stage fusion on raw multisensor signals using dynamic kurtosis weighting and global channel adaptive calibration, highlighting fault-sensitive signals and suppressing noise components. Second, a multiscale cascaded asymmetric convolution module (MSCAC) is constructed to achieve parallel multiscale hierarchical feature extraction and efficient fusion of the fused signal via cascaded asymmetric convolutions. Third, a temporal channel attention mechanism (TCAM) is proposed to adaptively enhance critical fault moments and discriminative channels in fault features in parallel and collaboratively along both temporal and channel dimensions. Finally, experimental validation is carried out on the Case Western Reserve University (CWRU) bearing dataset and the self-constructed PT890 dataset. Interpretability analysis of the multisensor fusion process and the model’s feature learning mechanism is performed using weight visualization, gradient-weighted class activation mapping (Grad-CAM), and quantitative indicators. The results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior diagnostic performance and robustness, and its interpretability improves the reliability of diagnostic results.

title: Pinching-Antenna System Design under Random LoS and NLoS Channels
authors: Yanqing Xu, Yang Lu, Zhiguo Ding, Tsung-Hui Chang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: Pinching antennas, realized through position-adjustable radiating elements along dielectric waveguides, have emerged as a promising flexible-antenna technology thanks to their ability to dynamically reshape large-scale channel conditions. However, most existing studies focus on idealized LoS-dominated environments, overlooking the stochastic nature of realistic wireless propagation. This paper investigates a more practical multiuser pinching-antenna system under a composite probabilistic channel model that captures distance-dependent LoS blockage and NLoS scattering. To account for both efficiency and reliability aspects of communication, two complementary design metrics are considered: an average signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) metric characterizing long-term link quality and fairness, and an outage-constrained metric ensuring a prescribed reliability level. Based on these metrics, we formulate two optimization problems: the first seeks to maximize the minimum average SNR across users, while the second seeks to maximize a guaranteed SNR threshold subject to per-user outage constraints. Although both problems are inherently nonconvex, we exploit their underlying monotonic structures and develop low-complexity, bisection-based algorithms that achieve globally optimal solutions using only simple scalar evaluations. Extensive simulations validate the effectiveness of the proposed methods and demonstrate that pinching-antenna systems significantly outperform conventional fixed-antenna designs even under random LoS and NLoS channels.

title: Society Officers & Administrative Committee
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Antennas and Propagation Magazine

title: An IRS Assisted Flexible Space Shift Keying Scheme with Pattern Modulation
authors: Qingjiao Xia, Yuyang Peng, Qi Jin, Mohammad Meraj Mirza, Fawaz Al-Hazemi, Juho Lee
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: In this letter, we propose an intelligent reflecting surface (IRS) assisted flexible space shift keying with pattern modulation scheme for high spectral efficient wireless transmission. By designing pattern information into grouped IRS elements and jointly exploiting flexible spatial index modulation, the proposed scheme effectively increases the data rate without requiring additional active radio frequency (RF) chains. A joint maximum likelihood (ML) detector is employed, through which a theoretical bit error rate expression is derived to evaluate the error performance of the proposed scheme. Both theoretical analysis and simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme provides flexible configurability and maintains low detection complexity, while achieving reliable performance in high spectral efficient scenarios.

title: Accurate and Robust: Dynamic Image Refinement for Remote Sensing Visual Grounding
authors: Xiaowen Zhang, Lingling Li, Long Sun, Licheng Jiao, Xu Liu, Fang Liu, Wenping Ma, Shuyuan Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Recent advancements in natural image visual grounding have shown promising results. However, existing approaches struggle with precise grounding in remote sensing (RS) images. This challenge arises from two key issues. First, the prevalence of small objects and dense spatial distributions in RS images makes accurate localization difficult. Second, the large domain gap and lack of contextual priors in RS data hinder language-vision alignment. In this paper, we propose RefineVG, a novel framework for fine-grained visual grounding in high-resolution RS images. Unlike methods that require training separate models for coarse localization and fine-grained refinement, RefineVG employs a unified model that iteratively refines predictions. Specifically, RefineVG first predicts a coarse bounding box using a multi-scale cross-modality encoder and a Transformer-based decoder, which progressively align and fuse visual and textual feature. Then, the predicted region is enlarged to capture additional contextual feature. Combined with only category information as text, the cropped region is then re-encoded by the same model to obtain a refined grounding result. Finally, a fusion block combines the coarse and refined predictions. To support this process, we propose a data augmentation strategy by randomly expanding ground truth bounding boxes in training dataset, simulating spatial uncertainty.Extensive experiment results show that RefineVG achieves outstanding performance on three benchmarks, outperforming the second-best method by 5.11% on the DIOR-RSVG dataset, 8.61% on the OPT-RSVG dataset, and 3.61% on the RSVG-HR dataset in P@0.9. Our code are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/XiaowZhang00/RefineVG</uri>.

title: “The Last Human Will See What We Saw”: The Test of the World's First Atomic Bomb Left its Observers Stupefied
authors: Emily Seyl
publish: IEEE Spectrum
abstract: In the North 10,000 photography bunker, Berlyn Brixner was listening to the countdown on a loudspeaker, his head inside a turret loaded with cameras and film. He was one of the only people instructed to look toward the blast—through his welder's glasses—ready to follow the path of the fire-ball as it launched into the sky. The two Mitchell movie cameras at his station would deliver the best footage to come out of the Trinity test, used by Los Alamos scientists to make some of the first measurements of the effects of a nuclear explosion.

title: Theoretical Model for the Rate-Constrained Multichannel Wiener Filter in Binaural Hearing Aid Applications
authors: Vitor Gelsleicher Probst Curtarelli, Márcio Holsbach Costa
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Binaural hearing aids (HAs) are embedded battery powered electronic systems that compensate for bilateral hearing loss. A key advantage of using intercommunicating devices over two independent HAs is their enhanced noise reduction capability, thereby improving speech intelligibility and acoustic comfort. This improvement is achieved by exchanging information between the right and left HAs via a wireless link. This paper proposes a novel theoretical model for predicting the noise-reduction performance of binaural HAs when the wireless communication link is subject to arbitrary bit-rate constraints implemented via quantization operations. It provides a valuable tool for hearing aid designers to understand and configure the parameters necessary to achieve desired performance levels. This is particularly relevant in low-battery scenarios, where bit-rate communication is decreased to avoid premature device shutdown. The model's performance was assessed using objective intelligibility and quality metrics and compared with those obtained from predictions using the widely used Widrow's model, which approximates quantization effects as independent, uniformly distributed additive noise. The modeling errors for both approaches were calculated against results from Monte Carlo simulations. Computational simulations provide strong evidence that the proposed model yields lower intelligibility and quality prediction errors. This allows more accurate modeling of the impact of rate-constrained wireless communication on binaural HAs, and provides more accurate predictions of the trade-off between noise-reduction performance and the quantization of the transmitted information, which affects battery lifetime.

title: Beyond One-Layer Decision: Hierarchical Multi-view Clustering with Progressive Cross-Layer Fusion
authors: Kaixuan Zhou, Pengyuan Li, Jiahui Zhang, Dongxia Chang, Yiming Wang, Yao Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Deep multi-view clustering has demonstrated remarkable efficacy by leveraging the powerful feature extraction capabilities of autoencoders. However, existing autoencoder-based methods typically rely solely on final-layer embeddings of the encoder for downstream tasks, ignoring rich hierarchical information embedded in intermediate layers. To address this, we propose a novel Hierarchical-Aware Multi-view Clustering (HAMVC) framework that integrates complementarity across layers to promote intra-cluster compactness and inter-cluster separability. Specifically, we design a cross-layer affinity propagation module that implements a shallow-to-deep refinement strategy. By leveraging a multi-step diffusion mechanism, this module progressively integrates fine-grained structural patterns from shallow layers into semantic-rich deep features, establishing a robust progressive refinement path. Furthermore, to regulate interactions among heterogeneous views, we introduce a manifold-invariant diversity learning module. This module enforces a decentralized alignment strategy to capture the shared intrinsic manifold across views, while simultaneously imposing a diversity constraint to preserve view-specific complementary information and prevent representation collapse. Extensive experiments on several real-world datasets demonstrate that HAMVC achieves substantial improvements over state-of-the-art methods.

title: An Overview of Microwave Two-Port Measurement Methods for Absorber Materials
authors: Mehmet Faruk Cengiz, Robert Vogt-Ardatjew, Niek Moonen, Frank Leferink
publish: IEEE Letters on Electromagnetic Compatibility Practice and Applications
abstract: Electromagnetic absorber materials are commonly characterized using two-port measurement techniques at microwave frequencies. These techniques directly measure complex S-parameters. Other quantities, such as material properties including relative permittivity and relative permeability, reflectivity, and absorption cross section, are obtained from these measurements. Different measurement methods describe absorber behavior at different levels. The relations between these levels are based on models and assumptions, which are not always accurate. Some measurement methods allow material-level information to be translated into higher level performance metrics through modeling. Other methods directly provide higher level quantities but do not provide detailed material specific information. Understanding these differences is essential for correct interpretation of measurement results. This paper provides an overview of common two-port measurement methods and explains what each method actually measures. Practical aspects such as calibration requirements, sample preparation, usable frequency range, setup complexity, and measurement challenges are briefly discussed. The main advantages and limitations of each measurement method are highlighted to support engineers in selecting the most appropriate approach for their specific application.

title: Diverse-Input Yield Prediction (DIYP) Model Applied Across Different Years with Variable Input Days
authors: Yen-Jen Chang, Ming-Hsin Lai, Yu-Dian Chen, Chien-Ho Wang, Yu-Shun Huang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The drone-collected multispectral data are useful in agriculture applications, particularly for crop yield prediction. However, the drone flying is often impacted by weather conditions, resulting in data miss or inaccurate information. In this paper, we propose a new rice yield prediction model, named Diverse-Input Yield Prediction (DIYP), which is based on the LSTM network and further enhanced with attention mechanisms and an encoder structure to address the varied input and cross-year prediction challenges. Our model can reduce the impact of missing data, and improve the yield prediction accuracy across different years. All rice datasets are provided by Taiwan Agricultural Research Institute, which consist of multispectral vegetation index data collected by drones. We built our DIYP model using only the 2020 dataset. The experimental results show that the proposed DIYP model outperforms state-of-the-art approaches across multiple metrics, achieving significant performance gains for both the 2021 and 2022 datasets. For the 2021 dataset, the RMSE decreases by 9-15%, while the ACC increases by 9-13%. Similarly, for the 2022 dataset, the RMSE is reduced by 5-23%, and the ACC is improved by 9%. Furthermore, our model achieves Kappa coefficients of 0.83 and 0.84 for 2021 and 2022, respectively, indicating strong agreement with ground truth measurements. In addition to conventional performance metrics, RMSE-based model selection criteria, including AIC, SBIC, HQIC, and AICc, were incorporated to provide a more rigorous comparison among the related models. The information-criterion-based results further confirm that DIYPv3 provides a favorable balance between prediction accuracy and model complexity.

title: A Comparative Study of Attention-Based Transformer and Deep CNN Architectures for Lithography Hotspot Detection in Advanced Process Nodes
authors: Krishnananda Shet, Manjunath Subramanyam, R. Manjunatha, S Ganesh, Mahesh Koti, Kumar N. Krishna Murthy
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: As VLSI technology continuously expands, especially below the 10-nm node, lithography-related process variations have become a major issue in semiconductor manufacturing. Consequently, traditional physics-based verification methods are becoming less practical for full-chip analysis, mainly due to their heavy computational demands and timing constraints. We explore the deep learning techniques for lithography hotspot detection in this work. Two architectures are examined: a Deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a lightweight Vision Transformer (ViT). Both models were trained and tested on the same dataset of layout clips. The input data is represented using a four-channel lithography-aware format that includes both intensity and gradient details to better capture the layout characteristics. The results of the experiment shows that theCNNhas an accuracy of 97.7%. TheVision Transformer gets a lower accuracy than CNN, but needs only 27,809 trainable parameters which makes it considerably more lightweight. Its self-attention mechanism allows it to learn long-range spatial relationships between layout features. CNNs tend to focus on local feature extraction while Transformer-based models are better suited for pulling in broader spatial context from the layout. As advanced process nodes increasingly exhibit non-local printing effects driven by optical proximity and complex process interactions, models capable of capturing distant feature relationships become particularly valuable. The compact size, scalability, and contextual reasoning capability of Transformer-based approaches indicate strong potential for deployment in next-generation Electronic Design Automation workflows focused on Design for Manufacturability optimization.

title: Idle Nodes Detection and Reactivation Aware-Buffer Management in Delay Tolerant Network Using Iemata and Spko
authors: Ajay Prasad, Sandeep Gurung, Kalpana Sharma
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: In Delay Tolerant Networks (DTNs), buffer management is crucial for optimized message delivery by focusing on message storage and prioritization. However, existing studies do not determine the state of a node, leading to buffer space wastage. Therefore, this paper presents a buffer management framework based on Idle Node Detection and Reactivation in DTN. Initially, nodes are initialized in DTN, followed by assessment of Time To Live (TTL) and message expiry detection. Non-expired and new messages are then load-balanced using Kolmogorov-Smirnov Logarithmic Jaynes K-Means (KSLJK-Means). Subsequently, the load-balanced messages are signed with the Digital Logarithmic Double Hashing Probing Signature Algorithm (DLDHPSA). Before message transmission, the idle/active state of nodes is identified using the Idle Exponential Moving Average Timer Algorithm (IEMATA). If a node is idle, it is activated via periodic beaconing. Next, messages are prioritized using Lorentzian Raised Cosine Fuzzy (LRC-Fuzzy). Afterward, messages are allocated to buffers following successful digital signature verification. Buffer utilization rates are estimated to determine available space; if space is available, messages are stored in the buffer. If the buffer is full, messages are dropped according to the Message Utility Score (MUS) based drop policy. The Shubert Pied Kingfisher Optimizer (SPKO) is employed for buffer allocation. The proposed model achieves a low buffer allocation time of 2165 ms. It offers significant benefits when deployed in satellite communications, vehicular networks, and IoT systems. Overall, the model provides resource-centric solutions to enhance the reliability of DTN environments.

title: PROMEOS: Protein-protein interaction prediction using a Mixture of Experts and multimodal learning by integrating Ontology and Sequences
authors: Jae-Woo Chu, Jong-Hoon Park, Gwang-Hyeon Yun, Young-Rae Cho
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Protein–protein interactions (PPIs) are fundamental to numerous biological processes, yet their experimental identification remains costly and time-consuming. Deep learning has emerged as a promising approach for PPI prediction, but many existing methods struggle to achieve high accuracy due to insufficiently leveraging complementary modalities. In addition, they face limitations in generalizing beyond specific experimental datasets and encounter challenges in training due to the large scale of PPI data. To address these challenges, we propose PROMEOS, PPI prediction using a mixture of experts and multimodal learning by integrating ontology and sequences. Across diverse experimental settings, PROMEOS outperformed strong baselines, achieving relative gains of 2.4% on the yeast dataset and 2.02% on the human dataset over the second-best model. Moreover, it achieved a 6.94% improvement in cross-species prediction and a 7.9% increase in prediction accuracy during confidence-threshold transfer evaluation. While more than doubling the parameter count with sparse mixture of experts, inference time increased by only 3.5%, and removing the decoder reduced training time approximately 10%. Overall, PROMEOS provides an effective solution for PPI prediction in terms of accuracy, applicability, and efficiency.

title: EEG-Based ADHD Classification Using Machine Learning and Boosting Algorithms
authors: Debasish Phukan, Daisy Das, Nabamita Deb
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) is a common neurodevelopmental disorder, and EEG-based biomarkers may help support clinical diagnosis. In this study, EEG data from 61 children with ADHD and 60 healthy controls (ages 7–12) were analyzed using multiple machine learning models. The signals were preprocessed using bandpass filtering (1–40 Hz), 50 Hz notch filtering, standardization, and ICA. Features were extracted using spectral and nonlinear methods, including band power, Katz fractal dimension, detrended fluctuation analysis, and Lempel–Ziv complexity. Twelve classifiers were evaluated using an 80:20 stratified train–test split, where 80% of the data were used for training and 20% for testing and performance was measured using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC. Among all methods, boosting models performed best, with XGBoost achieving the highest accuracy (92.81%), followed by Gradient Boosting (92.69%). KNN (90.30%) was the strongest traditional model, while Gaussian Naïve Bayes showed the lowest performance (51.02%), likely due to its simplifying feature-independence assumption. Overall, boosting models showed strong potential, but testing on larger and more diverse datasets is still needed.

title: Applications and Limitations of Large Language Models in Digital Forensics: A Survey
authors: Skyler Ashton Saunders, Mohamed I. Ibrahem, Mahmoud Abouyoussef
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Digital forensics (DF) plays a critical role in investigating and analyzing digital evidence across criminal and civil cases. Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have introduced new opportunities to support forensic workflows by automating or assisting tasks such as dataset generation, evidence extraction, analysis, reporting, and investigative support. Despite growing interest in this area, existing studies remain fragmented and lack a comprehensive examination of how LLMs are applied in digital forensics, as well as their associated limitations. This survey systematically reviews recent research on the use of LLMs in digital forensics, categorizing current applications and summarizing representative techniques and outcomes. In addition, the paper analyzes key challenges, including hallucinations, data privacy, explainability, performance cost, and legal admissibility, that currently limit real-world adoption. Finally, the survey outlines open research problems and future directions necessary to enable trustworthy and effective integration of LLMs into digital forensic investigations.

title: Chennamane: The Historiography, Gameplay, and Cultural Revival of an Obscure Folk Game from Southern India
authors: Gurunandan G R, Devika Udupa V, Bharath S Bhat, Prasanna Kumar, Clion Franstin Miranda, Gururaj K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: A classic board game from South India, chennemane also known as Mancala game, is frequently played with cowrie shells and wooden boards. The goal of this project is to use the game engine known as Unity to convey the cultural significance and strategic depth of Chennemane towards the realm of the web. Our goal in digitising this beloved game is to uphold its legacy.While enabling a worldwide audience to look at it. Creating an intuitive user experience with intricate visuals and animations while maintaining the genuine feel of an actual physical game is a key component of the game creation process. We employ gaming mechanisms that adhere closely to conventional guidelines, guaranteeing a genuine gameplay experience. The game will also allow players to play with others on the precise same device in both local person vs. player and single-player modes against AI.

title: Hair Baldness Progression Prediction Using XGBoost: Risk Forecasting Model
authors: Mark Maben, Arhath Kumar, Balachandra Rao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Hair-loss prediction is increasingly important, as early thinning is strongly influenced by genetic, hormonal, and lifestyle factors. This study presents a machine-learning framework for forecasting hair baldness progression using a structured dataset composed of clinical and behavioral indicators such as age, stress levels, hormonal influence, and family history. Data preprocessing included normalization, missing-value imputation, and categorical encoding. An XGBoost classifier was trained to estimate individual hair-loss risk and evaluated using multiple performance metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, ROC–AUC, and Brier score. The proposed model achieved strong predictive performance, with an accuracy of 90.12 percent and a ROC–AUC of 96.63 percent. Feature-importance analysis and SHAP-based explainability were employed to interpret model decisions, while a five-year risk projection module was developed to estimate future hair-loss progression. The results demonstrate that machine-learning techniques can provide scalable, interpretable, and preventive solutions for early hair-loss risk assessment.

title: Trojan Attribution via Code Stylometry: Writing Behaviors and Coding Habits in Obfuscated Binaries
authors: Khaled Saleh, Omar Darwish, Fathi Amsaad
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Attributing obfuscated Trojans to authors remains challenging because transformation obscures structure. We investigate whether code authorship—inferred from writing behaviors and coding style—survives obfuscation in Trojan binaries. Using a survey-aligned taxonomy (lexical, syntactic, semantic), we run an attribution pipeline on 440 samples (224 original, 216 obfuscated) including Trojans from the Malware Analysis and Obfuscation Dataset. Findings: original samples decompile at 83.5%, obfuscated at 0%; byte n-grams reach 99.3%; lexical evidence dominates (2.42/3 avg) and drops sharply for obfuscated; coding habits (70.2% while vs. 14.8% for) survive compilation but vanish when decompilation fails. For obfuscated Trojans, only byte-level and lexical signals persist—syntactic and semantic collapse. We discuss implications for Trojan attribution under obfuscation.

title: Compact Vision Transformers for BFMRX Bone-Fracture Detection
authors: Jonayed Hossain, Kazi Refat Ullah, Akib Sadman Nafi, Md. Mazenul Islam, Ahmed Faizul Haque Dhrubo, Souvik Pramanik, Mohammad Abdul Qayum
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: In this work, we investigate the influence of transfer learning on compact vision backbones for bone fracture detection across multiple anatomical regions. We use the Bone Fracture Multi-Region X-ray (BFMRX) dataset (10,580 images) and evaluate five backbones (DaViT-Tiny, MViT-v2-Tiny, TinyViT-5M, RepViT-m3, PiT-Ti) under both transfer learning and training from scratch. Transfer learning yielded better validation accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(98.28 \%-99.76 \%)$</tex> and lower loss <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(0.0173-0.0527)$</tex> compared to training from scratch (accuracy: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 0. 1 1 \%} \boldsymbol{-} \mathbf{9 8. 3 1 \%}$</tex>, loss: 0.0504-0.3173). DaViT backbones performed best, with a minimal drop in performance when trained from scratch (1.45 pp) and tight training-validation gaps. MViT-v2 showed strong reliance on pre-training <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(99.52 \% \rightarrow 90.11 \%$</tex> without transfer). Overall, transfer learning proved essential for achieving reliability and efficiency in BFMRX, with DaViT providing the best posttransfer performance for deployment.

title: An AI-Powered Solution for Sustainable Resource Management
authors: Prajwal Hegde N, Y Nidhi Shenoy, Vaishnavi N, R Ajay Prabhu, Abhijna N, Ankith Hebbar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: This paper aims to present an AI-powered energy optimization system designed to provide real-time insights, control, and predictions for residential or community-level energy usage. By integrating edge computing through a Local Backend, the system captures live data from energy monitoring hardware or simulation sources, processes it locally, and communicates with a cloud-based Main Backend using secure API key mechanisms. An LSTM-based AI model is employed to forecast upcoming energy trends, enabling users to make proactive decisions. Users interact with the system via a dynamic web interface that supports device control, dashboard analytics, and a personalized energy assistant chatbot. The IoT module, built using Arduino and PZEM-004T v3 energy meter, allows physical appliances to be monitored and toggled remotely. The system also features Server-Sent Events (SSE) for real-time feedback synchronization between cloud and edge layers. Designed with a human-in-the-loop approach, this solution balances automation with user oversight, making it scalable, efficient, and adaptable for modern smart home or gated community environments.

title: Spatio-Visual Helmet Violation Analytics Using YOLOv8
authors: S Sapthami, Prathwini
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Artificial intelligence is bringing a significant change in the area of traffic surveillance for automatic detection, real-time monitoring, and intelligent enforcement of traffic regulations. In this work, a motorcyclist detection system along with a no-helmet detection system is proposed using the YOLOv8 object detection technique, demonstrating the automatic detection of two-wheeler riders violating helmet laws while riding two-wheelers on the road. Basically, the aim of the proposed system is to enhance the safety features on the roads concerning traffic accidents caused by violations of traffic laws. Moreover, the proposed system makes use of advanced deep learning models for the detection system along with accurate inspection for violation detection with real-time processing. A customized dataset with images for both helmet-wearing and no-helmet-wearing riders is prepared for testing purposes. Also, the system is capable of working in different environments with varying illumination conditions along with different densities on the road. Open CV is employed for accurate detection of violations within video frames. Moreover, a simplified Streamlit interface is available for easy uploading, live detection, and automatic violation detection on the video upload system. Experimental results show that the proposed system achieves an mAP@0.5 score of 93% with high recall, demonstrating its effectiveness for real world traffic surveillance applications.

title: The Final Exam? [Microwave Surfing]
authors: Rajeev Bansal
publish: IEEE Microwave Magazine

title: A Comparative Framework for Automatic Image Annotation Using CNN Architectures and CLIP-ViT
authors: Tejas Jain, Jay Soni, Sumith N, Ashwath Rao B
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: With the exponential growth of images and videos in recent years, manual annotation has become one of the most time-consuming and costly tasks in computer vision. Automatic annotation is designed to reduce human labour by proposing useful labels suggested by deep learning and vision-language models. In this work, we provide a usecase for deep learning models such as classic convolutional neural networks (CNNs) models (ResNet50, Xception, and InceptionV3) to the multimodal CLIP-ViT-B/32 model for auto annotation task. Our work employs established feature extractors as pre-trained models, and several different classifiers such as Support Vector Machines (SVM), XGBoost, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Logistic Regression, and Random Forest. All the models were studied for Fashion Product Images dataset. The findings reveal that the CLIP-based features along with a SVM classifier are the most accurate and consistent in predicting the power of vision-language alignment in the auto-labeling of data. The study also leads to the further development of scalable, high-precision, and cost-effective labeling systems in the current computer vision tasks.

title: ESP32-Based Wi-Fi Signal Mapping and Machine Learning–Driven Access Point Optimization
authors: Tanya Mendez, Shreyas S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: The quality of the signal in an indoor environment highly depends on the optimal placement of access points (APs). Suboptimal positioning can lead to coverage gaps, reduced bandwidth, user dissatisfaction and degraded signal strength. This paper presents the design and development of a low-cost, portable ESP32-based device for spatial Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI) acquisition, integrated with a Flask analytics framework that performs heatmap visualization and machine–learning–driven AP placement recommendations. RSSI measurements are logged in real-time to Google Sheets using HTTP requests and later processed using K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) regression for signal interpolation and K-Means clustering for candidate AP positioning. The collected data is integrated into a Flask-based web application, enabling visualization through heatmaps and incorporating a machine learning model to recommend optimal AP placement. Quantitative evaluation using R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, RMSE, and coverage-gain metrics demonstrates that the ML-assisted placement improves predicted weak-zone coverage by 18–26% compared to manual placement. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed system offers a cost-effective and practical solution for residential and small-office Wi-Fi planning, serving as an affordable alternative to commercial site survey tools.

title: SecureHLSMem: Synthesizable Memory Safety for High-Level Synthesis of Accelerators
authors: Md Mahfuzul Haque Gazi, M Shifat Hossain, Arif Hussain, Sumit Kumar Jha, Md Rubel Ahmed
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: High-Level Synthesis (HLS) compiles C/C++ kernels into RTL for FPGA and SoC accelerators, but unsafe C/C++ memory semantics, such as out-of-bounds accesses, null dereferences, and overflowed indices, can be synthesized into hardware without any runtime exception mechanism. These violations cause silent data corruption, invalid control-state transitions, or unsafe memory-mapped transactions during FPGA execution. This paper presents SecureHLSMem, an end-to-end memorysafety framework for HLS that combines (i) lightweight compiletime hazard detection, (ii) a synthesizable bounds-checking unit (BCU) that enforces spatial safety at runtime, and (iii) a dynamic sanitization mode that records violations for diagnosis in simulation and on FPGAs. Across representative DSP and ML kernels with seeded vulnerabilities, SecureHLSMem detects all injected hazards and prevents silent corruption, incurring only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 - 4 \%}$</tex> LUT overhead on average with no observed frequency degradation.

title: CROSS-Sched: A Crossover-Aware ML Scheduler and its Benchmarking on Heterogeneous Systems
authors: Abhay Bhandarkar, Mallegowda M
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Modern computing systems increasingly feature heterogeneous architectures combining multicore CPUs with GPU accelerators, yet effectively distributing workloads across these diverse processing units remains challenging. This paper presents a comprehensive benchmarking study of machine learning-based adaptive scheduling for image convolution operations across CPU and GPU resources. We evaluate 96 experimental configurations spanning four diverse datasets with image sizes ranging from 28×28 to 224×224 pixels on a production system featuring dual Intel Xeon processors and an NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPU. Our RandomForest-based scheduler learns optimal device and thread count selection from historical performance data. Results demonstrate a critical performance crossover point: CPU-based OpenMP parallelization achieves superior performance for small images (up to 1.76× faster than GPU), while GPU acceleration dominates for larger images (11.80× speedup over sequential, 2.15× faster than best CPU). The adaptive scheduler achieves competitive performance with an 8.8% average gap to optimal, successfully navigating the CPU-GPU performance tradeoff without manual tuning. This work provides reference benchmarks and a complete reproducible artifact for future heterogeneous scheduling research.

title: Voice- and Text-Based Tasking for Swarm Robotics: A Leader-Centric Coordination Framework
authors: Qurat Ul Ain Zam Zam Mirza, Rimsha Kanwal, Humaira Ashraf, NZ. Jhanjhi, Adrian Reza
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Natural language interfaces provide a promising future of simplifying human-swarm interaction by enabling the operator to make high-level commands without the use of lowlevel code or special control panels. Nevertheless, the implementation of multi-robot systems with linguistic communications will pose the difficulties in understanding the commands, real-time dispersing, and reliability of executing tasks simultaneously. In this paper, we give a leaderfollower swarm control architecture where a single leader robot is able to receive spoken or textual instructions and broadcast structured tasks to a group of follower robots via a simple messaging protocol. The system was simulated and tested in Webots with a four-robot system configuration with natural language parsing and pursuit-based and a particle swarm optimization (PSO) search behavior. The experimental data show that the quality of interpretation of the commands can always be high when it concerns the typical operation instructions, whereas the leader-swarm command propagation can have a latency of sub-seconds. The results show that battlefield navigation, formation and search testing can be carried out reliably by swarm agents with minimum positional drift and with stable acknowledgment reporting and this can prove that commands issued in a natural language can be commanded directly to swarm architectures with minimal effect on swarm coordination performance. The presented system supports the viability of the humanswarm-teaming concept and provides it with a basis in the scalable natural-language-powered robots of the future.

title: Counterfeit RF Fingerprint Detection Using an Autoencoder-Based Approach
authors: Carlos Mex-Perera
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Radio frequency (RF) fingerprints, used for the authentication of wireless transmitters at the physical layer, can be targeted by adversaries seeking to create counterfeit fingerprints to impersonate legitimate devices. In this paper, we consider a threat model in which a rogue device attempts to replicate the in-phase/quadrature (I/Q) imbalance of a legitimate LoRa transmitter to produce a counterfeit RF fingerprint. We address the detection of such attacks using an anomaly detection approach. Specifically, we propose a one-class machine learning model based on a convolutional autoencoder (AE) network. As a baseline, detection performance is also evaluated using a one-class support vector machine (SVM). The performance of both methods is assessed under multiple signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions. The results show that the autoencoder consistently outperforms the SVM across all evaluated scenarios, indicating the robustness of the RF fingerprinting approach under the considered attack model.

title: Disaster Connect: A Unified Platform for Disaster Response and Relief using MobileNetV2 and BERT
authors: Babitha Ganesh, Ambika Hebbar, Apeksha Shetty, B M Samruddhi Rai, B Thrishali Gowda
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Natural disasters continue to disrupt communities all over the world, creating an urgent need for faster, smarter, and more coordinated response systems. We address this problem by proposing Disaster Connect, a unified mobile application that will provide real-time alerts, prioritize SOS messages using Artificial Intelligence (AI), and provide safe evacuation routes during emergencies. The system fuses a BERT-based text classifier and a MobileNetV2-based image classifier, separately trained on labeled SOS messages and a publicly available disaster image dataset, to decide the urgency of the distress input. On the experimental dataset, the text classifier achieved 93% accuracy, the image classifier reached 82.59% accuracy. The application also integrates official APIs for the monitoring of earthquakes, wildfires, cyclones, floods along with other disasters and leverages Google Maps Directions API to develop dynamic evacuation routes tailored to the user’s location. These results illuminate how Disaster Connect enhances situational awareness, supports the effective prioritization of emergency requests, and increases the speed and safety of evacuations, making it a practical and scalable solution for real-world disaster response.

title: Survey on Machine Learning and Deep Learning Techniques for Stutter Detection
authors: Jyothi V Prasad, Khushi Malli, Sanjana Nayak, Charan R. Karkera, Thrisha Shetty
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Automatic stuttering detection has become a major focus in speech technology, integrating advancements in signal processing, machine learning (ML), and deep learning (DL) for reliable fluency assessment. Stuttering and disfluencies such as repetitions, prolongations, and blocks affect speech intelligibility, while manual identification by speech language pathologists remains subjective and labor intensive. This survey analyzes current ML and DL based stutter detection methods, highlighting feature extraction approaches, datasets, model architectures, evaluation metrics, and research challenges. The review traces the shift from traditional ML classifiers to modern deep neural and multimodal models, noting limitations including data scarcity and poor cross domain generalization. Traditional ML relies on handcrafted acoustic and prosodic features, pitch, energy, spectral envelopes, and temporal cues paired with SVMs, HMMs, or GMMs, but these methods struggle with feature engineering and nonlinear speech patterns. DL based architectures such as CNNs, RNNs, LSTMs, and transformer models overcome these constraints by capturing complex disfluency characteristics and long range temporal dependencies, leading to significant improvements in automatic stutter detection performance.

title: Advanced Smart Accident Detection System with IoT and AI Integration
authors: Apeksha L Naik, Chaithanya, Kshama S, Meghana
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Road accidents are one of the leading causes of death worldwide, and delays in medical response often make the situation far more severe. This project aims to develop a smart road accident risk detection and alert system that identifies potential accident scenarios based on sudden impact and abnormal vehicle tilt patterns using an MPU-6050 sensor. The system is designed to automatically notify medical centers and registered contacts in real time, thereby improving the timeliness of emergency assistance.When the vehicle detects motion patterns that may indicate a high-risk event, an alarm is activated for a predefined duration to allow manual cancellation, thereby reducing false alerts. If no response is received within this interval, the system automatically transmits location information to emergency services using GSM and GPS modules. A mobile application further notifies registered users to accelerate response efforts.Ultimately, the proposed system aims to reduce response times during high-risk driving incidents that may lead to accidents, improve situational awareness, facilitate timely medical assistance, and contribute to saving lives.

title: A Global Perspective on Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities: Insights and Future Directions
authors: Sreekantha Desai Karanam, R.S. Kamath, Sampath Kini K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities (IDDs) represent a significant public health challenge, affecting cognitive, behavioral, and adaptive functioning across diverse populations worldwide. This paper presents a comprehensive global perspective on the prevalence, determinants, and societal impact of IDDs, drawing on epidemiological studies, healthcare data, and policy frameworks. Key insights include regional disparities in access to diagnostic and therapeutic services, the influence of socioeconomic and environmental factors on developmental outcomes, and emerging trends in early intervention and inclusive education. Furthermore, the paper identifies critical research gaps and proposes future directions for policy, clinical practice, and technological innovation to enhance the quality of life for individuals with IDDs. By integrating cross-cultural and interdisciplinary perspectives, this study aims to inform evidence-based strategies and promote equitable healthcare and social inclusion for affected populations globally.

title: Federated Learning for Human Activity Recognition on Resource-Constrained Sensors
authors: Claudio N. Silva, Pedro A. Torres, Alex F. R. Trajano, João Pedro B. Lima, Cassio V. S. Prazeres
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: We present TinyFL-HAR, a lightweight and synchronous federated learning (FL) framework for on-device human activity recognition (HAR) on resource-constrained sensors. Our framework performs complete training, local evaluation, and binary model serialization directly on ESP32 microcontrollers using real-world data from the WISDM dataset, and is further validated on the PAMAP2 dataset. We conducted four experimental scenarios by varying the neural network architecture, using two fully connected variants with 2,478 and 35,422 trainable parameters, and the number of clients (5 vs. 10), repeating each configuration across three runs. The 35k–5 Devices configuration achieved the highest accuracy (0.98), while the 2k–10 Devices configuration delivered the best recall and F1-score among the compact configurations, with training time under 100 ms. Experiments on PAMAP2 under the same four configurations provide additional evidence of robustness across datasets. In contrast, the 35k–10 Devices configuration required over 200 ms per round and substantially reduced heap memory. These findings highlight key trade-offs in model capacity, scalability, and runtime efficiency, supporting the feasibility of FL-based HAR in highly constrained IoT environments.

title: Robust Stability for a Class of Complex Balanced Chemical Reaction Networks with Time Delays and Time Varying Perturbations
authors: Hirokazu Komatsu
publish: IEEE Control Systems Letters
abstract: In the present paper, we give the global robust stability for a class of complex balanced chemical reaction networks satisfying the conditions of the Deficiency Zero Theorem, subjected to both arbitrary time delays and time-varying perturbations. We prove that if the system possesses no boundary equilibria and the time-varying perturbations are bounded and asymptotically decay to zero, any positive solution to the non-autonomous delay differential equation converges to a unique positive complex balanced equilibrium point of the unperturbed autonomous system, determined by the corresponding conservation quantities. To achieve this, we formulate a non-autonomous Lyapunov-Krasovskii functional and utilize Barbalat’s lemma alongside the invariant properties of the ω-limit set. Finally, we provide a biochemical example of a reversible competitive inhibition network to demonstrate the validity and applicability of our theoretical results.

title: OCDS: Consortium Blockchain-Empowered Oblivious and Consistent Data Asset Sharing for Internet of Vehicles
authors: Saiyu Qi, Ke Li, Wei Wei, Xu Yang
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The Internet of Vehicles (IoV) produces massive volumes of vehicular data rich in traffic patterns, driving behavior, and location intelligence, rendering it a valuable asset for transportation systems, urban planning, and commercial applications, thus necessitating secure and efficient data asset sharing. However, existing vehicular data sharing solutions do not protect data access patterns, risking privacy leaks. In this paper, we propose OCDS, a consortium blockchain-empowered oblivious and consistent data asset sharing for IoV. OCDS enables vehicles to write and read data assets in an oblivious way by introducing BdcORAM, which employs a tree-structured world state and implements a two-phase oblivious write protocol alongside a consistent oblivious read protocol. Additionally, we further design two optimized variants of BdcORAM to optimize performance for unbalanced read-write workloads. Through comprehensive security analyses and experimental evaluation, OCDS demonstrates it achieves data confidentiality, access anonymity, and secure read consistency without incurring significant performance overhead.

title: DynaTrack: Dynamic Memory-Enhanced Transformer with Deformable Attention for Robust Single Object Tracking
authors: K S Sachin Sakthi, Jae Hoon Jeong, Woo Young Choi
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Single Object Tracking (SOT) remains a fundamental yet challenging problem in computer vision, particularly under real-world conditions involving occlusion, background clutter, scale variation, and abrupt appearance changes. Although transformer-based approaches have recently advanced the field, they are often hindered by high computational cost and limited ability to preserve long-term target appearance. To address these challenges, we propose DynaTrack, a dynamic memory–augmented transformer framework that unifies robust representation learning, adaptive memory modeling, and efficient attention mechanisms. A hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)–Transformer backbone integrates local spatial features with global semantic context through hierarchical fusion, enhancing target discrimination in complex environments. A confidence-gated dynamic memory bank selectively stores reliable target embeddings and suppresses corrupted templates, thereby improving resilience to occlusion and long-term appearance variation. Multi-scale deformable cross-attention efficiently correlates template, search, and memory features by attending to salient regions, reducing computational complexity without compromising accuracy. Finally, a unified prediction head jointly estimates bounding boxes, occlusion states, and confidence scores, ensuring consistent and interpretable tracking outcomes. Extensive experiments on well-known benchmarks show that DynaTrack achieves state-of-the-art results in terms of both accuracy and robustness.

title: Fragmented Graph Pattern Matching on Large Graphs
authors: Faming Li, Shengli Qiu, Xiaochun Yang, Bin Wang, Jianzhong Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: The corpus of knowledge that we have amassed is often fragmented in nature. That is, we only possess a mere fraction of understanding regarding various objects and lack a holistic view of the interconnections between them. Graph, a widely used model to represent data and relationships between data, encompasses significant amounts of fragmented knowledge. In this paper, we study fragmented graph pattern matching (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathsf {FPM}$</tex-math></inline-formula> for short) on large graphs, a typical problem of fragmented knowledge management. Given several query graphs, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathsf {FPM}$</tex-math></inline-formula> searches for matches whose subgraphs are isomorphic to the given query graphs and these subgraphs are connected in a specified way. <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathsf {FPM}$</tex-math></inline-formula> plays a crucial role in detecting financial crime, identifying abnormal behavior and mining latent knowledge. We propose an algorithm <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathsf {FraMatch}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, which adopts a traversal-based manner to adaptively identify matches for the given query graphs and enumerates the interconnections among these matches. To improve the efficiency of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathsf {FraMatch}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, an index, represented as a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math></inline-formula>-partite graph, is constructed to help organize the candidate matches. Additionally, several methods, including four filtering strategies, parallel optimization and a dynamic loading strategy, are proposed to further accelerate the algorithm and reduce memory usage. We conducted extensive experiments on 4 real graphs to validate <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathsf {FraMatch}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. The results demonstrate that <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathsf {FraMatch}$</tex-math></inline-formula> outperforms its comparisons 2000× in running time and reduces memory usage by 8% on average. A real application on anti-money laundering further verifies the effectiveness of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathsf {FPM}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study on <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathsf {FPM}$</tex-math></inline-formula> on large graphs.

title: Expertise and Information Seeking in the Age of Generative AI: New Procedures, New Problematics
authors: Shannon Lodoen, Alexi Orchard
publish: IEEE Transactions on Technology and Society
abstract: As generative AI chatbots reroute procedures around information seeking, challenges increase with assessing and implementing relevant source material. Interactions between users and chatbots differ from prior paradigms of information seeking such as retrieval and archival systems (search engines, library catalogs, and physical stacks). AI chatbots summarize information probabilistically through dialogic interactions, leveraging natural language signifiers of expertise and intelligence to influence users’ perception of their trustworthiness. This new mode of information seeking could present significant risks for users who engage with AI chatbots without clear expectations or expertise on the topic of their query. At this juncture, it is important to develop an understanding of how the procedures surrounding information-seeking practices are informed by users’ values, experiences, and expectations—and how this might in future be altered by the emerging turn towards AI “experts” and authority. Our methodology in this paper comes from Ian Bogost’s concept of procedural rhetoric, which examines how arguments are constructed (and persuasion takes place) through rule-based processes. We investigate how interactions relating to high-stakes domains that value accuracy and precision, such as medicine and academic research, are subtly (and not-so-subtly) reshaping users’ experiences and expectations surrounding information seeking.

title: Night-to-Day Translation via Illumination Degradation Disentanglement
authors: Guanzhou Lan, Yuqi Yang, Zhigang Wang, Dong Wang, Xuelong Li, Bin Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Night-to-Day translation (Night2Day) aims to achieve day-like vision for nighttime scenes. However, processing night images with complex degradations remains a significant challenge under unpaired conditions. Previous methods that uniformly mitigate these degradations have proven inadequate in simultaneously restoring daytime domain information and preserving underlying semantics. In this paper, we propose N2D3 (Night-to-Day via Degradation Disentanglement) to identify different degradation patterns in nighttime images. Specifically, our method comprises a degradation disentanglement module and a degradation-aware contrastive learning module. Firstly, we extract physical priors from a photometric model based on Kubelka-Munk theory. Then, guided by these physical priors, we design a disentanglement module to discriminate among different illumination degradation regions. Finally, we introduce the degradation-aware contrastive learning strategy to preserve semantic consistency across distinct degradation regions. Our method is evaluated on two public datasets, demonstrating a significant improvement in visual quality and considerable potential for benefiting downstream tasks.

title: FedLIC: Personalized Federated Learning with Layer-Wise Importance-Aware Encryption and Compression
authors: Hengzhou Ye, Fucong Zhao, Yan Chen
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Personalized federated learning has emerged as a promising paradigm to address statistical heterogeneity across clients in distributed systems. However, existing methods often struggle to jointly balance three key objectives: high predictive accuracy under non-IID data, low communication overhead in bandwidth-constrained environments, and reducing the exposure of client-specific parameters during communication. Building on the FedPer-style base+personalization architecture, we propose FedLIC, an importance-aware layer-wise personalized federated learning framework. FedLIC decouples a globally shared feature extractor from client-specific classifier heads that are kept local, and introduces a dynamic loss-balancing mechanism that gradually shifts the optimization focus from shared representation learning to client-specific classification, enhancing both generalization and personalization. For the shared base, FedLIC computes layer-wise importance scores and selectively synchronizes only highly informative layers across clients. The corresponding shared-base updates are quantized and encrypted before transmission to reduce communication volume and provide communication-level confidentiality against external eavesdropping, while client-specific heads remain local. Extensive experiments on Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet under controlled heterogeneous settings show that FedLIC achieves a favorable accuracy–communication trade-off rather than uniformly outperforming all baselines in every metric. System-level profiling further indicates that layer selection and quantization introduce limited computational overhead, whereas encrypted transmission incurs measurable encryption, decryption, and ciphertext-size costs. These results clarify both the effectiveness and the practical cost of the proposed design, suggesting that FedLIC is a communication-aware personalized FL framework under the evaluated benchmark settings.

title: Abnormal Behavior Recognition of Dairy Cows via DeepLabCut-Based Limb Decomposition
authors: Yan Zhao, Xiaoxu Pei
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The early recognition of abnormal behavior in dairy cows is crucial for lameness warnings, disease detection, and welfare assessment. However, traditional methods that rely on holistic features struggle to distinguish normal from abnormal behaviors with similar postures. This paper proposes a hierarchical method spanning keypoint detection, limb decomposition, and abnormal behavior recognition based on the DeepLabCut framework. On a self-constructed 18-keypoint cattle pose dataset of approximately 4,000 annotated frames, two scene-separated Bayesian-optimization (BO) pose networks—ResNet101+BO for single-cow frames and top-down ResNet101+BO for multi-cow frames—serve as skeleton sources with inference-time adaptive switching. The 18 keypoints are organized into five functional limb regions. Geo-metric features (joint angles, normalized limb lengths, and region centroids) and motion features (velocity, gait cycle, limb coordination, and gait symmetry) are extracted to construct a 40-dimensional structured feature vector. An abnormal behavior detection framework that integrates statistical thresholds and machine learning automatically detects lameness, gait abnormalities, and feeding anomalies. The experimental results demonstrate that the method achieves a behavior recognition accuracy of 91.6% with Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), an abnormal gait detection sensitivity of 86.4%, and a specificity of 93.1%. The system achieves a tracking success rate of 96.5% and 28 frames per second (FPS) on a real farming video. This study bridges pose estimation and abnormal behavior understanding, providing an automated technical route for early lameness warnings and health management of dairy cows.

title: Direct Lightning Overvoltage and Flashover Risk in Complex Distribution Line Network Considering Electromagnetic Coupling From Lightning Channel
authors: Chuge Zhu, Jinxin Cao, Jianguo Wang, Li Cai, Mi Zhou, Runyu Fu, Yadong Fan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electromagnetic Compatibility
abstract: There are few studies on the characteristics of lightning overvoltage and flashover distribution in complex distribution line networks, considering both direct lightning surge injection and the lightning electromagnetic coupling pulse (LEMP) effect during direct lightning strikes. This article adopts a hybrid computational approach to analyze this issue, with an actual distribution line network modeled for a case study. The overvoltage waveforms and flashover pole counts under different lightning conditions, including the effects of LEMP, lightning strike points, lightning current characteristics, and the inhomogeneity of grounding resistance, are investigated. The results indicate that insulator overvoltage is highly sensitive to the LEMP effect, particularly when lightning strikes the poles. Increased pole density leads to greater overvoltage dispersion compared to sparse configurations. As the amplitude of the lightning current increases, the amplitude of the overvoltage on the insulator also increases, but the distribution of overvoltage remains essentially constant, which is significantly influenced by the complexity of the topology. The impact of uneven grounding conditions in distribution networks on overvoltage and flashover primarily depends on the grounding performance of the lightning-struck pole. The sensitivity of the LEMP effect to the lightning strike attachment point, the grounding resistance, and the line topology, as well as the comparison with that of the simple line configuration, were further summarized.

title: Mutual Information of Dynamic Metasurface Antenna-Enabled MIMO With Finite-Alphabet Inputs
authors: Hao Xu, Yitong Liu, Chongjun Ouyang, Hongwen Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Dynamic metasurface antennas (DMAs) are promising hardware architectures for supporting massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) transmission. This paper investigates DMA-assisted MIMO systems with practical finite-alphabet signaling and proposes efficient precoding methods to maximize the mutual information (MI). For the single-stream case, an effective precoder approximation (EPA)-based algorithm is developed to jointly optimize the DMA coefficients and the digital precoder. The study is then extended to the more challenging multi-stream transmission scenario. Due to the intractable MI expression in multi-stream systems with finite-alphabet inputs, the cut-off rate (CR) is employed as a surrogate objective, and a penalty dual decomposition (PDD)-based optimization framework is proposed. To further reduce computational complexity, two low-complexity designs are proposed based on the low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) asymptotic CR and the Gaussian signaling assumption, respectively. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm in the single-stream transmission significantly outperforms benchmark methods, while the proposed multi-stream designs effectively operate across a wide range of regimes, including low- and high-SNR regimes as well as different numbers of data streams.

title: Generative AI-enabled Cooperative Jamming for Secure Semantic Communication
authors: Xingyu Wang, Jiajia Huang, Shunpu Tang, Lisheng Fan, Xianfu Lei, Arumugam Nallanathan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Communications
abstract: Semantic communication (SemCom) has recently emerged as a promising approach to enhance communication efficiency by transmitting only important semantic information. In eavesdropping environments, the semantic information may be overheard by the eavesdropper even under poor channel conditions with a powerful semantic decoder. Some advanced secure communication deployments such as autoencoder-enhanced Sem-Com (AE-SemCom) and generative model-enhanced SemCom (GEN-SemCom) have been introduced to efficiently transmit the semantic information. However, these secure solutions require retraining the semantic encoder and decoder, which limits the flexibility and practical deployment. To address this issue, we propose a secure SemCom framework for AE-SemCom and GEN-SemCom without altering the parameters of the semantic encoder and decoder. Specifically, we propose to deploy a cooperative jammer to transmit optimized jamming signal to deteriorate the decoding ability of eavesdropper, while a residual refinement module (RRM) is used at the legitimate receiver to mitigate the impact of these jamming signal. Moreover, to achieve an efficient trade-off between transmission security and reliability, we then introduce a cooperative training strategy by formulating this optimization problem as a two-player game between the jammer and Bob. Finally, we conduct extensive simulations to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed framework across face recognition dataset. In particular, with a jamming power of 0.3, the eavesdropper experiences up to a 12dB reduction in the peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) of reconstructed images compared to the baselines without the proposed security framework while maintaining high-quality image reconstruction for legitimate users.

title: Coordinated Control Strategy for Unbalanced Power and Bipolar Voltage in Electrolytic Capacitorless Vienna Rectifier
authors: Jiarui Wang, Qiwei Wang, Gaolin Wang, Binxing Li, Dawei Ding, Dianguo Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: In bipolar DC-bus systems, the extreme-value constraint on the neutral point (NP) current limits the unbalanced power operating range and degrades grid-side power quality in electrolytic capacitorless Vienna rectifiers. To address these challenges, this article proposes a coordinated control strategy for unbalanced power and bipolar voltage. A prediction-based clamping selection mechanism is proposed by computing a one-step-ahead NP voltage estimate at each sampling instant, effectively suppressing bipolar voltage fluctuations. Furthermore, the mapping between the power imbalance degree and the bipolar voltage is exploited to derive the optimal voltage imbalance degree, through which the unbalanced power operating range under NP current constraints is expanded. Additionally, a voltage vector trajectory optimization based on vertical projection minimizes the voltage vector synthesis errors within infeasible regions. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed strategy extends the unbalanced power operating range, improves grid-side power quality, and enhances the stability of the bipolar DC-bus voltage in an electrolytic capacitorless Vienna rectifier.

title: A Real-time FBG-DCMs-based Large-Scale Chromatic Dispersion Emulator
authors: Yuming Zhao, Yang Zou, Shenmao Zhang, Qi Yang, Xiaoxiao Dai, Jian Xu
publish: IEEE Photonics Journal
abstract: Transoceanic submarine optical communication links face the critical challenge of ultra-large accumulated chromatic dispersion (CD), reaching hundreds of thousands of ps/nm over thousands of kilometers, which severely degrades high-speed coherent signal performance. Traditional dispersion emulation schemes like dispersion-compensating fiber (DCF) suffer from high loss and bulkiness, while fiber Bragg grating dispersion compensation modules (FBG-DCMs) offer high dispersion efficiency but are limited by narrow compensation bandwidth (∼0.1 nm). This paper proposes a large-scale dispersion emulator based on cascaded optical-electrical-optical (OEO) conversion and FBG-DCMs. By leveraging the complex conjugate control capability of single-ended driven modulators, this emulator achieves polarity-switchable dispersion loading while overcoming the inherent narrowband limitation of FBG-DCMs. The architecture supports, for the first time, the generation of ultra large positive/negative dispersion (±102,155 ps/nm, equivalent to 6000 km of G.652 fiber) at arbitrary C-band wavelengths. It provides an essential tool for accurately emulating transoceanic link dispersion effects in laboratory settings. Experimental validation demonstrates robust support for Dual-Polarization Quadrature Phase-Shift Keying (100G and 200G) and Dual Polarization Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (200G and 300G) signals at symbol rates up to 64 GBaud. The emulator successfully generates ultra-large dispersion up to ±102,155 ps/nm, introducing a Q-margin penalty below 1.2 dB while maintaining BER under the 2E-2 threshold. The overall insertion loss is effectively compensated to near 0 dB via EDFAs. The system exhibits excellent stability with less than 1.2 dB Q-margin fluctuation over a 24-hour continuous test. Link loss is compensated via five cascaded EDFA stages. Compared to DCF based solutions, this design reduces device footprint by over 90% and insertion loss by 85%, establishing a new pathway for algorithm validation and performance optimization in high-speed coherent transmission systems.
