title: AeroThreatModel: LLM-Empowered Spatiotemporal Fusion Dynamic Threat Modeling Framework for Air-Space-Ground Integrated Air Traffic Management Networks
authors: Siqi LI, Buhong WANG, Chengkai PIANO, Yongjian GUAN, Ruochen DONG, Xiaofan LV, Jin XIAO
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: The ATM system has evolved into a highly complex cyber-physical infrastructure that integrates air, space, and ground networks, where security threats propagate across domains and dynamically evolve throughout flight phases. Conventional static threat modeling methods, which depend heavily on expert knowledge, struggle to address this dynamic complexity. In order to overcome this challenge, this study introduces AeroThreatModel, a spatiotemporal dynamic threat modeling framework powered by LLMs. The approach constructs a structured semantic dataset combining multi domain network data and flight phase features. It applies a LoRA- based fine-tuning strategy to open-source LLMs for efficient domain adaptation. A dynamic threat modeling agent is further developed to enable continuous threat perception, assessment, mitigation, and report generation. Experimental results indicate that the fine-tuned Qwen3-14B-LoRA model achieves a BLEU-4 score of 61.95% and an accuracy of 98.73% in STRIDE threat classification. Results demonstrate that AeroThreatModel enhances the intelligence and adaptability of ATM network threat modeling, offering a proactive defense paradigm for critical air traffic infrastructure.

title: Multi-Frequency Resolution-Oriented Reconstruction for Single-Pixel Terahertz Imaging
authors: Amir Masoud Molaei, Adolphe Ndagijimana, Iñigo Ederra, Miguel Heredia Conde, Rahul Sharma, Nidhi Simmons, Okan Yurduseven
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Single-pixel terahertz (THz) imaging enables spatial reconstruction using a single detector and a sequence of coded modulations. However, practical systems often exhibit non-ideal sensing operators due to amplitude-only modulation, diffraction, and frequency-dependent propagation. This paper presents a practical multi-frequency reconstruction framework for experimentally calibrated single-pixel THz sensing operators, implemented in a matrix-free form that avoids explicit stacking across frequencies. By using a resolution-oriented penalized inversion solved via reweighted quadratic updates, the results show consistent improvements in reconstruction fidelity and target contrast over matched filtering, total variation-regularized and least-squares baselines as the measurement count and number of frequency samples increase.

title: Beyond Static Prompts: Dynamic Token Fusion for Temporal-Align Visual-Language Tracking
authors: Tianpeng Liu, Xinrong Hu, Jing Li, Lefei Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Visual-language (VL) tracking leverages natural language descriptions to enhance visual object tracking. However, a critical yet overlooked issue is the inherent temporal heterogeneity between static language descriptions and dynamic visual sequences, which limits the efficacy of cross-modal fusion. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel Temporal-Align Visual-Language Tracker (TAVLT). Unlike prior works that simply concatenate multimodal tokens, TAVLT processes template, search, and language features separately to explicitly model their interrelations. Our framework introduces two core components: 1) a Temporal Semantic Enrichment Module (TSEM) that constructs a Spatio-Temporal Semantic Affinity Matrix to dynamically recalibrate language token weights, ensuring temporal alignment with the visual sequence; and 2) a lightweight Cross-Modal Feature Fusion Module (CMFFM) that leverages the affinity matrices for efficient feature integration. Furthermore, we introduce a Consistency Learning Balance (CLB) strategy to mitigate performance disparities across different perceptual constancy tasks (e.g., shape vs. color). Extensive experiments on LaSOT, LaSOT-ext, TNL2K, and OTB99-Lang demonstrate that TAVLT achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining high inference efficiency (49 FPS). Our approach effectively addresses the temporal misalignment in VL tracking, setting a new standard for robust and efficient cross-modal learning.

title: Communication-Pipelined Split Federated Learning for Foundation Model Fine-Tuning in UAV Networks_supp1-3697889.pdf
authors: YingChang Liang
publish: N/A
abstract: Deploying foundation models (FMs) on uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) promises broad “low-altitude economy” applications. Split federated learning (SFL)-based fine-tuning leverages distributed data while keeping raw data local and reduces client-side burden by partitioning the model between client and server. However, the per-round training latency is dominated by stragglers. Training paradigms featuring parallel gradient transmission (GT) allocate dedicated portions of downlink communication resources to each client. They may leave resources idle and suffer from prolonged GT latency, especially in UAV networks, where the communication latency typically far exceeds the computation latency. To address this, we propose a sequential GT paradigm, where the server dedicates all downlink resources for the current GT. We further propose communication-pipelined SFL (CPSFL), characterized by downlink GT priority scheduling and intra-round asynchronous training. We investigate CPSFL-based LoRA fine-tuning of FMs in UAV networks and formulate an optimization problem to minimize a weighted sum of per-round training latency and worst-case client energy consumption by optimizing the split point selection (SPS) and the computing and communication resource allocation (CCRA) (the uplink bandwidth allocation and the server computing frequency allocation). To solve this problem, we develop an attention-based deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework, where the base station agent decides the split point and the CCRA in each round by leveraging previous round information, including UAV trajectories. Simulation results show that the proposed DRL-based CPSFL scheme outperforms the parallel GT baselines, the ablation variants, and the heuristic CCRA scheme while approaching the best fixed-SPS scheme.

title: Virtual Oscillator Control for Synthesising Virtual Inertia in a Smart Transformer-Based AC Microgrids
authors: Sahil Gaurav, Chandan Kumar, Marco Liserre
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: The smart transformer (ST) is a power electronics-based transformer that serves as an energy router and provides various ancillary services in the electrical distribution grid. However, an ST-based microgrid has limited inertia, leading to frequency instability, sustained voltage oscillations, and synchronization problems during disturbances such as load changes, line switching, generation loss, etc. In this article, a new virtual inertia control is proposed for the ST low-voltage (LV) converter and distributed generation (DG) converters. The proposed adaptive decentralized virtual inertia-virtual oscillator (VI-VO) control for ST-based microgrids provides frequency containment reserve (FCR) services and offers fast dynamic response with time-domain implementation. It further enables accurate power sharing among converters, improves frequency characteristics, ensures stable operation under varying operating conditions, and eliminates the need for phase-locked loop (PLL). In addition, the proposed controller allows the ST LV converter to support frequency restoration reserve (FRR) service in the microgrid. The eigenvalue analysis demonstrates the small-signal stability of the proposed system. The simulation and experimental results are shown to verify the performance of the proposed virtual inertia synthesis.

title: A LiDAR-Inertial SLAM Algorithm for Robot Detection Tasks in Unknown Complex Environments
authors: Chao Wang, Yan-Jun Liu, Qi Gao, Shu Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: When performing autonomous exploration in unknown and complex environments, robots rely on high-accuracy real-time environmental maps for reliable navigation. However, existing LiDAR-based simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) methods often encounter challenges such as point cloud feature redundancy, motion distortion, and cumulative errors in such scenarios. To address these issues, we propose a LiDAR-inertial SLAM algorithm that integrates point cloud clustering segmentation with Scan Context-based loop closure detection. First, this method constructs an odometry using inertial measurement unit (IMU) preintegration to mitigate motion distortion in the LiDAR point cloud. Second, it achieves efficient and robust feature extraction through point cloud clustering segmentation and curvature analysis. Finally, the Scan Context descriptor is introduced for loop closure detection, and a factor graph is built to globally optimize the LiDAR pose, effectively suppressing cumulative errors. Experimental results show that, compared to mainstream algorithms like tightly-coupled LiDAR inertial odometry via smoothing and mapping (LIOSAM), the proposed method significantly improves both mapping accuracy and efficiency, particularly for robotic exploration in unknown, complex environments.

title: Stability Optimization Oriented Real-Time Energy Management System for Turbo-Hybrid Electric Aircrafts
authors: Yu Wu, Ruizhen Li, Linke He, Alexandre Ravey, Daniela Chrenko, Weilin Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Hybrid-electric aircraft combine the advantages of fuel and electric power, with secondary energy conversion unified as electricity, offering benefits such as extended range and low noise. However, the complex power architecture, variable operating conditions, and high-impact loads pose significant challenges to the stability and energy management of the power supply system. To address this issue, this study proposes a stability optimization oriented hierarchical control framework, which achieves online optimal control under multiple constraints, targeting both system stability and fuel consumption. In this article, the stability region of the hybrid-electric power system under different operating conditions have been quantitatively analyzed, establishing a stability criterion for the real-time controller of hybrid-electric power systems. A distributed control method employing virtual impedance is developed to simultaneously improve system stability and regulate power distribution in real time control layer. A receding-horizon energy management system is proposed for the long-term energy management layer of aircraft hybrid power systems, where model predictive control performs online rolling optimization to minimize fuel consumption while enforcing stability constraints. Hardware-in-the-loop simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach maintains stability across all operating conditions while achieving 7.56% reduction in fuel consumption, 19.8% extension of turbine engine lifetime, and 7 times faster computation speed compared to finite state machines and genetic algorithms baselines respectively.

title: MTT-S Africa Initiative Visit to South Africa [Around the Globe]
authors: Carla Pieterse, Maurizio Bozzi
publish: IEEE Microwave Magazine

title: Masterless Cooperative Control for Modular Parallel Inverters with Accurate Current Sharing and Seamless Fault Transition
authors: Jiangfeng Wang, Hao Zhang, Danrui Liu, Huawu Liu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Parallel inverter systems commonly suffer from current imbalance due to mismatch in output impedance and output voltage differences. Conventional parallel current-sharing control struggles to achieve both accurate power sharing and rapid transition during faults, while often suffering from compromised system stability due to master module failures. To address these issues, this paper proposes a modular current-sharing control strategy based on masterless cooperative control. Parallel master-slave modules employ a modified droop control scheme with unified voltage compensation. This control strategy offers the advantages of high current-sharing accuracy while maintaining effective operation with low-frequency communication requirements, which also maintains stable operation even with variations in output and line impedances. Moreover, by introducing an automatic master-slave election mechanism, the system allows any module to be connected to or disconnected from the system, thereby avoiding the single-point failure issue in interconnected systems. The proposed control maintains a current-sharing error within 2% during both steady-state and transient operations, while enabling seamless transition under any module failure. The effectiveness of the proposed method is verified by experimental results.

title: A Data-Driven Approach for EV Load Modeling and Multi-Objective approach for integration of EVCS and DG in Distribution Systems
authors: Anand Kumar Pandey, Abdullah Altamimi, Ankesh Kumar, Iram Akhtar, Md. Aftab Alam
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The increasing demand for electric vehicles and the requirement of electric vehicle charging stations are both a challenge and an opportunity for a power distribution system. Knowing the load demand of EVs at any given time and placing the EVCS at the right place in the distribution scheme is an important and challenging task. If it is not done accurately, the distribution system can face many problems. In literature, different authors have addressed these problems differently under different constraints and problem formulations. In this paper author have discussed problem statement in two parts: first part a data driven approach in detailed is given to modelled the grid-load profile of a mixed EV fleet using different random variables considering all-Electric Range (AER), battery Capacity (Cn), charging power (P), stochastic behavior inputs, Time of arrival, daily distance, time of departure, and state of charge (SoC) of the vehicles. Probability distributions—normal for temporal behavior and lognormal for mileage—are applied to reflect the inherent uncertainty and variability in EV usage patterns. The resulting model accurately estimates the aggregated EV power demand, offering a realistic load profile for planning studies. The second part author analyzed how optimal DG placement and capacity reduce the impact of EVCS and DG loads, proposed a zone-based EV placement strategy considering driver comfortability factors, employed a robust hybrid JAYA-ABC algorithm to minimize Multi-Objective Function (MOF) values, and tested the methodology on IEEE 69-bus and 33-bus RDS to assess the effects of voltage stability and power loss. Power loss for the 33 bus scheme is decreased from 210.998 kW to 107.3 kW (55%), and the VSI improved from 0.667 to 0.744. For the 69 bus scheme, the power loss is reduced to 89.804 kW, and the VSI of the overall system improved to 0.855. It represents a reduction of 60.08% and an improvement of 25.13%, respectively. The proposed methodology shows the usefulness of planning the scheme size and location, and shows the convergence of the optimization procedure applied to the objective function.

title: Enhanced Ship Routing Through Ensemble Wave Forecasting and Causality-Constrained Departure Windows
authors: Lingyi Zhang, David Sidoti, Krishna R. Pattipati, Charles R. Sampson, Efren A. Serra, John A. Knaff, Samuel J. Edwards, Michael D. Levine
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Maritime operations face significant challenges from tropical cyclones each year, with substantial risks to vessel safety and in achieving mission objectives. This paper presents two key enhancements to the Tool for Multiobjective Planning and Asset Routing (TMPLAR): (1) incorporation of tropical cyclone-specific wind, wave, and wave probability forecasts for marine hazard avoidance, and (2) flexible departure time window optimization with causality constraints to enable strategic voyage timing. Our approach integrates meteorological forecasts with vessel-specific response characteristics, helping to better balance the inherent trade-off between safety and operational effectiveness in maritime operations. Analysis of historical tropical cyclone events from 2022 demonstrates that the enhanced TMPLAR system significantly improves both safety and efficiency outcomes. The wave-informed tropical cyclone routing capability reduces high-wave encounters by up to 35% and decreases ship motion exceedances by up to 18% compared to conventional routing. Simultaneously, the system achieves 14-15% reduction in fuel consumption while often offering faster transit times. The flexible departure time optimization provides substantial operational benefits, achieving more than 10% reduction across the board with respect to route distance, fuel consumption, and transit duration in the case study examined, while maintaining equivalent safety margins with wave heights consistently below operational thresholds. These combined enhancements significantly reduce risks to personnel and equipment while improving operational performance. The results demonstrate potential value for maritime planners seeking to make more informed routing decisions and avoid adverse weather and ocean conditions.

title: MCPST: A Multi-Phase Consensus and Spatio-Temporal Learning for Few-Shot Traffic Forecasting via Physical Dynamics
authors: Abdul Joseph Fofanah, Lian Wen, David Chen
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Accurate traffic flow prediction remains a fundamental challenge in intelligent transportation systems, particularly in cross-domain, data-scarce scenarios where limited historical data hinders model training and generalisation. The complex spatio-temporal dependencies and nonlinear dynamics of urban mobility networks further complicate few-shot learning across different cities. This paper proposes MCPST, a novel Multi-phase Consensus Spatio-Temporal framework for few-shot traffic forecasting that reconceptualises traffic prediction as a multi-phase consensus learning problem. Our framework introduces three core innovations: (1) a multi-phase engine that models traffic dynamics through diffusion, synchronisation, and spectral embeddings for comprehensive dynamic characterisation; (2) an adaptive consensus mechanism that dynamically fuses phase-specific predictions while enforcing consistency; and (3) a structured meta-learning strategy for rapid adaptation to new cities with minimal data. We establish extensive theoretical guarantees, including representation theorems with bounded approximation errors and generalisation bounds for few-shot adaptation. Through experiments on four real-world datasets, MCPST achieves competitive performance against twenty-one (21) state-of-the-art methods in spatio-temporal graph learning, dynamic graph transfer learning, prompt-based spatio-temporal prediction, and cross-domain few-shot settings. Our framework delivers strong average prediction accuracy while requiring up to 90% less training data, and performs on par with the leading baselines in statistically significant comparisons. The implementation code is available at https://github.com/afofanah/MCPST.

title: IEEE App
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Microwave Magazine

title: A Millimeter-Wave Low Profile Dual-polarization Phased Array Operating Under Glass Enclosures with Beamforming Co-Design for 5G IoT Mobile Terminals
authors: Ren-Rong Zhao, Fan Wu, Chao Yu, Wei Li, Kai Chen, Xiaoyue Xia, Zhi Hao Jiang, Jun Xu, Yu Yao, Wei Hong
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: This paper presents a bandwidth-enhanced, low-profile dual-polarization (dual-pol) patch antenna array with a height of only 0.03λ₀ for glass-enclosed mobile platforms in 5G-enabled IoT applications at the millimeter-wave band. The bandwidth improvement and low-profile characteristic are achieved through a single-layer radiating patch fed by closely positioned lateral microstrip resonators, forming a multi-resonance structure that expands bandwidth while reducing the overall height by minimizing vertical feeding components. Dual-pol performance is accomplished via common-mode excitation of back-to-back C-shaped quarter-wavelength resonators on one side of the patch for one polarization, and differential-mode excitation of folded line-shaped resonators symmetrically placed on opposite sides for the orthogonal polarization, achieving extended bandwidth and high isolation. Building on this antenna element, a 1×4 dual-pol phased array is implemented for beam steering, fabricated using high-density interconnect (HDI) technology. Measurements reveal an operating bandwidth of 25.56–28.04 GHz (a fractional bandwidth of 9.5%) for x-pol and 24.42–28.49 GHz (15.6%) for y-pol. Beam scanning across ±45° shows a gain degradation of less than 3 dB, with cross-polarization levels remaining below -20 dB within the main lobe at each scan angle. This design is intended for integration within the camera region of a mobile terminal and has been optimized for operation in an under-glass environment. Performance tests conducted with a glass cover confirm the robustness of the design as an effective under-glass antenna for terminal applications.

title: Magnet Segmentation Strategy Considering Electromagnetic Force in Large-Scale Wind Generators with Surface-Mounted Magnets
authors: Wenting Wang, Yuqi Wang, Chenning Zhang, Hui Wen, Zhengxuan Xun, Lijian Wu, Tianjie Zou, David Gerada, Chris Gerada
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: Circumferential and axial segmentation are effective in reducing eddy current losses in magnets of large-scale wind generators. However, improper segmentation strategies can induce uneven electromagnetic forces (EFs) with pronounced fluctuations, threatening the reliability of magnet immobilization. This paper investigates segment-level transient EFs and compares different segmentation strategies by jointly considering magnet fixation reliability and eddy current losses. The results indicate that axial-only segmentation with a sufficient number of segments provides a favorable compromise between magnet stability and eddy current loss reduction.

title: Data Visualization for Teenage Teaching: A Review of Current Applications, Pedagogical Strategies, and Impact on Learning
authors: Xingyu Lan, Xian Xu, Yifan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Data visualization has long been recognized and applied as a powerful tool for education. However, systematic reviews within the visualization community focusing on this specific area remain limited, and existing studies have focused more on higher education or early childhood education. In this work, we focus on a particular stage of education: teenage education, where students are undergoing significant changes in cognitive abilities and facing increased curriculum demands, making visualization a crucial support tool. We are particularly interested in two research questions: (i) What is the existing method space of implementing visualization in teenage teaching, and (ii) How are the effects of the implemented methods? Driven by these questions, we reviewed 112 papers that provide valuable empirical evidence on how data visualization has been applied in teenage education and analyze three main aspects: (i) visualization applications (e.g., visualization types, forms, tools, educational goals); (ii) its pedagogical integration with curricula and teaching activities; and (iii) how it affects learning performance and user experience. Based on our analysis, we discuss a set of key trends, such as a predominant focus on STEM disciplines and the increasing integration of AR/VR and AI technologies with visualization. We also highlight various implementation challenges, such as the gaps between professional visualization tools and the actual needs of educational settings, as well as the complexity of student characteristics. Through this review, we hope to provide visualization researchers, developers, and educators with in-depth insights to better align the use of visualization with the developmental needs of teenage learners.

title: Polar Code With Parity Check-Assisted Pruning for Error Correction in Modern Flash Memory
authors: Debao Wei, Huqi Xiang, Yongchao Wang, Zhelong Piao, Liyan Qiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Three-dimensional (3-D) <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">nand</small> flash memory has emerged as the predominant solution for high-capacity nonvolatile data storage. However, the continuous increase in storage density has concurrently led to significant challenges in data reliability degradation. This article investigates the application of polar codes for error correction in <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">nand</small> Flash memory. We propose a combined Monte Carlo (CMC) method to dynamically determine information bit positions in polar code encoding for flash memory chips under varying reliability statuses. For decoding, we identify that the hard-decision read mechanism in flash memory introduces extreme log-likelihood ratio (LLR) quantization effects. This quantization phenomenon may erroneously prune correct decoding paths during successive cancellation list (SCL) decoding, thereby degrading the error correction capability of polar codes. To address this critical issue, we develop a cascaded parity-check (PC)-assisted path pruning scheme integrated with the SCL algorithm. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed polar code algorithm can significantly enhance the reliability of data storage in <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">nand</small> flash memory. In the long code length scenario, the proposed polar decoding scheme with PC-assisted pruning achieves a maximum uncorrectable bit error rate (UBER) reduction of 98.62% compared to algorithms without PC. The proposed approach demonstrates an 8.56 times increase in the raw bit error rate (RBER) threshold for achieving 100% error correction success, significantly surpassing the performance of conventional low-density parity-check (LDPC) codes, especially toward the end of the flash memory’s lifespan.

title: Comparative Analysis of Traditional Machine Learning and Causal Inference Models for ICU Drug Treatment Outcomes
authors: Kamepalli S L Prasanna, Nithin Polimera, Meegada Nishanth Reddy, Divi Mahanth Sri Ram, Reddi Varun Rahul
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Accurate and interpretable treatment evaluation is essential in Intensive Care Units (ICUs), where clinical decisions must account for rapidly evolving physiological states. Traditional machine learning models achieve high predictive accuracy but remain limited by their reliance on correlations, making them unsuitable for estimating true treatment effects. This study investigates whether Causal Artificial Intelligence (Causal AI) can complement predictive models by providing robust and clinically meaningful treatment insights. Using the eICU Collaborative Research Database, we analyzed demographics, vital signs, laboratory values, and treatment timelines to evaluate the effect of early antibiotic administration on 30-day mortality. Logistic Regression, Random Forest, and XGBoost were trained for outcome prediction, with ensemble models achieving strong performance (AUC ≈ 0.99) compared to Logistic Regression (AUC = 0.68). Causal estimators—including Propensity Score Matching (PSM) and Inverse Probability Weighting (IPW)—indicated a mortality reduction of 5–17% among patients receiving early antibiotics. These results show that while predictive models excel in discrimination, causal inference provides actionable, interpretable, and clinically aligned treatment-effect estimates. The combined framework underscores the potential of Causal AI to support trustworthy and decision-centric care in critical settings. We also perform leakage-aware feature selection and explicitly state causal assumptions and limitations of observational ICU data.

title: Harnessing Vision Language Models for Negation Sensitivity in Visual Question Answering
authors: Sourav Roy, Fardin Alam, Yeash Zuddan Ali, Hussain Md. Sahariyar, Md. Nahin Alam, Ahmed Faizul Haque Dhrubo, Mohammad Abdul Qayum
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Negation flips the meaning of a question, yet many vision language models (VLMs) still answer as if the prompt were positive. We present a simple, reproducible pipeline that measures negation sensitivity in yes / no Visual Question Answering (VQA) using BLIP and CLIP. We parse questions, create rule based negated counterparts, and evaluate paired original and negated (Questions and Answers) for the same image so that wording, not content, drives differences. On a validation set of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N=1181$</tex> pairs, BLIP achieves Affirmative and Negation accuracies of 0.484, 0.516 while CLIP achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.516,0.484$</tex> on (Epoch:1, Learning rate for BLIP 1e-4 and for CLIP 2e-4) respectively. A small combined (Avg.) negation differential (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta=-0.015$</tex>) suggests reduced affirmation bias compared with common baselines. We include model comparisons, qualitative results per-sample, and clear reporting to support reuse.

title: A Universal Machine-Learning-Based Framework for Detecting and Preventing File Inclusion Attacks in PHP Web Applications
authors: Fawad Zia Khan, Jared Oluoch, Mohammad Niamat
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: The purpose of this study is to investigate File Inclusion Attacks conducted through interactive PHP web applications. In addition to examining the nature of these attacks, this study aims to develop a framework for their detection and prevention. The proposed framework involves the design of a specialized Web Application Firewall (WAF) that is primarily limited to File Inclusion Attacks. The objective of this firewall is to identify and prevent File Inclusion Attacks using machine learning(ML) techniques. The firewall also provides additional capabilities, including retraining support and a limited form of static analysis. This study further compares the performance of the proposed machine learning-based firewall with that of a conventional rule-based firewall system, namely ModSecurity.

title: Pre-ISP Adversarial Patch Detection in RAW Bayer Space for Trustworthy IoT Vision
authors: Anusuya Sijapati, Bibek Maharjan, Fathi Amsaad
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Patch-based physical attacks are a practical threat to IoT and edge vision systems because a printed sticker can steer downstream model behavior without requiring device access. Most patch defenses operate on ISP-rendered sRGB images, but demosaicing, denoising, tone mapping, and 8-bit companding can weaken sensor-domain evidence. We study adverserial patch detection earlier in the pipeline by operating directly on RAW Bayer measurements. Our approach normalizes RAW mosaics using camera metadata, unpacks the Bayer pattern into a four-channel tensor, injects Bayer-consistent patch textures using an inverse-demosaicing mapping, and trains a lightweight ResNet-18 variant (BayerResNet) to classify frames as clean or patched. On a held-out RAW evaluation set derived from MIT-Adobe FiveK, the detector achieves strong separation between clean and patched samples (accuracy 0.997998, recall 1.000000, F1 0.997963) with a low false positive rate. These results support pre-ISP detection as a practical trustworthiness layer for IoT cameras.

title: AI-Assisted Forensic Triage of Multilingual Online Gaming Chats Using Large Language Models
authors: Jerrold Johnson, Mohamed I. Ibrahem, Mahmoud Abouyoussef
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Online gaming platforms generate massive volumes of short, informal, and multilingual messages that increasingly appear in digital forensic investigations. Manual examination of such data is time-consuming and difficult to scale, motivating the need for automated triage mechanisms that assist investigators without replacing human judgment. This paper evaluates zero-shot large language models (LLMs) as decision-support tools for early-stage forensic triage of online gaming communication. Using real-world Discord messages from the publicly available DISCO dataset, we construct a forensic analysis pipeline incorporating multilingual preprocessing, a classical Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency (TF–IDF) with Logistic Regression baseline, zero-shot LLM classification, and human validation. On a 200-message human-annotated subset, GPT-4.1-mini achieved higher agreement with human judgments (accuracy 0.77) than the classical baseline (accuracy 0.655). A consistency analysis further indicates that repeated LLM runs agree on 72% of evaluated messages, highlighting both practical utility and the continued need for human oversight. These findings suggest that LLMs can effectively support forensic triage by prioritizing potentially relevant messages while maintaining a human-centered investigative process.

title: Fake News Detection Using Machine Learning Techniques
authors: Meghana Reddy Veerati, Anvitha Sriramula, Muhammad Zubair Khan, Anand Kumar Bapatla
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: The proliferation of misinformation on digital platforms demands reliable, scalable detection systems. While transformer-based models achieve strong results, their computational demands limit real-world deployment. This study evaluates three lightweight supervised classifiers-Logistic Regression, Linear Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Random Forest-on the Kaggle Fake and Real News Dataset using TF-IDF feature extraction. On a stratified 80/20 test split (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n=8,980$</tex>), the models achieve accuracies of 98.64%, 99.48%, and 99.84%, respectively. With explicitly documented preprocessing, hyperparameters, and evaluation protocols, the results confirm that classical machine learning methods remain highly competitive, reproducible, and computationally efficient baselines for fake news detection. Beyond raw accuracy, this study systematically examines the tradeoffs between model complexity, interpretability, training time, and inference cost. We demonstrate that the choice of TFIDF vectorizer configuration-including n-gram range, sublinear term frequency scaling, and vocabulary pruning-substantially influences downstream classifier performance, often more so than the choice of classifier itself. Our findings are contextualized within broader concerns about dataset bias arising from source-level labeling, and we offer concrete recommendations for practitioners seeking robust, low-resource misinformation detection systems that generalize beyond single-source benchmarks.

title: A Scalable and Privacy-Preserving Sealed-Bid Auction Framework for IoT Data Marketplaces
authors: Hassan Mahmoud, Wade Little, Mahmoud Nabil Mahmoud, Ahmad Alsharif
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: IoT data marketplaces require auction mechanisms that are fair and privacy-preserving, yet efficient enough for frequent, large-scale trading. This paper proposes an efficient decentralized sealed-bid auction framework in which bidders submit encrypted additive shares of their bids and cryptographic commitments to a blockchain smart contract. Bids evaluation is performed by an efficient two-party protocol between the seller and an auctioneer using secure comparison over additively shared bids, enabling winner determination without revealing bid values to either party. The submitted commitments to the blockchain enforce bid binding and non-repudiation, ensuring that bidders cannot modify or deny their bids after submission. The proposed framework is implemented and evaluated across different bid sizes <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\ell$</tex> and bidder counts <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N_{b}$</tex>. Bids preparation in the proposed design is non-interactive and fully parallelizable across bidders with execution time independent of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N_{b}$</tex>, while bids evaluation scales linearly with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N_{b}$</tex>. Compared to multiparty computation-based auction designs, our protocol substantially reduces end-to-end runtime complexity from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{O}\left(N_{b}^{2}\right)$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{O}\left(N_{b}\right)$</tex>.

title: Improving Rating Accuracy in a Comparative Study of Web and Android Platforms Using a Quantitative Survey of Chef Booking Applications
authors: Vunnam Ganesh, Shri Vindhya A, Fikri Arif Wicaksana
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: The research project revolves around improving rating precision in a personalized application for booking chefs to cook at home, called BookAChef. To improve the preciseness of ratings and customer satisfaction in this paper, we analyze the dependability of the rating systems on web and Android platforms. We achieve our objectives with user input analysis, optimization techniques, and performance testing. Materials and Methods: This study will consider two applications: an Android application, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{N}=15$</tex>, and a Web application, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{N}=15$</tex>. This implies that the sample size for the study will be <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N=30$</tex>. Sample size calculations are carried out using G Power to achieve a statistical power of 80%. This was also in agreement with rating accuracy assessment in the BookAChef app through user text message summaries. Results: The rating accuracy of the Android application is 85.2667%, while the online application has a rating accuracy of 90.6000%. However, the p=0.028 significance gives a result of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{p}_{\dot{c}} 0.05$</tex>, meaning there is no significant statistical difference between the two groups within the data. Conclusion: Based on the comparison, web applications have received higher ratings than Android applications.

title: Hybrid Ransomware Detection Model Using Dynamic Behavioral
authors: Muhammad Faisal Saeed, Sidra Tahir, Sumaira Sarwar, Farzeen Ashfaq
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Ransomware are one of the biggest challenges to the cybersecurity. This malware is continuously evolving using advanced obfuscation and signature- evasion techniques. The traditional methods become ineffective to detect this continuously evolving malware. This research proposes a hybrid two-stage ransomware detection framework which uses dynamic behavioral analysis with the deep learning models to improve accuracy and adaptability. In the first stage, it dynamically analyzes system-level behavioral data like API call sequences, registry modifications and file system activities using Bidirectional LSTM-based sequence autoencoder (BiLSTM-AE). In the second stage, a Deep Neural Network (DNN) learns the temporal and semantic patterns in these behaviors to differentiate between the legitimate software programs and malware. This hybrid-framework is designed to minimize false negatives and keeping the false positives at a very low level for the early and reliable detection of the malware. The model is trained in a sandbox environment with the diverse dataset of both ransomware and normalware samples. This study develops an automated, strong and intelligent solution which could keep up with the evolving ransomware environment.

title: Physical Evaluation of Naturalistic Adversarial Patches for Camera-Based Traffic-Sign Detection
authors: Brianna D'Urso, Tahmid Hasan Sakib, Syed Rafay Hasan, Terry N Guo
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: This paper studies how well Naturalistic Adversarial Patches (NAPs) transfer to a physical traffic sign setting when the detector is trained on customized dataset for autonomous vehicle (AV) environment. We construct a composite dataset, CompGTSRB (which is customized dataset for AV environment), by pasting traffic sign instances from the German Traffic Sign Recognition Benchmark (GTSRB) onto undistorted backgrounds captured from the target platform. CompGTSRB is used to train YOLOv5 model and generate patches using a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) with latent space optimization, following existing NAP methods. We carried out a series of experiments on our Quanser QCar testbed utilizing the front CSI camera provided in QCar. Across configurations, NAPs reduce the detector's STOP class confidence. Different configurations includes distance, patch sizes, and patch placement. These results along with a detailed step-by-step methodology indicate the utility of CompGTSRB dataset and the proposed systematic physical protocols for credible patch evaluation. The research further motivate reseaching the defenses that address localized patch corruption in embedded perception pipelines.

title: PickyRTL: A Static Analysis Tool for Detecting Hardware CWEs at Register-Transfer Level
authors: John Parks, Logan Reichling, Boyang Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Hardware vulnerabilities pose significant security risks in modern systems. If they remain undetected, attackers can exploit them to steal sensitive data or modify critical system configurations. However, tools to automatically detect hardware vulnerabilities in Register-Transfer Level code at pre-silicon stage remain limited in their coverage of specific hardware Common Weakness Enumerations (CWEs). This paper introduces PickyRTL, a static analysis tool that automatically detects hardware CWEs in RTL code. Our algorithm first parses RTL code into an Abstract Syntax Tree and then traverses the tree to identify CWE-specific structural and semantic vulnerabilities. Our current tool support the detection of four specific CWEs (CWE-1233, CWE-226, CWE-1245, and CWE-1431), which are associated with critical vulnerabilities in Finite State Machines, security-sensitive controls, cryptographic results, and reuse of sensitive data. We evaluate PickyRTL on our dataset, which contains 39 Verilog and SystemVerilog files containing injected vulnerabilities across the targeted CWEs. Our evaluation results demonstrate that PickyRTL achieves 85.5 % precision and 73.8 % recall over the four CWEs we examine.

title: Retrieval-Augmented AI Platform for Accessible Legal Support in India
authors: Sai Mahitha Kurri, Rakesh Naidu Jerripothula, Rushika Manchala, Riyanka Manna
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Nyay-Mitra is an AI-driven legal assistance system developed to simplify judicial information and support litigants in understanding court documents. The proposed framework integrates Large Language Models—LLaMA-8B and Gemma-7B—with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to produce factually grounded and precedent-aware legal responses. A curated instruction-based question–answer dataset, along with four years of Supreme Court judgments, is used to fine-tune the models through Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and 4-bit quantization, enabling efficient deployment in resource-constrained settings. Performance is evaluated using Semantic Similarity, BLEU, and ROUGE metrics, where both models demonstrate strong accuracy and coherence, with Gemma showing marginally superior results. The findings indicate that lightweight, domain-specific legal LLMs can deliver reliable and explainable assistance, highlighting Nyay-Mitra’s potential for scalable judicial support systems.

title: Identification of Sarcasm, Emoji Utilization, and Aspect Recognition in Feedback Using Sentiment Analysis
authors: A Samved Rao, Mamatha Balipa, Aarthik K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Customer feedback often includes sarcasm, emojis, informal phrases, and fragmented text, making traditional sentiment analysis unreliable. To address these challenges, this study proposes a lightweight Feedback Analyzer that detects sarcasm, identifies relevant aspects, and computes aspect-wise sentiment using a hybrid machine learning and rule-based approach. Sarcasm—present in an estimated 10%–25% of social media reviews—can reverse literal sentiment, making accurate interpretation essential for understanding true user experience.The system is trained on datasets such as TweetEval and SemEval-2014 and is designed for short informal text found in reviews and social media posts. It extracts emojis, detects aspects (food, service, ambience, price, location), performs clause-level Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA), and applies heuristic adjustments when sarcasm is detected. An emoji-only sentiment model enhances analysis for minimal-text inputs.All components are integrated to produce a unified mood score and a clear summary through a Gradio interface with an optional FastAPI endpoint. Experimental results show that combining textual, contextual, and emoji cues significantly improves sentiment interpretation, especially for sarcasm-heavy feedback.

title: A Hybrid Deep Learning Framework for Brain Stroke Diagnosis Using MRI and CT Scans
authors: Akhilraj V. Gadagkar, Shifana Begum
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Stroke is a critical neurological emergency that occurs due to the disruption of blood flow to the brain, resulting in oxygen deprivation and subsequent brain cell damage. Early diagnosis is essential for timely intervention, yet conventional diagnostic methods like CT and MRI imaging are often limited by cost, accessibility, and reliance on specialist interpretation. This paper presents a comprehensive deep learning-based framework for early stroke prediction and classification using CT and MRI brain scans. The system employs Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for feature extraction and integrates ensemble classification algorithms—including Random Forest, Support Vector Machines (SVM), Bagging, and XGBoost—to enhance predictive performance. A semantic segmentation module using a SegNet encoder-decoder architecture is implemented to identify stroke-affected brain regions. A web-based application is also envisioned for remote access and usability. The framework demonstrates significant potential for improving stroke detection accuracy, enabling faster clinical decision-making, and enhancing preventive care.Key contributions include: (1) CNN-based architecture for stroke classification, (2) multi-algorithmic ensemble learning for robust predictions, (3) semantic segmentation of stroke regions, and (4) a deployable user interface for clinical and patient use.

title: Review of Antenna Engineering and Radio Wave Propagation With MATLAB [Book/Software Reviews]
authors: James Chu
publish: IEEE Microwave Magazine
abstract: Presents reviews for the following list of books, Antenna Engineering and Radio Wave Propagation With MATLAB.

title: Toward Real-time High-fidelity Immersive Robotic Telepresence via Online 3D Gaussian Splatting_supp1-3700426.mp4
authors: Jingxin Du
publish: N/A
abstract: Immersive robotic telepresence offers new possibilities for users to feel like they are present in remote locations. This provides benefits for many application areas, such as remote inspection, virtual tourism, and hazardous environment monitoring. However, due to limitations in their underlying technologies, architecture design, and hardware constraints, existing robotic telepresence systems often force a trade-off between visual quality, system performance, and display immersiveness. Furthermore, much of the existing research exhibits a narrow focus, often centering on improving and evaluating a single technical aspect, such as reconstruction fidelity or rendering speed, while failing to capture the holistic user experience. We present a novel online 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)-based virtual reality (VR) robotic telepresence system that more effectively balances these competing objectives through a careful co-design spanning the system's algorithms, architecture, and pipelines across the reconstruction, rendering, and streaming processes. We also introduce a comprehensive evaluation framework that goes beyond reconstruction fidelity and rendering speed to evaluate key interaction-critical factors such as convergence speed and system latency. Empirical results demonstrate that our system outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in terms of both fidelity and performance, while also providing an immersive experience.

title: Remembering Fred E. Gardiol [In Memoriam]
authors: Juan R. Mosig, Michel Ney
publish: IEEE Antennas and Propagation Magazine
abstract: Recounts the career and contributions of Fred E. Gardiol.

title: Nonconvex Low-Rank Tensor Representation with Self-Enhanced Structure Penalty for Multi-View Subspace Clustering
authors: Zhuo Liu, Dong Hu, Zhi Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: In addition to the high dimensionality and heterogeneity of multi-view data, its underlying subspaces contain a wealth of intrinsic correlation information. However, existing MvSC models still remain struggling in effectively exploiting the high-order complementary information and handling the confusing points in disjoint subspaces. To this end, we propose a novel tensorized MvSC model by integrating the nonconvex tensor Schatten-2/3 quasi-norm with a self-enhanced structure penalty constraint. The proposed model can not only characterize high-order correlation through adaptive subspace components encoding, but also manipulate the confusing points meticulously by capturing the refined geometric structure of multiple disjoint subspaces. As a result, a promising representation can be learned for the authentic subspaces revealment. More importantly, an effective algorithm has been proposed to solve the challenging nonconvex optimization problem of the proposed model, and the resulting solution sequence is theoretically guaranteed to converge to a Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) point. Furthermore, it can be proven that several key variable sequences are Cauchy ones. Extensive experiments conducted on various datasets demonstrate the superiority of our proposed model in comparison with many state-of-the-art models. MATLAB code is available at https://github.com/wangzhi-swu/SeT-MvSC.

title: Rank-based No-reference Quality Assessment for Face Swapping
authors: Xinghui Zhou, Tianyi Wei, Weiming Zhang, Nenghai Yu, Ming Mao, Wenbo Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Face swapping has emerged as a significant research area in generative models due to rapid technological advancements. Current image quality assessment metrics for face swapping images depend on comparing facial attributes between fake and real images (e.g., target faces and source faces). However, obtaining such corresponding real images is often impractical. To address this challenge, we propose a no-reference image quality assessment method for face swapping. We observed that consistency among errors in various facial attributes can indicate overall image quality. Based on this insight, we created a large-scale face swapping dataset with multi-attribute ranking labels to produce quality ranking labels for objective attribute errors. We then trained a Siamese network to learn consistency patterns among these errors, demonstrating through experiments that these patterns align with human subjective judgments. Our method achieved state-of-the-art results in both coarse- and fine-grained tests. Finally, we applied this as a loss function to enhance existing face swapping models, leading to improvements in expression and pose attributes. Extensive experiments confirm the superiority of our method over existing general no-reference image quality assessment metrics and the latest metric for facial image quality assessment, making it well-suited for evaluating face swapping images in real-world scenarios.

title: Hierarchical Channel-aware Optimization Graph Convolution with Contrastive Learning for Emotion Recognition from Body Motion
authors: Yongtao Chen, Zhelong Wang, Daoyong Peng, Hongyu Zhao, Jiahao Jiang, Fang Lin, Xin Shi, Giancarlo Fortino
publish: IEEE Transactions on Affective Computing
abstract: Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) have shown strong potential in skeleton-based emotion recognition. However, existing methods often struggle to capture fine-grained details of key body parts. A Hierarchical Channel-Aware Optimization Graph Convolutional Network (HCO-GCN) is proposed. This framework enhances emotion recognition by integrating hierarchical graph decomposition to capture multi-scale dependencies, and the channel-aware optimization mechanism dynamically learns emotion-discriminative spatial patterns through channel wise computations, focusing on fine-grained motion details of critical body joints. To further enhance the representations learned by HCO-GCN, we introduce a multi-stage, multi-level contrastive learning (MMCL) framework. This design encourages more discriminative feature and graph representations across network stages, promoting improved intra-class consistency and inter-class separability. Furthermore, we propose a multi-task learning strategy within a primary–auxiliary task framework, where prior affective knowledge is integrated into the network through affective feature prediction, emotion classification, and contrastive learning, thereby boosting generalization. Experimental results on two public and one private datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in enhancing emotion recognition performance.

title: Writing Science in the Twenty-First Century: Christopher Thaiss and Ashley Rose Mehlenbacher: [Book Review]
authors: Diane Martinez
publish: IEEE Transactions on Professional Communication

title: Regulating Effect of Asynchronous Interruption on Dynamic Voltage Distribution of Series Vacuum Circuit Breaker
authors: Hao Zhang, Naiwen Zhang, Fengjie Liu, Wenli Li, Feng Zhao, Huaqing Dong, Minfu Liao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: During the breaking process, the voltage distribution of series vacuum circuit breakers is unbalanced. Therefore, the breaking capacity is significantly limited. The common methods are paralleling grading capacitors and resistors across the breaks. However, the parameters of the power system are changed, which may cause over-voltage in the power system. In this article, a double-break synthetic circuit experimental platform is established to control voltage distribution. Reducing the opening speed of higher voltage break, shortening the arcing time of the higher voltage break, and increasing the magnetic field on the higher voltage break can balance the voltage distribution of the series vacuum switch. Under experimental conditions, the most effective measure is shortening the arcing time on the higher voltage break. While the arcing time of the low-voltage break is held constant and the arcing time of the high-voltage break is decreased, the voltage distribution decreases from 65.02% to 55.03%. It can be concluded that changing the parameters of a higher voltage break can improve voltage distribution, obviously.

title: TileInspector-HRD-Net: Efficient Feature Pyramid and Dynamic Head for Real-time High-Resolution Tile Defect Detection
authors: Shi Yue, Chen Fangwei
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Tile surface defect detection plays a critical role in modern industrial quality control systems. However, existing high-precision defect detection models suffer from high computational complexity and large parameter volumes, making them unsuitable for real-time detection and resource-constrained embedded deployment on production lines. Additionally, defects such as cracks, scratches, and color variations often exhibit small sizes and variable morphologies against complex textured backgrounds, posing significant challenges for precise localization and recognition. To address these issues, this paper proposes TileInspector-HRD-Net, a novel real-time high-resolution tile defect detection network featuring three key innovations: a Multi-scale Feature Efficient Aggregation Module (MFEAM) that enhances feature representation while reducing computational costs through lightweight attention and cross-scale dense connections; a Deformable Dynamic Head (DDH) that adaptively focuses on irregular defect shapes via deformable convolutions and dynamic routing mechanisms; and a Multi-task Collaborative Progressive Distillation (MCPD) learning approach that transfers knowledge from a powerful teacher model to a compact student model, particularly emphasizing hard example mining for improved performance under limited data conditions. Experimental results on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that TileInspector-HRD-Net achieves 78.5% mAP@0.5:0.95, 82.3% F1-Score, and 156 FPS inference speed on RTX 3080, while requiring only 4.2M parameters and 8.7G FLOPs, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baselines including YOLOv5s, FCOS, and EfficientDet-D0 in both accuracy and efficiency.

title: Synergistic Pixel- and Object-Level Anomaly Detection for Robust Autonomous Driving
authors: Chuan-Yuan Huang, Yu-An Chen, Cheng-Tsung Chen, Kuan-Wen Chen
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Detecting unexpected obstacles is a critical safety requirement for autonomous driving. Pixel-level anomaly segmentation enables fine-grained localization but often leads to excessive false alarms, whereas object-level detection yields practical bounding boxes but may lack sufficient spatial precision. In practice, false positives are frequently triggered by visually complex backgrounds (e.g., foliage, shadows, and rough textures), particularly in off-road regions, which can lead to spurious warnings and conservative driving behaviors. To address the aforementioned limitations, this study developed a framework called Overestimation-Refined Anomalous Object Detection (OR-AOD), which integrates pixel- and object-level anomaly cues through a bidirectional refinement loop. In this framework, refined pixel-level anomalies guide object attention, and object predictions suppress residual texture-induced false positives in the final anomaly map. The OR-AOD framework uses an overestimation map based on the negative LogSumExp function to enhance sensitivity and employs a lightweight refinement module to regularize its background activation. This framework outputs both a refined anomaly map and anomalous object bounding boxes, providing complementary dense and instance-level cues for downstream decision-making. The proposed framework was tested using the LaF NoKnown and ObstacleTrack benchmarks, and it achieved the Area Under Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) values of 86.9% (with a false positive rate, FPR<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">95</sub>, of 0.9%) and 90.2%, respectively, supporting reliable hazard perception with reduced false alarms for safety-critical deployment.

title: Cross-Modality Fourier Prompt Tuning for Generalizable Visual-Language Tracking
authors: Zhibin Zhang, Wanli Xue, Kaihua Zhang, Yangcan Wu, Shengyong Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Visual-Language Tracking (VLT) enhances traditional visual trackers by incorporating high-level semantic cues from natural language descriptions. However, existing methods often rely on full fine-tuning of the visual encoder and late-stage global alignment between visual and textual features, leading to high training costs and limited generalization ability. To address these issues, we propose CFPTrack, a generalizable VLT framework based on Cross-modality Fourier Prompt Tuning (CFPT), which efficiently aligns visual and textual tokens in the frequency domain using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT). CFPTrack consists of two core components: a CFPT module and a Dynamic Sparse Sinkhorn Attention (DSSA) module. Within CFPT, we introduce Visual-Language Fourier Prompt Tuning (VLFPT), which injects learnable frequency-aware prompts into both visual and language encoders. These prompts capture spatial and frequency-domain statistics and align cross-modal features using optimal transport, enabling the implicit modeling of structural mappings across modalities, thereby bridging the modality gap and improving generalization. The DSSA further enhances robustness by employing a sparse Sinkhorn attention mechanism to highlight informative target cues and suppress background noise. Guided by language semantics, DSSA ranks and prunes visual tokens through a differentiable Top-K sorting strategy, reducing redundancy and improving inference efficiency. Extensive experiments on five VLT benchmarks (TNL2K, LaSOT, LaSOT<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ext</sub>, OTB99-Lang, and VastTrack) and two UAV benchmarks (UAVDT and DTB70) demonstrate that CFPTrack achieves state-of-the-art real-time performance with significantly reduced training time while maintaining competitive accuracy.

title: ${\mathcal{L}}_{\infty}$ Control of Switched T–S Fuzzy Systems Under Relieved Asynchronous Switching: A Zonotope Analysis Strategy
authors: Qian Shen, Shengyuan Xu, Yinlong Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
abstract: This article addresses the zonotopic <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\mathcal {L}}_{\infty } $</tex-math> </inline-formula> dynamic output-feedback control problem for discrete-time switched Takagi–Sugeno (T–S) fuzzy systems. To overcome the conservatism of existing methods, a novel relieved asynchronous average dwell time (ADT) switching scheme is proposed, which successfully eliminates the conventional restrictive requirement that the subsystem dwell time (DT) must strictly exceed the maximum asynchronous duration. First, state and output zonotopes are constructed, facilitating the introduction of multiple radius and center-distance functions. Distinct from Lyapunov-dependent approaches, by leveraging the radius- and center-distance-based analysis technique, sufficient conditions are derived to guarantee the dual convergence and the prescribed <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\mathcal {L}}_{\infty } $</tex-math> </inline-formula> performance of the zonotopes. Subsequently, building upon these zonotopic results, the allowable ADT switching signals and switched fuzzy dynamic output-feedback controllers are codesigned to achieve the stability of the closed-loop systems. Finally, the superiority and effectiveness of the proposed zonotopic control scheme are validated through an illustrative switched T–S fuzzy example.

title: Wideband Evanescent Mode Bandpass Filter Based on Double Ridge Rectangular Waveguide Partially Loaded With Broadside Printed Strips
authors: Metehan Bulut, Birsen Saka
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: The double ridge rectangular waveguide (DRRW) partially loaded with a dielectric substrate on which broadside copper strips are printed is presented for the evanescent mode filter design with a wide passband and a wide spurious-free stopband. Broadside strips printed on the substrate miniaturize the DRRW and cause the monomode bandwidth of the DRRW to widen. The filter topology is based on creating empty evanescent mode DRRW sections along the longitudinal direction of the broadside printed strips loaded-DRRW (BPS-DRRW) by cutting the corresponding parts of the printed circuit board (PCB) off. The PCB is sandwiched between the upper and lower rigid metallic parts of the DRRW. Conducting posts at the edges of the substrate ensure the continuity of the narrow wall current. A fifth-order, all-pole bandpass filter with input/output microstrip line transition circuits is designed for a passband of 2.5–3.5 GHz and a wide spurious-free stopband up to 18 GHz with a rejection greater than 25 dB.

title: Context-Aware Recipe Recommendations using NLP with Multilingual Support
authors: Vandana B.S, Manjula Gururaj, Diyadarshini Amin, Disha Rani
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Technology growth paved the way for automation in the field of cooking and the development of intelligent assistant that helps in processing natural language queries to impart accurate, responsive recipe recommendations to the users. The Hybrid NLP Framework, a full-stack dynamic application is focus is major focus of this paper. This application is designed to provide multi-language platform for easy understanding. Queries are analysed on a rule-based approach and a model based on TF-IDF with Cosine Similarity for clarity. The result obtained by using TF-IDF and Cosine Similarity produces an overall accuracy of 76.4% with balanced precision, recall. The F1-scores and the rule-based approach achieved an entity accuracy of 80.95%. For further conversational abilities, the new model called Large Language Models (LLMs) will be incorporated. This allows the assistant to understand all the inquiries and recommend ingredient alternatives thereby as a smart culinary partner. The combination of rule-based accuracy, TF-IDF and Cosine Similarity, and LLM flexibility illustrates a hybrid framework of precise, and user-friendly recipe suggestions.

title: Multi-Year Upgrade of Pre-Existing Power System to Support SQKD for Increasing Key rates
authors: Mariam Gado, Muhammad Ismail
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: This paper studies the problem of upgrading the pre-existing cyber layer of a transmission power system to generate unconditionally secure keys using semi-quantum key distribution (SQKD) for an increasing minimum required key rate over years. Only a subset of cyber nodes and links is required to be upgraded to full quantum servers and fiber links, respectively. The upgrade problem is formulated as a binary optimization problem and a genetic algorithm is proposed to provide a solution to reduce computational complexity. In the IEEE 14-bus test system, for a source rate of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">8</sup> photon per second (pps) and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10^{10} \text{pps}$</tex>, the proposed algorithm requires 48.48% and 67.92% less upgrades compared to the quantum key distribution (QKD) solutions, respectively. In the IEEE 39-bus test system, for a source rate of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10^{7} \text{pps}, 10^{8} \text{pps}$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10^{10} \text{pps}$</tex>, the proposed algorithm requires <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$70 \%, 80 {\%}$</tex>, and 90%, respectively, less updates compared to the QKD solutions.

title: LLM-Assisted Side-Channel Leakage Analysis of AES Power Traces
authors: Sufian Al Majmaie, Niraj Prasad Bhatta, Fathi Amsaad
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Side-channel attacks exploit physical leakages such as power consumption to recover secret information from cryptographic hardware. Among the most effective techniques, Correlation Power Analysis (CPA) can identify key-dependent leakage by correlating measured traces with predicted leakage models. In this work, we present an LLM-assisted framework for side-channel leakage analysis of AES power traces. The proposed approach first performs CPA on the CHES CTF dataset to localize the temporal region of key-dependent leakage using a Hamming-weight-based leakage model. After identifying the leakage point, statistical features of the leakage region are extracted, including correlation peak, mean power, variance, and standard deviation. These results are then provided to a Large Language Model (LLM) to generate high-level security interpretation, explain likely cryptographic operations responsible for leakage, and suggest practical countermeasures. Experimental analysis demonstrates that the framework can detect meaningful leakage regions in AES traces and automate the interpretation process for security reporting. This work highlights the potential of combining traditional statistical side-channel techniques with LLM-based reasoning for automated hardware security assessment.

title: Assessment of Grid Voltage Fluctuations on the Performance of a Three-Phase Grid-Integrated Inverter
authors: Mensia Gariely, Peter M. Makolo, Jackson J. Justo, Ramesh C. Bansal, Galina Demidova
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Grid-connected inverters face challenges from abnormal grid conditions, such as voltage fluctuations, imbalances, and harmonics, which become more critical with increased penetration of renewable energy sources. To analyze and predict the inverter's response, this paper employs MATLAB Simulink for simulation studies. Simulating these scenarios allows for safe testing of control strategies like the Phase Locked Loop, LCL filter, and grid support functionalities like Low Voltage Ride Through. The paper demonstrates how voltage fluctuation impacts power quality, harmonic distortion, and stability during grid disturbances.

title: Explainable AI Models for Transparent and Ethical Customer Relationship Management Decision
authors: Sourabh Kukar
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Customer Churn is a severe problem of banking institutions, which directly affects profitability and sustainability. To solve this problem, this paper offers an explainable artificial intelligence (XAI)-based framework of transparent and ethical customer relationship management (CRM) decision-making, namely, churn prediction. The dataprocessing, feature engineering, and class balancing based on SMOTE, as well as machine learning models in the form of the ensemble, were used with the help of the Bank Turnover (Churn Modelling) dataset. While comparing the Random Forest, Logistic Regression. The experimental results indicate that ensemble models are better than traditional methods, where Gradient Boosting has the highest ROC-AUC of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 6. 1 4 \%}$</tex> and XGBoost has the highest accuracy of 84.25%. To increase transparency, several XAI methods, such as, partial dependence plot, permutation feature importance, and decision path analysis and individual conditional expectation plot were utilized. These results indicate that behavioral and demographic characteristics are the major sources of churn, which allow reliable, interpretable, and practical CRM strategies.

title: Secure and Scalable IoT Data Sharing with Dynamic Group Management via Re-Encryption
authors: Bowu Zhang, Jinho Hwang
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Despite its growing popularity, the Internet of Things (IoT) poses significant challenges to data security and user privacy. These challenges stem from ubiquitous data access, device heterogeneity, and, most critically, the lack of effective security mechanisms such as robust encryption and access control. Existing IoT security solutions often suffer from low efficiency and limited scalability, making them unsuitable for deployment across large-scale IoT networks with constrained computational resources. To address these issues, we propose a new secure data collection and sharing scheme tailored for IoT applications. Specifically, we leverage Partially Homomorphic Encryption (PHE) to strengthen data security by supporting secure temporal data association. Furthermore, we design flexible data sharing mechanisms, including group-based sharing that utilizes re-encryption to efficiently handle dynamic membership changes within IoT groups. Our analysis demonstrates that the proposed scheme not only offers strong cryptographic guarantees for both data collection and sharing but also achieves high efficiency in managing dynamic group keys, making it well-suited for resource-constrained IoT environments.

title: Vision Transformers for Accurate Inferior Alveolar Nerve Classification in Cone Beam Computed Tomography
authors: Roopitha C H, Veena Mayya, Vathsala Patil
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: The precise localization of the Inferior Alveolar Nerve (IAN) is crucial for safe dental and maxillofacial surgical procedures. This study compares and contrasts five Transformer architectures—Vision Transformer (ViT), Shifted Window Transformer (SwinT), Class-Attention in Image Transformers (CaiT), Gradient Focal Transformer (GFT), and the Data-Efficient Image Transformer (DeiT)—for automated classification of IAN position in Cone Beam Computed Tomography (CBCT) images. SwinT outperformed the other models and achieved the highest accuracy of 80.00% due to its hierarchical, multi-scale window attention mechanism, which effectively captures sensitive anatomical information and generalizes well on limited medical imaging data. In contrast, GFT performed lowest, mainly because of extreme overfitting caused by the gating operations, which enhanced training characteristics but were unable to generalize to new data. Overall, the results reveal that transformer-based designs have great potential for precise and reliable IAN position classification in CBCT for universal healthcare systems, especially those with hierarchical attention like SwinT.

title: Real-Time Violence Detection Using Lightweight Deep Learning
authors: Santhosh S, Ashwin Shenoy M, Karthik Prabhu, Kiran Patgar, Sayed Anas Ahmed, Shravan SP
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: The rising need for automated surveillance systems has increased the necessity for accurate and efficient violence detection models. The practice of manually monitoring public spaces can be unreliable and takes an excessive amount of time to enact a response, especially during critical situations. This paper proposes a light-weight deep learning framework that employs the MobileNetV3Small convolutional neural network (CNN) to analyze video sequences that are either violent or non-violent, in real time. The architecture takes advantage of a lightweight convolutional backbone to achieve efficient inference while maintaining high accuracy classifications. The methodology is evaluated on two benchmark datasets, namely, the Real-World Fighting (RWF-2000) dataset and the Real-Life Violence Dataset (RLVD). Different preprocessing steps, like frame normalization and frame augmentation, were used to support generalization across various lighting and motion scenarios. The results showed that the computational time in classifying a video sequence was approximately 95% accuracy, with 88% precision, and 92 F1-score, outperforming traditional models, while sustaining considerable computational efficiency. The results confirm the model’s suitability for real-time monitoring and safety applications with respect to smart surveillance, law enforcement, and social media moderation, to name a few. In summary, the proposed MobileNetV3Small- based violence detection framework achieves high classification accuracy while requiring minimal resources i.e deployment in modern computing environments where resources are constrained.
