title: Dog Activity Detection from Accelerometer–Gyroscope Signals Using an Integrated CNN–BiLSTM Model
authors: Ankitha K, Sankirthan, Keerthan R Sanil, Gautham Kini T, Arshith, Krishnaraj Chadaga
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Making use of wearable sensors in identifying dog behavior helps in monitoring veterinary health and improves both training and understanding of the animals. This study presents an integrated CNN-BiLSTM to allow the multi classification of dog behavior using supervised activity recognition with extensive feature engineering, a semi supervised gap filling process, and extensive comparison of optimizers to optimize accuracy for each model and completeness for the dataset. The dataset used contains triaxial accelerometer and gyroscope data collected from several dogs with 100 Hz sampling rate using ActiGraph GT9X Link sensors. The data for input were organized into overlapping 2 second windows containing 200 samples. The hybrid CNN-BiLSTM model architecture combines convolutional layers to extract spatial feature with bidirectional LSTMs and a fully connected dense layer. Among the several optimizers used AdamW performed the most highly rated results, with a valida tion accuracy and weighted F1 score of 93.27respectively. The model associated with acceptable results in overall precision (over 91scores. The multistage gap filling process involved using model predictions with confidence ( softmax probaility) ≥ 0.5 to forward and backward filling missing labels and even neighbor mode selections with a global fallback effectively to fill in approximately 25gap filling method improves dog activity recognition accuracy by 28.36framework to handle imbalanced and semi labels time series data in animal behavior research.

title: A Lightweight Security Framework for Stateless Communication in Low-Power IoT Systems
authors: Sheikh Tareq Ahmed, Josemaria Chukwuemeka Osuorah, Annamalai Annamalai
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Low-power Internet of Things (IoT) devices are increasingly deployed in infrastructure-limited and missioncritical environments, where secure and efficient communication is essential despite stringent energy, memory, and computational constraints. Existing security solutions often rely on session-based protocols, control-plane signaling, or infrastructure support, which are poorly suited for dynamic and resource-constrained IoT deployments. This paper proposes a lightweight, stateless security framework that provides packet-level authentication, replay protection, and integrity verification for low-power IoT systems operating over untrusted broadcast channels. The proposed design embeds freshness and authentication directly into each packet and enables security-aware forwarding decisions based solely on local state and observable link information. By eliminating persistent sessions and centralized coordination, the framework reduces overhead and attack surface while preserving scalability and robustness under intermittent connectivity. This work focuses on architectural design and threat-driven analysis, establishing a practical foundation for secure stateless communication in low-power IoT environments.

title: ICSHIve: A Framework for Measurable and High-Interactive Honeypots in Industrial Control Systems
authors: Wenlu Zhang, Naipeng Dong, Taejun Choi, Abigail Koay, Ryan K. L. Ko
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Existing industrial control systems (ICS) honeypots primarily focus on simulating programmable logic controllers (PLCs). However, these honeypots are mostly considered “low-interaction” honeypots, resulting in ineffective tracing of attacker tactics, techniques, and procedures. From existing literature, we observed a lack of metrics and frameworks to guide “high-interaction” ICS honeypot development. In this article, we propose metrics to measure ICS honeypot interactivity and apply them to assess existing honeypots. Using our metrics, we design ICSHIve, a novel framework to build high-interaction honeypots across PLC models and vendors. Guided by our framework, we implement honeypots supporting prominent vendors: Allen-Bradley and Schneider—validating ICSHIve’s generalizability and device-independent benefits. ICSHIve is evaluated using MITRE ATT&CK to showcase its realism against state-of-the-art reconnaissance tools and accurate physical process simulation. We deployed ICSHIve on Amazon Web Services where it demonstrated high interactivity, accuracy, and realistic responses and significant effectiveness in attracting attackers. ICSHIve’s source code is available online.

title: A Sandwich-like Dual-band Shared-aperture Dielectric Lens Antenna with Large Frequency Ratio and Wide Scanning Range
authors: Yuntao Ye, Zhixi Liang, Shaoyong Zheng, Kwok Wa Leung
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: A sandwich-like, dual-band shared-aperture dielectric lens antenna (DLA) configuration is proposed to realize a wide scanning range in the microwave (MW) and millimeter-wave (MMW) bands with a large frequency ratio. The configuration comprises three vertically stacked lens layers separated by air gaps. This layered structure spatially decouples the propagation regions: electromagnetic (EM) waves of the MW band propagate throughout the entire lens, whereas those of the MMW band are confined to the middle layer by the air gaps and frequency-selective metamaterials (MTMs) in the top and bottom layers. This decoupling fundamentally avoids severe modal interference. Moreover, this configuration allows the adoption of a heterogeneous MTM design with distinct periods across layers, reducing structural complexity and enhancing fabrication tolerance. Two prototypes were designed, fabricated, and measured for verification. The cylindrical DLA covers the 5.8 GHz ISM band and the n258 band (24.25–27.5 GHz), achieving a frequency ratio of 4.48:1 and a wide azimuthal scanning angle of ±77° in both bands. The spherical DLA is used to illustrate the configuration’s scalability in expanding the frequency ratio and scanning dimensions. Covering the 2.4 GHz ISM and n258 bands with an ultra-large frequency ratio of 10.61:1, it achieves an azimuthal scanning angle of ±55° in both bands and an elevational scanning angle of ±55° in the MW band.

title: Dynamic assessment of the competencies and systems-thinking skills of civil aviation personnel in an age of technological breakthroughs
authors: Kayrat Koshekov, Marina Kalekeyeva, Almat Sakhov, Nataliia Levchenko, Abay Koshekov, Kazbek Aldamzharov, Yakub Kurbanov
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Technological breakthroughs in the rapidly developing aviation industry have exacerbated the problems with associated assessing the competencies and systems-thinking skills of personnel. Traditional assessment methods based on static models and measuring previously acquired knowledge are losing relevance because they fail to capture critical indices such as flexible thinking, adaptability, and a willingness to continuously learn. Herein, a comprehensive method for assessing the competencies and systems-thinking skills of civil aviation personnel was developed. Item response theory was applied to survey data from the personnel of an airport in Kazakhstan to develop latent competency indices. The relationships between indices were considered through a dynamic Bayesian network and their dynamics were predicted up to 2030 across three development scenarios. The Technique for Order of Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution method integrated the individual indices into a single metric the human capital readiness index that quantitatively assessed competencies and systems-thinking skills in the context of rapid technological changes. The results demonstrated the ability of the proposed method to objectively assess the potential of civil aviation personnel in a dynamic and technology-intensive environment, confirming the need to rethink traditional approaches to personnel assessment. The proposed method is expected to facilitate the development of targeted training and retraining programs as well as the establishment of qualification standards for civil aviation personnel.

title: Secure Two-Party Matrix Multiplication from Lattices and Its Application to Encrypted Control
authors: Kaoru Teranishi
publish: IEEE Control Systems Letters
abstract: In this study, we propose a two-party computation protocol for approximate matrix multiplication of fixed-point numbers. The proposed protocol is provably secure under standard lattice-based cryptographic assumptions and enables matrix multiplication at a desired approximation level within a single round of communication. We demonstrate the feasibility of the protocol by applying it to the secure implementation of a linear control law. Our evaluation reveals that the client achieves lower online computational complexity compared to the original controller computation, while ensuring the privacy of controller inputs, outputs, and parameters. Furthermore, a numerical example confirms that the proposed method maintains sufficient precision of control inputs even in the presence of approximation and quantization errors.

title: Edge Coordinated 6G IoT System for Autonomous Biomechanical Analysis and Fatigue Perception Performance Optimization in Competitive Sports
authors: Ting Liang, Huazhe Wan
publish: IEEE Communications Standards Magazine
abstract: This tutorial presents a standards-oriented framework for an edge-coordinated 6G IoT system that performs autonomous biomechanical analysis and fatigue perception for injury-prevention in competitive sports, positioned as a demanding remote patient monitoring use case for 6G healthcare networks. An interoperable device-edge-cloud reference architecture is specified to convert heterogeneous multimodal wearables data into edge-resident fatigue-risk inference under low-latency/high-reliability (LLHR/URLLC) service guarantees, while the cloud supports longitudinal analytics, lifecycle governance, and controlled model rollout. To meet SI priorities, the article defines minimum interoperability profiles (data models, interfaces, time synchronization, and consent metadata), outlines autonomous edge orchestration with QoS-aware task scheduling for multi-athlete operation, and integrates privacy-preserving collaborative analytics through consent-gated federated learning. A conformance-oriented benchmarking suite is then proposed covering mobility/handover, overload, backhaul loss, sensor degradation, and multi-vendor integration with measurable KPIs and evidence artifacts to enable repeatable testing, certification-style evaluation, and trustworthy industrial deployment beyond sports medicine.

title: State of Power Prediction for Series-Connected Battery Packs Considering the Inconsistency in State of Health of Cells
authors: Simin Peng, Jinkang Chen, Daohan Zhang, Yuanliang Wang, Jiarong Kan, Aihua Tang, Yan Ma, Michael Pecht
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: The state of health (SOH) across a battery pack varies due to differing degradation rates among individual cells, introducing dynamic errors into state of power (SOP) predictions. A significant challenge for existing SOP prediction methods lies in simultaneously achieving cell aging identification, accurate SOP estimation, and compensation for errors arising from SOH inconsistency. To overcome these limitations, a multi-stage collaborative optimization approach for enhanced SOP prediction for series-connected battery pack is developed. The aging degrees of battery cell are quantitatively classified using fuzzy C-means clustering combined with fuzzy logic system. An adaptive step-size unscented Kalman filter is presented to improve state of charge estimation accuracy for aging cells. Accurate compensation for dynamic SOP errors is achieved by a Transformer-bidirectional long short-term memory (BiLSTM) model coupled with a Chebyshev-black Kite algorithm. Experimental results under UDDS conditions demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high SOP prediction accuracy for series-connected battery pack with a root mean square error of 0.138, which is 70.8% lower than that of grey wolf optimizer-Transformer-BiLSTM (0.472), while maintaining robust performance across different SOH levels.

title: A Worm-like Robot for Ferromagnetic Surface Inspection_supp2-3683248.mp4
authors: Rezvan Nasiri
publish: N/A
abstract: Industrial inspection is a crucial task for ensuring the longterm maintenance of infrastructures with ferromagnetic surfaces, such as power plants, wind turbines, and chemical facilities. These environments are often unsafe for human access, motivating the use of climbing robots to perform inspection tasks reliably and repeatedly; nevertheless, the existing robots often have limited maneuverability, reduced stability during transitions, and high energy consumption. In this article, we present a novel robotic system inspired by the body morphology of caterpillars. The design consists of three modules connected via a spring joint mechanism and equipped with a passive magnetic adhesion system. The design’s maneuverability, stability, energy efficiency, and falling robustness are studied analytically and experimentally. Experimental results confirmed the robot’s ability to perform internal/external corner transitions, thin surface transitions, pipe circling, and surface turning in real-world environments, including rusty, coated, and uneven surfaces. Furthermore, we found that compared with simple locomotion, the designed wormlike locomotion yielded an average 10.2% increase in forward velocity and 6.7% reduction in the cost of transportation (COT). These findings suggest that the proposed design offers a practical and versatile approach for inspecting ferromagnetic surfaces.

title: Computable Knowledge Discovery in Soft Magnetic Materials via Large Language Model Driven Literature Meta-Analysis
authors: Ruixuan Ying, Yunhao Liang, Takuya Taniguchi, Yifan Song, Zhenyu Long, Satoshi Okamoto
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: Soft magnetic materials have accumulated a vast body of literature over decades, yet their large-scale thematic organization and long-term evolution remain insufficiently quantified. Here, we present a large language model (LLM)-driven literature meta-analysis framework applied to approximately 720,000 candidate Scopus records, from which 108,949 cleaned soft-magnetic-materials abstracts were used for embedding-based semantic analysis. The abstracts are encoded using a large embedding model, projected into two dimensions by UMAP, clustered by HDBSCAN, interpreted through hierarchical LLM summarization, and further profiled along materials-science dimensions. A kernel density estimation (KDE)-based differential density analysis is also introduced to quantify long-term topic growth and decline in the shared semantic space. The analysis resolves 310 non-noise topic clusters and distinguishes sustained growth hotspots from declining thematic regions over multi-decadal timescales. These results demonstrate a scalable and interpretable framework for artificial intelligence (AI)-assisted literature intelligence in soft magnetic materials and related materials domains.

title: News
authors: Jackie Snow, Rahul Rao, Tereza Pultarova, Matthew S. Smith, Katherine Bourzac
publish: IEEE Spectrum
abstract: The Colorado River begins as snow. Every spring, the mountain snowpack of the Rockies melts into streams that then feed into reservoirs supplying 40 million people across seven U.S. states. The system has worked, more or less, for a century. That century is over.

title: Statistical Properties of Closure Phases in SAR Interferometry
authors: Yan Yuan, Paco López-Dekker
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The concept of closure phases is introduced in Inter-ferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) as a circular summation of three multilooked interferometric phases. Traditionally considered conservative, recent studies have shown real data examples exhibiting non-conservative closure phases resulting from phase noise and geophysical signals. Current literature attributes geophysical closure phases to dielectric variation in media, volumetric scattering in combination with perpendicular baseline, and skewed differential motion. While non-zero closure phases have been studied at local scales, a comprehensive and systematic statistical characterization of closure phases across broader spatial and temporal scales remains lacking. In this study, we present extensive analyses of closure phases across the diverse Iberian Peninsula to provide a general perspective on their occurrence, underscore the limitations of current understanding in explaining the observed signals, and qualitatively explore potential unexplained origins. Our results confirm that statistically significant closure phases are common rather than exceptional, show that their signatures vary across sub-regions depending on local geophysical conditions, and quantify that for the geophysical component, approximately five-sixths of the variance remains unexplained by existing models.

title: Full Speed Range Sensorless Drive of Nonsalient PMSMs Based on PLL-Type Speed and Position Estimator
authors: Mengji Zhao, Quntao An, Guoqiang Zhang, Xiaoguang Zhang, Gaolin Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: In the drive system of nonsalient permanent magnet synchronous motors, sensorless control at low speed remains challenging, since the existing saliency-based and model-based methods may be ineffective. To address this issue, this article proposes a sensorless control method capable of full-speed-range operation. In the proposed method, an extended state observer estimates the back-electromotive force in the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm{\gamma}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm{\delta}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> frame, while a phase-locked loop-type estimator extracts the rotor position and speed. Experimental results demonstrate that the motor can start up from any initial position under rated load and achieve reliable speed reversal. Moreover, the method maintains high estimation accuracy even when the motor runs at 2% rated speed. This indicates that a single-method full-speed-range sensorless solution is feasible.

title: Semi-Supervised Semantic Segmentation of Remote Sensing Images with Foundation Model Distillation and Multi-Level Consistency
authors: Pu Xiao, Xiaoyan Lu, Yao Sun, Yuting Dong, Ji Zhao, Yanfei Zhong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Semantic segmentation of remote sensing images plays a crucial role in urban planning, environmental monitoring, and ecosystem management. However, its development is often constrained by the high cost of obtaining pixel-level annotations. Existing semi-supervised learning approaches mitigate this problem by leveraging a small amount of labeled data together with abundant unlabeled data. However, in scenarios with limited annotations, their performance is often degraded by pseudo-label noise and the insufficient utilization of structural information in unlabeled data. To address these challenges, we propose FDMatch, a semi-supervised semantic segmentation framework for remote sensing images that integrates foundation model distillation with multi-level consistency learning. Specifically, FDMatch employs a pre-trained remote sensing foundation model as a knowledge source and transfers its rich spatial priors and high-level semantics to a lightweight segmentation network through an adaptive distillation mechanism, thereby enhancing the feature discrimination capability under limited supervision. Furthermore, a multi-level consistency regularization strategy is developed to overcome the limitation of conventional pixel-level consistency, which explores the potential structural information in unlabeled data by modeling inter-class dependency patterns and inter-pixel correlations, thereby providing informative and reliable supervisory signals for model optimization. Comprehensive evaluations on three benchmark remote sensing datasets demonstrate that FDMatch consistently outperforms existing semi-supervised methods across different label ratios, particularly exhibiting superior accuracy and robustness in scenarios with scarce labels.

title: Collaborative Voltage Harmonic Mitigation at a Remote Sensitive Node in Inverter-Based Islanded Microgrids
authors: Jaume Carol, Miguel Castilla, Jaume Miret, Àngel Borrell, Antonio Camacho
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: The presence of nonlinear loads in islanded microgrids produces harmful voltage harmonics that need to be addressed. Existing solutions established in the state-of-the-art aim to compensate the harmonic distortion at the node where the problematic loads are connected or at the inverters’ output. However, certain scenarios may require mitigation to be performed at a given node of the microgrid where sensitive equipment is installed. Given that the sensitive node could be located at any point of the network, it should be considered as a remote node from the inverters’ perspective. This article presents a novel control strategy to mitigate the voltage harmonic distortion from a remote sensitive node using the system’s already present grid-forming inverters. Its novelty lies in the use of complex-valued unilateral band-pass filters to reduce the voltage distortion of the different harmonic orders and their corresponding positive-negative sequences. Unlike conventional integrator-based approaches, the finite-gain behavior of the proposed controller allows inverters to collaboratively work toward the compensation, enabling seamless control integration. Furthermore, the use of optimally designed control parameters guarantees a fair distribution of the control effort between all inverters. The work includes the mathematical framework of the system followed by the design guidelines of the control to achieve its distinct characteristics. Finally, a selection of experimental results is presented to validate the behavior of the proposal.

title: Nonintrusive Current Inversion for Paralleled Chips in Press-Pack IGBTs With a Coil Matrix and Data-Driven Modeling
authors: Yuanfang Lu, Hong Shen, Xinlin Xu, Huan Niu, Hui Su, Bo Yao, Lei Qi, Xiangyu Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Current imbalance among paralleled chips in press-pack IGBTs (PPIs), driven by parameter mismatch and parasitic differences, poses a serious reliability concern. To assess this current distribution, intrusive measurement schemes must open the package and thus compromise insulation and hermeticity, making them unsuitable for production testing and field inspections. Nonintrusive magnetic sensing could reflect the internal current distribution, but conventional analytical methods only yield low-order indicators such as equivalent current bias and cannot resolve finer details. To address this issue, an inversion model between the external flux measurements and chip currents is first established via a forward transfer matrix, and the singular-value spectrum of this matrix is analyzed to derive a quantitative relationship between chip-current inversion error and sensor configuration. On this basis, a nonintrusive, data-driven chip-current inversion method is proposed. A one-time sacrificial measurement uses printed-circuit board (PCB) Rogowski coils and an external coil matrix to acquire chip-current ground truth and external magnetic flux for training a backpropagation neural network that maps external flux measurements to chip currents. Experiments on a five-chip PPI yield an average error of about 1.44% on an independent test set, demonstrating that accurate chip-current inversion could be achieved without opening the package.

title: Joint Beamforming and Position Optimization for IRS-Aided SWIPT with Movable Antennas
authors: Yanze Zhu, Qingqing Wu, Xinrong Guan, Ziyuan Zheng, Honghao Wang, Wen Chen, Yang Liu, Yuan Guo
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications
abstract: Simultaneous wireless information and power transfer (SWIPT) has been envisioned as a promising technology to support ubiquitous connectivity and reliable sustainability in Internet-of-Things (IoT) networks, which, however, generally suffers from severe attenuation caused by long distance propagation, leading to inefficient wireless power transfer (WPT) for energy harvesting receivers (EHRs). This paper proposes to introduce emerging intelligent reflecting surface (IRS) and movable antenna (MA) technologies into SWIPT systems aiming at enhancing information transmission for information decoding receivers (IDRs) and improving receive power of EHRs. We consider to maximize the weighted sum-rate of IDRs via jointly optimizing the active and passive beamforming at the base station (BS) and IRS, respectively, together with the positions of MAs, while guaranteeing the individual requirement of each EHR. To tackle this challenging task due to the non-convexity of associated optimization, we develop an efficient algorithm combining weighted minimal mean square error (WMMSE), block coordinate descent (BCD), majorization-minimization (MM), and penalty duality decomposition (PDD) frameworks. Besides, we present a feasibility characterization method to examine the achievability of EHRs’ requirements. Simulation results demonstrate the significant benefits of our proposed solutions. Particularly, the optimized IRS configuration may exhibit higher performance gain than MA counterpart under our considered scenario.

title: Focused Region Exclusion Learning for Cross-View Geo-Localization
authors: Shuangjiang Li, Guopu Zhu, Hongli Zhang, Xiangyang Luo, Yicong Zhou, Ligang Wu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Cross-view geo-localization aims to establish matching relationships between images of the same scene captured from different viewpoints. However, significant viewpoint discrepancies can render some visual information ineffective across views, and complex scene structures lead to dispersed retrieval-relevant features, which together pose substantial challenges to geo-localization. To address these issues, existing methods often rely on alignment-based strategies to reduce viewpoint discrepancies and suppress irrelevant information during retrieval. Nevertheless, such methods typically exhibit limited generalizability across diverse scenarios and often fail to effectively handle complex visual scenes. To fully leverage effective visual information, we propose a novel vision-centric method called Focused Region Exclusion Learning (FREL). The method employs primary and auxiliary branch types, in which the auxiliary branch uses attended regions of the primary branch as guidance and actively explores complementary cues beyond those regions, thereby uncovering discriminative visual information overlooked by the primary branch and enhancing the diversity and robustness of the learned features. To achieve dynamic modulation of visual attention across branches, we propose a heatmap-guided mask filtering module. In addition, a cross-guided learning loss is incorporated to facilitate effective feature fusion between the different branches. The results of experiments on four benchmark datasets CVUSA, CVACT, VIGOR, and University-1652 demonstrate that the proposed method exhibits strong dataset applicability and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across these benchmarks.

title: The Lighter Side of Control Systems
authors: Brian Douglas
publish: IEEE Control Systems

title: Text Prompt Controllable Image Exposure Correction
authors: Xiang Huang, Yuan-Ming Li, Kun-Yu Lin, Qing Zhang, Jian-Fang Hu, Wei-Shi Zheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Exposure correction aims to adjust the brightness of an image to a level that is satisfactory to humans. Current exposure correction methods almost always perform global correction on the entire image, ignoring the need for humans to perform brightness adjustments on local areas of the image. We propose a method to adjust the brightness of any object in an image using a text prompt, named the PCEC model: given an image and text prompt that tells the model what objects need to be brighter or darker, the model will follow these prompts to adjust the brightness of the corresponding objects in the image. Due to the lack of datasets that satisfy the task of controllable exposure correction, we propose a novel data engine to construct a large-scale dataset. Specifically, we built a data engine through Tag2Text, Grounding DINO, and Segment Anything to assist us in training our model without a corresponding dataset. In addition, we design a boundary harmonization framework to eliminate the disharmonious boundary problem that occurs when our model adjusts the brightness of certain backgrounds. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method through extensive experiments.

title: Improved-PPO-based Adaptive Safety-Critical Control for Orbit-Attitude Takeover of Failed Spacecraft with Cellular Satellites
authors: Xuanyu Luo, Chuang Liu, Xiaokui Yue
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This research investigates an adaptive safety-critical control strategy for the orbit-attitude takeover of failed spacecraft using cellular satellites under stringent motion constraints. The proposed framework, based on an improved proximal policy optimization (IPPO) algorithm, efficiently transforms the unsafe desired trajectories into safe ones, ensuring collision avoidance and protection of onboard optical payloads from direct sunlight. To address challenges such as unknown model parameters and unavailable velocity states, a radial basis function neural network (RBFNN)-based state observer is designed alongside an adaptive finite-time controller. This approach achieves high tracking accuracy without explicit parameter identification, thereby reducing the computational burden. Comparative simulations validate the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed control scheme.

title: Digitally-tunable Mixer Design and Simulation with Modulated Signals within an Adaptive Receiver Front-end and with FPGA-based Demodulation
authors: Diptashree Das, Francesco Pessia, Minghan Liu, Mohammad Abdi, Francesco Restuccia, Marvin Onabajo
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Circuits and Systems
abstract: Reconfigurability and self-optimization are becoming essential in radio frequency integrated circuit (RFIC) design due to the rapid growth of wireless devices and dynamically changing spectrum conditions. This work presents a CMOS mixer with a wide digitally tunable bias current, enabling machine learning (ML) assisted adaptation within a self-optimizing receiver (RX) architecture. The proposed mixer topology incorporates a programmable helper current (PHC) circuit to preserve voltage headroom across a broad tuning range while dynamically minimizing power consumption according to system-level requirements. Post-layout results demonstrate up to 8× power reduction while maintaining an input third-order intermodulation intercept point (IIP3) greater than −2.4 dBm across all operating modes. The adaptive mixer topology is integrated into a fully tunable RX front-end consisting of a digitally programmable LNA, in-phase/quadrature-phase (I/Q) mixers, and a tunable low-pass filter. A hardware–software co-simulation framework is introduced, combining a spectrum-agile GNU Radio transmitter, the CMOS adaptive front-end, and an FPGA-based real-time demodulation engine for system-level performance extraction. The front-end achieves a power tuning range of 0.73–3.60 mW, with corresponding conversion gain of 20–33.6 dB, noise figure of 12.2–30.5 dB, IIP3 of −20 to −7.6 dBm, and 1-dB compression point of −27.4 to −14.7 dBm. Wireless system-based simulations resulted in error vector magnitudes ranging from 4% to 18.7%, demonstrating effective power–performance tradeoffs. The results validate the wide-range adaptive power scaling capability with preservation of adequate linearity and noise performance under varying wireless conditions.

title: Beyond Surface Similarity: Evaluating Structured Output Consistency in Large Language Models
authors: Jinze Yu, Guanghui Wang, Yanping Deng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) become central to production systems requiring structured data generation, evaluating output consistency remains an open challenge. Existing metrics—designed for natural language text—fail to capture the semantics of structured formats like JSON, leading to both false positives and false negatives. We present an evaluation framework with two contributions: (1) a semanticstructural similarity metric that recognizes equivalent representations while detecting breaking changes, and (2) a consistency scoring methodology that aggregates pairwise measurements to quantify model reliability. Through experiments on 2,400 synthetic test cases with controlled variations, we show that our metric scores 0.86–0.90 for semantic equivalents while scoring near-zero for structural violations (flattening: 0.051, nesting: 0.000)—whereas BERTScore (0.90–0.99) and DeepDiff (0.78–0.81) assign high similarity to both, failing to distinguish them. Benchmarking six LLMs reveals that Claude-3.7-Sonnet maintains near-perfect structural reliability even at T = 0.9, while other models degrade substantially. The framework enables informed decisions about model selection, temperature configuration, and prompt design for structured output tasks.

title: Collision-Free Output Feedback Distributed Coordination of MASs Under Unreliable Communication Topologies
authors: Can Zhao, Liwei An, Lili Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: This article investigates the problem of collision-free output feedback distributed coordination (OFDC) of multi-agent systems under unreliable communication topologies. Unreliable communication topologies (e.g., induced by communication failures and adversarial attacks) can disrupt cooperative decision-making among agents, and only output information being available can also limit the transient performance of the local controller, thereby inducing collisions. A distributed command governor is presented, which dynamically generates safe formation command signals. When the communication is disrupted, sufficiently loose collision-avoidance formation signals are generated to tolerate the effects of transient performance degradation caused by unreliable communication topologies. Incorporating the dynamic high-gain scaling technique, an observer-based safety tracking controller is designed. The barrier function is introduced into the dynamic scaling variable, and the transient tracking performance of the agents at risk of collision is strictly guaranteed, even if only output information is available, thus achieving collision-free coordination. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed scheme is verified through a simulation example.

title: Enabling Multicasting Supports For O-RAN Virtual Function Deployment in Multi-Cloud Environment
authors: Minh Hai Dao, Kim Khoa Nguyen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cloud Computing
abstract: The proliferation of multi-user applications in next-generation cellular networks sparks the need to simultaneously deploy multicast trees and provisioning services to provide efficient communication. Such a feature allows taking full advantage of the high flexibility of the Open Radio Access Networks (O-RAN) architecture and its open standards, virtualization, and multi-cloud deployment. Enabling multicasting supports in O-RAN, such as 3GPP's 5G Multicast Broadcast Service capabilities, will unlock and improve a multitude of new O-RAN use cases. Unfortunately, such integration has not yet been clearly defined in O-RAN specifications or fully investigated in prior studies. In this paper, we jointly formulate the Multicast Service Function Chain Embedding (MSE) and O-RAN functional split optimization problems in a multi-cloud environment as an Integer Linear Programming (ILP) model. We address both single and multiple flow request scenarios and propose a Pseudo Knapsack-based heuristic algorithm with polynomial-time complexity, combining feasible search optimization and dynamic programming techniques. Experimental results show that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, approximating the optimal solution while significantly reducing computational time.

title: Physics-Data Fusion with FBG Sensing for In-Situ Solid Lubricant Coating Degradation Monitoring under Microgravity Emulation
authors: Zhenhui Feng, Lei Qi, Xiaobo Rui, Lina Wang, Qi Yan, Junchi Zhang, Yu Zhang
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: The condition state of solid-lubricant coatings is critical for the tribological performance of space gear transmission systems. In microgravity-like environments, condition monitoring is challenged by limited dynamic sensing capability and noisy periodic responses, which complicate robust feature extraction. To address these challenges, this study develops an integrated physics–data fusion pipeline based on fiber Bragg grating (FBG) tooth-root strain sensing for dynamic degradation assessment of gear solid-lubricant coatings. An autocorrelation-driven adaptive window function (AWF) is combined with time-synchronous averaging (TSA) to adaptively determine the window shape and ensure coverage of a complete meshing period, enabling stable extraction of degradation-related features from periodic strain signals. An exponential degradation model is then combined with support vector regression (SVR) to account for nonlinear effects that are difficult to represent in the analytic formulation, and Lasso regression is employed for feature selection to retain interpretability and reduce redundancy. The baseline pipeline is validated on a microgravity emulation test platform, achieving a coefficient of determination of 0.992 and an RMSE of 0.1089 μm for coating-thickness prediction, and is further evaluated on additional same-specification gear pairs through endpoint validation using final residual coating thickness. The 95% confidence interval width remains within ±0.35 μm, indicating consistent predictive performance. The results show that the integrated approach can capture stage-dependent nonlinear coating degradation associated with elemental migration, supporting its feasibility for in-situ monitoring of same-specification MoS₂-coated gear pairs under the tested magnetic-bearing microgravity-like support conditions.

title: Breaking the Vicious Cycle: Coherent 3D Gaussian Splatting from Sparse and Motion-Blurred Views
authors: Chaoran Feng, Zhankuo Xu, Yingtao Li, Jianbin Zhao, Jiashu Yang, Wangbo Yu, Li Yuan, Yonghong Tian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a state-of-the-art method for novel view synthesis. However, its performance heavily relies on dense, high-quality input imagery, an assumption that is often violated in real-world applications, where data is typically sparse and motion-blurred. These two issues create a vicious cycle: sparse views ignore the multi-view constraints necessary to resolve motion blur, while motion blur erases high-frequency details crucial for aligning the limited views. Thus, reconstruction often fails catastrophically, with fragmented views and a low-frequency bias. To break this cycle, we introduce CoherentGS, a novel framework for high-fidelity 3D reconstruction from sparse and blurry images. Our key insight is to address these compound degradations using a dual-prior strategy. Specifically, we combine two pre-trained generative models: a specialized deblurring network for restoring sharp details and providing photometric guidance, and a diffusion model that offers geometric priors to fill in unobserved regions of the scene. This dual-prior strategy is supported by several key techniques, including a consistency-guided camera exploration module that adaptively guides the generative process, and a depth regularization loss that ensures geometric plausibility. We evaluate CoherentGS through both quantitative and qualitative experiments on synthetic and real-world scenes, using as few as 3, 6, and 9 input views. Our results demonstrate that CoherentGS significantly outperforms existing methods, setting a new state-of-the-art for this challenging task. The code and video demos are available at https://potatobigroom.github.io/CoherentGS/.

title: A Study of M-Ary Encoding Genetic Algorithm for Closely-Spaced Multipath Parameter Estimation
authors: Qing Zhang, Bingjie Xue, Yue Lyu, Wanbing Hao, Wei Wang
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: With the rapid development of wireless communication technologies, high-precision channel parameter estimation has become increasingly significant for applications such as positioning and sensing. To address the limitations of traditional binary encoding GA, including discontinuous Hamming distances and low coding efficiency, this letter proposes m-ary encoding genetic algorithm (MGA). Through comprehensive comparison of binary, quaternary, octal, and decimal encoding schemes, the simulation results demonstrate that quaternary encoding achieves an optimal balance between estimation accuracy and computational complexity. This letter reveals the impact of MGA on channel parameter estimation and the Quaternary GA outperforms alternative encoding schemes, demonstrating enhanced adaptability and improved accuracy in complex channel environments.

title: Flow-Aware Diffusion for Real-Time VR Restoration: Mitigating Cybersickness with Enhanced Spatiotemporal Coherence_supp1-3697013.pdf
authors: Yuyang Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: Cybersickness remains a critical barrier to the widespread adoption of Virtual Reality (VR), particularly in scenarios involving intense or artificial motion cues. Among the key contributors is excessive optical flow-perceived visual motion that, when unmatched by vestibular input, leads to sensory conflict and discomfort. While previous efforts have explored geometric or hardware-based mitigation strategies, such methods often rely on predefined scene structures, manual tuning, or intrusive equipment. In this work, we propose U-MAD, a lightweight, real-time, AI-based solution that suppresses perceptually disruptive optical flow directly at the image level. Unlike prior handcrafted approaches, this method learns to attenuate high-intensity motion patterns from rendered frames without requiring mesh-level editing or scene-specific adaptation. Designed as a plug-and-play module, U-MAD integrates seamlessly into existing VR pipelines and generalizes well to procedurally generated environments. The experiments show that U-MAD consistently reduces average optical flow and enhances temporal stability across diverse scenes. A user study further supports the finding that reducing visual motion defects can improve perceptual comfort and alleviate cybersickness symptoms. These findings demonstrate that perceptually guided modulation of optical flow provides an effective and scalable approach to creating more user-friendly immersive experiences. The code will be released at https://github.com/XXXXX (upon publication).

title: Collaborative Orthogonal Learning for Constrained Multi-Objective Optimization
authors: Yubo Wang, Chengyu Hu, Xinyi Wu, Tingyu Zhang, Wenyin Gong, Xuesong Yan, Liang Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Evolutionary Computation
abstract: Balancing feasibility, convergence, and diversity is a fundamental challenge in constrained multi-objective optimization, particularly in landscapes with irregular or disconnected boundaries. Existing methods primarily exchange solution positions but neglect valuable convergence direction information contained within the evolutionary process, which may lead to inefficient and redundant searches for the constrained Pareto front (CPF). To address this, we propose a dual-swarm competitive optimizer with collaborative orthogonal learning (COL) strategy, which effectively decouples global exploration and diversity exploitation. Specifically, the main swarm performs trend learning to identify convergence directions from boundary and winner-loser interaction information, enabling global exploration through infeasible regions toward the CPF. Guided by the learned trends, the auxiliary swarm executes orthogonal learning to search complementary subspaces, which broadens the solution distribution while avoiding redundant searches, ensuring diversity exploitation capability. Additionally, a niche-guided subset selection strategy is introduced to maintain uniform distribution within the objective space through a three-level subset division mechanism based on local niche capacity. Extensive experiments on standard and extended benchmark instances demonstrate the robustness and superiority of our approach over nine state-of-the-art methods.

title: Optimization Output Control of Distributed Lighting System Based on NSGA-II Method and MLP Network
authors: Chenshuo Chang, Haoting Liu, Zhipeng Ye, Wenbo Hou, Qing Li
publish: IEEE Systems Journal
abstract: The optimization of light source output control is a key challenge in intelligent lighting systems, involving the balance of light efficiency uniformity, shadow reduction, and glare management. This study proposes an innovative lighting output control method that integrates artificial neural networks (ANNs) with the nondominated sorting genetic algorithm II (NSGA-II) for multiobjective optimization. First, an ANN model is developed to evaluate lighting performance indicators, including light efficiency index, shadow ratio, and glare ratio, across various light source configurations. Second, NSGA-II is employed to achieve multiobjective optimization, effectively balancing conflicting lighting performance criteria. Third, the computational efficiency of NSGA-II is enhanced by incorporating ANN-based rapid predictions, reducing the need for frequent fitness evaluations. Experimental results on the Jetson Xavier NX platform show that the proposed method improves the light efficiency index by 10.6%, reduces the shadow ratio by 69.3%, and decreases the glare ratio by 52.9%, significantly enhancing lighting efficiency while suppressing shadows and glare.

title: A Robust CLEAN-like Approach to ISAR Image Decomposition with Non-stationary Signal Components
authors: Karol Abratkiewicz
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Inverse synthetic aperture radar (ISAR) has recently garnered significant attention in both civilian and military applications, with current research primarily focusing on target imaging, recognition, and classification. To support these tasks, ISAR images are often decomposed in order to isolate dominant scatterers, which are typically assumed to carry the most relevant information. The CLEAN algorithm is commonly used for this purpose; however, it inherently assumes stationary sinusoidal components and therefore neglects important non-stationary effects such as amplitude and phase modulation. This article proposes a robust information-preserving framework for ISAR image decomposition in the presence of non-stationary signals. The proposed approach, termed INformation-preserving Signal Processing for ISAR REconstruction (INSPIRE), enables the decomposition of two-dimensional non-stationary signals into individual signal components while preserving their full complex-valued representation. Unlike CLEAN, the proposed method retains both amplitude and phase variations of the extracted components, even when they deviate from pure sinusoidal behavior. Furthermore, in contrast to sparse reconstruction techniques such as Orthogonal Matching Pursuit (OMP), INSPIRE does not rely on a sparsity assumption, which often constitutes a limiting factor in practical radar scenarios. The effectiveness of INSPIRE was validated using both simulated radar data and experimental ISAR measurements. Comparative results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves more accurate signal decomposition than both CLEAN and OMP, requiring significantly fewer iterations while providing superior image quality. In particular, the method confirms that effective ISAR image filtering and reconstruction can be achieved by selectively extracting physically meaningful signal components, resulting in noise suppression and enhanced target representation. Quantitative evaluation using standard metrics such as entropy, contrast, structural similarity index measure, and peak signal-to-noise ratio further confirms the advantages of the proposed approach.

title: Adaptive Resource Allocation and Semantic Priority Control for Multi-UAV-Aided Relaying Systems
authors: Le-Tuan Nguyen, Nguyen Cong Luong, Thai-Hoc Vu, Nguyen Tien Hoa, Xingwang Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: In this paper, we investigate a novel Semantic Communication (SemCom)-enabled multi-Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV)-aided relaying system operating within a multi-time slot framework, each comprising two distinct phases. In the initial phase, the UAVs perform user association and receive data from their associated users while capturing images and extracting semantic triplets from the images. In the subsequent phase, the UAVs forward both the accumulated user data and extracted triplets to a remote base station (BS). While user association can be effectively managed using the Signal-to-Interference-plus Noise Ratio (SINR) metric, data transmission in the second phase encounters significant challenges due to the limited number of available channels, as well as the heterogeneous nature and varying priorities of user data and semantic triplets. To address this, we formulate an optimization problem aimed at jointly optimizing channel association, transmit power, and the priority level thresholds for triplet selection for the UAVs. The problem is inherently non-convex with time-varying parameters including the user data, contextual information, scene graphs, fading channels and distances between the users and UAVs. To tackle this, we propose a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) algorithm with low computation complexity. This approach models the system as a decentralized partially-observable Markov game (Dec-POMDP), where UAVs learn optimal policies, guided by reward functions that prioritize high-QoS relay data. Simulations using the Open Images V6 dataset show our MARL algorithm improves relay data transmission by up to 18% and enables more triplet transmissions by reducing average priority thresholds by up to 50% (vs. single-agent learning). Notably, a system-centric mode with enhanced UAV coordination significantly outperforms a user-centric approach.

title: A 120° FOV Angular Diversity Receiver for Underwater Wireless Optical Communications
authors: Haipeng Wang, Qingrui Chen, Shaoda Yuan, Baokang Chen, Zejun Zhang, Jing Xu
publish: IEEE Photonics Technology Letters
abstract: Wide field-of-view (FOV) receivers are essential for relaxing alignment requirements in underwater wireless optical communication (UWOC). This letter proposes a novel angular diversity receiver (ADR) comprising five lens-detector modules (L-DMs). By optimizing the inclination angle of the four side modules to 50°, a uniform composite FOV exceeding 120° is achieved across the full 360° azimuthal range. Experimental validation in a 7-m water tank confirms that the fabricated ADR prototype can support a data rate of 200 Mbps with a bit error rate (BER) below the forward error correction (FEC) limit. Furthermore, angular measurements verify that the wide coverage of ±60° in zenith angle is maintained regardless of the azimuthal orientation. These results demonstrate the great potential of the proposed ADR for reliable UWOC links without strict alignment.

title: MEETINGS AND SYMPOSIA
authors: Raymond P. Wasky
publish: IEEE Antennas and Propagation Magazine

title: Cognera: An Intelligent Tool For Dyslexia Detection And Correction
authors: Sadhana B, Dhananjaya B, Sahana B, Chrisel Eithal Gudinho, Prajna Prajna, Sahana Nagappa Devadiga, Shravya DS
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Dyslexia is a learning problem that affects a person’s ability to read, write, and spell and sometimes to speak. It is not related to vision problems or lack of motivation but it is based on how the brain processes structure and function. This problem can be lifelong, but with early diagnosis it can develop strategies to improve reading and writing skills. Cognera addresses the specific needs of individuals with dyslexia, providing them with a valuable tool to improve their reading and writing skills. It also aims to bridge the gap between technology and education. It is a software-driven project with potential applications in school, personal learning tools and many other. It has a user-friendly interface and an intelligent backend, it is a step toward adaptive learning for individuals with dyslexia. The main purpose to overcome dyslexia is by text correction and error detection. We address the challenges faced by individuals with dyslexia by proposing Cognera. The system reads handwritten content, which performs optical character recognition using a transformer based OCR model, and refines the output through correct text detection. On a set of handwritten samples including dyslexic and normal writing, the method produces highly readable corrected text, with qualitative improvements clearly observed across all test cases in initial testing, the corrected sentences successfully resolved 85–90% of common spelling errors and produced coherent, fluent sentences in most samples. This enables students, teachers, and parents to understand written content more easily, reduces frustration, and provides a practical tool for improving communication and learning for individuals with dyslexia. By bridging advanced text recognition and correction with a user-friendly interface, Cognera offers a meaningful step toward adaptive learning, making handwritten content more accessible for people of all ages.

title: Macro-to-TWh: A Log-Stabilized Benchmark of 21 Supervised Regressors for Country-Level Primary Energy Consumption Prediction
authors: Jim Howlader, Abir Bin Faruque, Maidul Islam, Hussain Md. Shahariyar, Shauaib Siddique Shawon, Afsana Afraine Tithi, Ahmed Faizul Haque Dhrubo, Souvik Pramanik, Mohammad Abdul Qayum
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Accurate estimation of national energy demand is essential for long-term planning, policy design, and sustainable development. This paper presents a comparative study of supervised regression techniques for predicting country-wise primary energy consumption using macro-level indicators from a largescale World Energy Consumption dataset (country-year records). After data cleaning and organization by country and year, missing values were handled through interpolation, and a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\log 1 \mathrm{p}$</tex> transformation was applied to reduce skewness and stabilize variance. Multiple regression models, including linear baselines and ensemble-based learners, were evaluated under a consistent train-test protocol using standard error metrics <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(R^{2}$</tex>, RMSE, MAE, and MAPE). Experimental results show that ensemble methods substantially outperform simpler regressors, confirming the non-linear relationship between the predictors and energy consumption. Among all evaluated models, Gradient Boosting achieved the best overall performance, delivering the highest coefficient of determination and the lowest prediction errors, while other ensemble strategies (e.g., voting and stacking) also produced competitive results. The findings suggest that boostingbased regressors offer a robust and practical approach for macrolevel energy consumption estimation, and they provide a strong baseline for future work involving time-aware validation and richer explanatory features.

title: Hybrid Deep Learning Models for Detecting Anomalous Behavior in Peer-to-Peer Payment Systems
authors: Muthu Selvam
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: The rising use of real-time peer-to-peer (P2P) payment technologies like RTP, Zelle, and ACH has increased banking industry fraud efforts that are nuanced, fast-evolving, and hard to intercept using standard rule-based systems. This research introduces a hybrid deep learning system that detects P2P payment transaction abnormalities using CNNs and LSTM networks. The model detects fraud by learning spatial patterns from transaction data and temporal relationships in sequential behaviour. The system outperformed traditional methods by 8.6 % in detection accuracy and 23 % in inference time on realworld financial datasets with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 4. 2 \%}$</tex> precision and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 2. 8 \%}$</tex> recall. Experimental results show statistically significant increases in detection and inference efficiency over competing baselines. Results suggest real-time financial systems are feasible, but large-scale production validation is needed. The framework supports scalable adaptive fraud detection in current digital payment infrastructures.

title: CTGAN-Augmented Stacked Ensemble of Machine and Deep Learning Models for Diabetes Prediction
authors: Shirin Sasna, Muhammad Atlas Malik, Vinod Kumar, Muhammad Atif Saeed, Azka Atiq, Akhtar Jamil
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Early prediction of type 2 diabetes enables timely intervention and may substantially reduce long-term complications and healthcare costs. This paper proposes a CTGAN-augmented stacked ensemble framework that integrates both ML and DL models for binary diabetes prediction on the widely used PIMA Indian Diabetes Dataset. The original dataset (768 records, eight numerical features) is cleaned using interquartile range (IQR) based outlier clipping and scaled with Min-Max normalization. Class imbalance is addressed by generating realistic minority class samples for only the training split using Conditional Tabular GAN (CTGAN). Three ML models (XGBoost, Random Forest, Decision Tree) and three DL architectures (Deep Neural Network, Deep & Cross Network, and TabNet) are tuned with Optuna for optimal hyperparameters. Their probabilistic outputs feed a two-level stacking scheme with Random Forest as level1 meta learner and XGBoost as level-2 meta-learner. The introduction of stacked ensemble learning substantially enhanced overall performance, with the two-stage stacking architecture consistently outperforming all individual models. On the held-out test set, the ML-based level-2 stacker achieved highest accuracy (0.931) and AUC-ROC (0.935), outperforming DL ensemble (Accuracy: 0.914, AUC-ROC: 0.927). Results confirm that well-tuned treebased ensembles and hierarchical stacking offer a robust and generalizable strategy for small, structured clinical datasets.

title: Model Predictive Control: A Revisit [Histories of Control]
authors: Raman Mehra
publish: IEEE Control Systems

title: Zero-Trust Agentic Federated Learning for Secure Internet of Things (IoT) Defense Systems
authors: Samaresh Kumar Singh, Joyjit Roy, Martin So
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: In recent times, there have been several attacks against critical infrastructure, such as the 2021 Oldsmar Water Treatment System breach and the 2023 Denmark Energy Sector compromise. These breaches clearly show the need for security improvements within the deployment of Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT). Federated Learning (FL) provides a path to conduct privacy preserving collaborative intrusion detection; however, all current FL frameworks are vulnerable to Byzantine poisoning attacks and do not include a method for authenticating agents. In this paper we propose Zero-Trust Agentic Federated Learning (ZTA-FL), a defense-in-depth framework using Trusted Platform Module (TPM)-based cryptographic attestation, which has an extremely low (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<\mathbf{1 0}^{-7}$</tex>) false acceptance rate and a new SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP)-weighted aggregation algorithm with explainable Byzantine detection under non-Independent and Identically Distributed (IID) conditions with theoretical guarantees, and uses privacy-preserving ondevice adversarial training. Experiments were conducted on three different Intrusion Detection System (IDS) benchmarks (EdgeIIoT set, CIC-IDS2017, UNSW-NB15) using standard evaluation metrics and compared against baseline methods to calculate the performance of ZTA-FL. The results indicate that ZTAFL achieved a 97.8 % detection rate, a 93.2 % detection rate when subjected to 30 % Byzantine attacks (an improvement over FLAME of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3. 1 \%, ~} \mathbf{p}<\mathbf{0. 0 1}$</tex>) and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 9. 3 \%}$</tex> adversarial robustness, while reducing the communication overhead by 34 %. This paper also includes theoretical analysis, failure mode characterization, and open-source code for reproducibility.

title: Handloom Saree Classification Using Transfer Learning with MobileNetV2
authors: Sandhya D Kotian, Raksha Puthran, Anusha Prashanth Shetty, Shabarish Chandu Bangera
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Handloom sarees represents rich cultural diversity and tradition and are currently manually classified that is time-consuming and susceptible to errors due to the presence of subtle visual differences and class-imbalances. In the present work, we propose a simple deep learning architecture based on MobileNetV2 for automatic classification of three dominant handloom sarees: Bandhani, Banarasi and Ikat. Transfer learning was employed using ImageNet pretrained weights, and we fine-tuned the model to 2,130 images that were curated timely. For addressing the class-imbalance problem, weighted cross-entropy loss was adopted and this led to strong generalization among all of the classes. The model obtained a test accuracy of 93.90% and precision and recall greater than 0.87. for each category. Evaluation metrics such as confusion matrix and classification report confirm the model’s reliability and cultural awareness. This paper demonstrate a possibility to use effective CNN architectures to textile classification and provides a basis for implementing real-time application in e-commerce, heritage recording and fashion analytics. Index Terms— Handloom Classification, MobileNetV2, Transfer Learning, Deep Learning, Textile Recognition, CNN

title: Enhancing the Reliability of Sunspot Number Prediction Intervals Using MC Dropout and Isotropic Regression

title: A Worm-like Robot for Ferromagnetic Surface Inspection_supp7-3683248.mp4
authors: Rezvan Nasiri
publish: N/A
abstract: Industrial inspection is a crucial task for ensuring the longterm maintenance of infrastructures with ferromagnetic surfaces, such as power plants, wind turbines, and chemical facilities. These environments are often unsafe for human access, motivating the use of climbing robots to perform inspection tasks reliably and repeatedly; nevertheless, the existing robots often have limited maneuverability, reduced stability during transitions, and high energy consumption. In this article, we present a novel robotic system inspired by the body morphology of caterpillars. The design consists of three modules connected via a spring joint mechanism and equipped with a passive magnetic adhesion system. The design’s maneuverability, stability, energy efficiency, and falling robustness are studied analytically and experimentally. Experimental results confirmed the robot’s ability to perform internal/external corner transitions, thin surface transitions, pipe circling, and surface turning in real-world environments, including rusty, coated, and uneven surfaces. Furthermore, we found that compared with simple locomotion, the designed wormlike locomotion yielded an average 10.2% increase in forward velocity and 6.7% reduction in the cost of transportation (COT). These findings suggest that the proposed design offers a practical and versatile approach for inspecting ferromagnetic surfaces.

title: Focused ultrasound improves microcirculation to treat painful peripheral neuropathy in type 2 diabetic rats
authors: Jiun-Yi Chiou, Kang-Chih Fan, Hsin-Yun Chang, Gin-Shin Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Ultrasonics
abstract: Focused ultrasound (FUS) has emerged as a noninvasive modality to improve peripheral circulation and nerve function. However, its therapeutic effects on diabetic peripheral neuropathy (DPN) in type 2 diabetes (T2D) remain unclear. Here, we evaluated the efficacy of FUS in a T2D rat model induced by a high-fat diet and low-dose streptozotocin. FUS was applied to the plantar artery. Real-time sonographic imaging and laser Doppler flowmetry assessed arterial flow and toe skin perfusion, respectively. Behavioral pain tests of mechanical allodynia and thermal hyperalgesia, along with histological analysis of intraepidermal microvessels and nerve fibers, were performed. A single FUS session significantly increased arterial flow and toe skin perfusion by approximately 2-fold, with effects lasting up to 3 minutes. Two-week FUS treatments significantly enhanced skin perfusion (n = 8, p < 0.05), capillary density (n = 5, p < 0.05) and nerve fiber density (n = 5, p < 0.05), while reducing pain behaviors (n = 8, p < 0.05). Compared with the previous type 1 DPN study, type 2 DPN rats exhibited greater perfusion recovery (+109% vs +51%) and higher peripheral nerve fiber density (+81% vs +58%). These differences likely reflect distinct neuropathic mechanisms, with T1D primarily involving axonal degeneration and T2D characterized by microvascular dysfunction and metabolic impairment. Thermal measurements confirmed FUS localized heating remained within safe thresholds, and no tissue damage was observed. These findings suggest that FUS elicits distinct, beneficial vascular responses between T1D and T2D models, supporting its potential as a targeted therapy for painful DPN.

title: A Tightly Coupled EKF for Hardware-Trigger-Free Radar Inertial Odometry based on Dynamic State Retrodiction
authors: Jun Wang, Qiangwen Fu, Qi Zhou, Lihang Cui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: In millimeter-wave Radar-Inertial Odometry (RIO), temporal desynchronization between radar point clouds and Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) data is a prevalent issue, primarily stemming from internal signal processing latency and the absence of external hardware triggering mechanisms. This temporal misalignment causes a severe discrepancy between observation data and state propagation in the time domain, inducing non-linear distortions in dynamic constraints and significantly degrading system robustness and localization accuracy. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a hardware-trigger-free tightly-coupled RIO framework based on Dynamics-Based Kinematic Retrodiction and Cascaded Dynamic-Geometric Filtering. First, a dynamic retrodiction mechanism is introduced to retrodict critical kinematic states (velocity and attitude)—essential for Doppler modeling—from the data arrival timestamp to the effective physical sampling timestamp, thereby achieving precise temporal alignment between system states and measurements. Building upon this, a cascaded filtering strategy is constructed, which initially employs a Global Doppler Dynamic Gate for preliminary filtering based on motion consistency, followed by RANSAC Geometric Verification to eliminate outliers induced by dynamic objects and multipath interference. Experimental results on multiple public datasets show that the proposed method achieves localization accuracy surpassing hardware-synchronized baselines without requiring hardware synchronization, while significantly enhancing system robustness and trajectory consistency in real time.

title: Performance Analysis of Full-Duplex Cooperative NOMA-Enabled ISAC in Heterogeneous Networks
authors: Vimal Bhatia, Shravan Kumar Namdeo, Ambuj Kumar Mishra, Amit Baghel, Ondrej Krejcar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: This paper investigates the performance of an in tegrated sensing and communication (ISAC)-enabled heterogeneous network (HetNet) using cooperative non-orthogonal multiple access (C-NOMA). To address overlap of frequency bands caused by dense device deployment and to improve data rates, spectrum utilization, and hardware efficiency, a novel ISAC-enabled HetNet architecture featuring both the macro base stations (MBSs) and the pico-base stations (PBSs) is introduced. The PBS performs both sensing and cooperative communication, acting as a full-duplex (FD) decode-and-forward relay to assist MBS-to-user transmission. NOMA is applied in both MBS-to-user and PBS-to-user links to enhance spectral efficiency. The target nodes are modeled using a Poisson point process, with a nearest-neighbor connection policy to improve the sensing accuracy compared to ISAC-OMA. Performance evaluation and comparison are performed using outage probability, system throughput, and probability of detection. The results show that, the proposed C-NOMA achieves upto 58% higher system throughput than OMA and 32% higher than conventional NOMA.
