title: Lay All Your Morph on UV: 3D Geometry-Aware Canonical UV Representations for Morphing Attack Detection
authors: Simone Maurizio La Cava, Andrea Panzino, Gian Luca Marcialis
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science
abstract: Morphing attacks remain a critical threat to face recognition systems, particularly in document-style acquisition settings where such manipulations have the most significant operational impact. Most existing detection methods primarily focus on texture-level or pixel-wise inconsistencies, often neglecting geometric cues that more directly reflect morph-induced deformations. This work investigates the use of UV texture maps derived from 3D facial reconstruction as a canonical, geometry-aware representation for morphing attack detection. We hypothesize that UV parametrization can enhance morph detectability by exposing distortions on the reconstructed facial surface and by amplifying mapping inconsistencies propagated through the reconstruction process, including those indirectly influenced by artifacts outside the facial region. Extensive experiments conducted on document-like datasets and across multiple deep learning architectures show that integrating UV-based representations with original images consistently improves the separability between bona fide and morphed samples. Evaluations under challenging cross-dataset and cross-manipulation conditions further confirm the robustness of the proposed approach, which outperforms models trained solely on raw images and achieves superior performance compared to representative state-of-the-art baselines. Overall, these findings indicate that UV texture maps can act as a geometry-induced canonicalization layer for morphing attack detection, largely independent of the underlying classifier or morphing strategy, and represent a promising direction for enhancing the reliability of MAD systems in document-oriented applications.

title: FedGB: A Generator-Based Federated Learning Against Reconstruction Attacks
authors: Soha Sandouka, Muhamad Felemban
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: Federated learning (FL) is a decentralized machine learning paradigm in which multiple clients train models locally without sharing private data. Although FL is designed to preserve privacy, recent studies have shown that shared model updates remain vulnerable to powerful inference attacks, where adversaries intercept gradients or parameters to recover sensitive information. A prominent example is the reconstruction attack. In this paper, we propose FedGB, a generator-based FL framework that leverages Conditional Generative Adversarial Networks (CGANs) to reduce the exposure of sensitive features. Unlike classical FL and prior GAN-based approaches, FedGB shares only the generator with the server, thereby avoiding leakage through classifiers or feature extractors. FedGB improves privacy by minimizing information exchanged with the server while maintaining competitive accuracy. Extensive experiments on FMNIST, CIFAR-10, and Digit5 demonstrate that FedGB achieves competitive accuracy-privacy trade-offs. In particular, FedGB reduces the image reconstruction quality, measured by PSNR, from 19.79 to 9.31 on CIFAR-10 and from 20.17 to 6.67 on Digit5.

title: LLM-guided Adaptive Compensator: Bringing Adaptivity to Robotic Feedback Control with Large Language Model
authors: Zhongchao Zhou, Yuxi Lu, Yaonan Zhu, Yifei Zhao, Qian Niu, Bin He, Liang He, Wenwei Yu, Yusuke Iwasawa
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Recent advances in code generation and reasoning have enabled the growing use of large language models (LLMs) in robotics. The design of traditional robotic feedback controllers often requires extensive expert knowledge and iterative computational effort, motivating the exploration of LLM-assisted controller development. However, existing efforts are largely restricted to overly simplified systems, provide limited comparison with human-designed controllers, and lack validation on real-world robotic platforms. To address these gaps, this work investigates the use of LLMs in robotic feedback control by focusing on adaptive control and proposing an LLM-guided adaptive compensator. Instead of generating a complete controller from scratch, the LLM is guided to design a compensator that modulates the response of an unknown system to match that of a predefined reference system, thereby achieving adaptivity. The proposed approach is evaluated using five adaptive controllers on a two-degree-of-freedom (DoF) soft robot and a one-DoF shoulder joint of a humanoid robot. Both simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that the LLM-guided adaptive compensator achieves performance comparable to four conventional adaptive controllers. Lyapunov-based analysis further establishes the stability and generalization capability of the proposed compensator, while analysis of the reasoning process suggests a more structured design approach. This study introduces a novel framework for integrating LLMs into robotic feedback control, with promising practical applicability.

title: Distribution Network Modeling, Parameters, and Metering Data: Test Systems
authors: Konrad Schmitt, Larissa Souto, Meisam Mahdavi, Manohar Chamana, Stephen Bayne, Luciane Canha, Francisco Jurado
publish: IEEE Canadian Journal of Electrical and Computer Engineering
abstract: After initial development and testing with synthetic data, distribution systems solutions are required to be verified under diverse network topologies and consumption patterns to confirm their deployment feasibility. Even though the literature has presented data for several test distribution networks over the years and smart metering datasets have been made available, finding them according to the research requirements is time-consuming. Especially in reconfiguration and restoration studies, test networks must have a networked topology, which makes the search for models even more limited. Still, the available network models and datasets tend to be oversimplified or omit crucial data and users are often left with the need to make assumptions. This article compiles and presents eight different reconfigurable distribution networks ranging from 12 to 328 nodes and nine smart metering datasets for residential. Along with the compiled data, this article discusses the available and missing information of each model and dataset, while potential assumptions and their impacts are discussed. With that, the main intention of this article is to provide a complete and centralized resource to support the development of various active distribution networks’ studies.

title: Homography-Based Visual Servoing of a UAM for Payload Delivery Onto a Dynamic Target
authors: Yanjie Chen, Hongyuan Cai, Yifan Lin, Yuhao Liu, Huimin Lu, Hui Zhang, Yaonan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: This article proposes a dynamic homography-based visual servoing (HBVS) control strategy for payload delivery by an uncrewed aerial manipulator (UAM). Unlike traditional approaches that assume planar-target motion, this strategy constructs a rotation-robust dynamic homography matrix to decouple image dynamics from the adverse effects of target rotation. Ahigh-order sliding mode observer (HOSMO) based on decoupled error signals is developed to address unmeasurable relative velocities, lumped disturbances, and the susceptibility of existing observers to target rotation. The proposed HOSMO isolates the estimation process from target rotation, thereby considerably improving estimation accuracy. This observer enables the UAM to achieve stable tracking within the HBVS framework without requiring direct velocity measurements or prior knowledge of external disturbances. Moreover, a virtual camera-based dual-loop HBVS architecture is proposed to dynamically coordinate the UAM and the onboard manipulator, overcoming the restricted fields of view (FOVs) and heterogeneous visual features that limit existing manipulator control strategies. Isomorphic visual features are unified to dynamically correct UAM deviations, improving payload delivery accuracy. The stability of the proposed control strategy is rigorously analyzed using Lyapunov theory. Finally, comparative simulations and outdoor experiments are conducted to validate the effectiveness of the proposed strategy.

title: Field Evaluation of a MEMS-Based Silicon Resonant Pressure Sensor for Seafloor Pressure Observation
authors: Masanao Shinohara, Takashi Kunugi, Shin Aoi, Shigeto Iwai, Takashi Yoshida, Toshihiko Oomachi, Ryuichiro Noda, Tetsuya Takeda, Masashi Mochizuki, Kenji Uehira, Tomoaki Yamada
publish: IEEE Journal of Oceanic Engineering
abstract: Seafloor pressure observations mainly use precise quartz resonant sensors at present due to their high sensitivity. Recently, a new silicon resonant sensor using microelectromechanical systems (MEMS) technology has emerged with low-power consumption, compact size, constant quality, and high sensitivity. High stability is expected due to the small size of a silicon resonant pressure sensor, and interunit variability among sensors is believed to be small because of the MEMS technology. The influence of the movement of the sensor itself is small due to the mechanism of pressure transmission. Nankai Trough seafloor observation network for earthquakes and tsunamis, named N-net, is equipped with the new sensors. Before routine observations using this new sensor, we evaluate the performance of the new silicon resonant sensor for seafloor pressure observation. An existing free-fall pop-up type ocean bottom pressure gauge has a quartz crystal resonant sensor. We replaced the sensor with the new silicon resonant pressure sensor and performed seafloor observations off the Boso peninsula near Tokyo. We obtained pressure data from two new sensors. Noise spectra of the new sensor were compared to those of the ordinary crystal sensor, which was installed at the same position. The new and ordinary gauges have identical noise levels at periods longer than about 50 s. Although the new silicon gauges seem to have slightly higher noise levels at periods between 50 and 10 s, the new pressure gauge can detect pressure changes larger than 1 hPa throughout the whole observation band. The new pressure gauge observed sea level fluctuation by volcanic eruption in Tonga and the identical waveforms were obtained from the new and ordinary pressure gauges.

title: Towards Secure and Reliable SAGIN: Learning-Driven Multi-Dimensional Resource Scheduling for Multi-RIS Assisted OTFS Transmission
authors: Zimo Feng, Zhi Lin, Hongjun Wang, Ruiqian Ma, Kang An, Yuanzhi He
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications
abstract: As a key component of the space-air-ground integrated network (SAGIN), low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites aim to provide global coverage and reliable services under high-speed mobile conditions, which are critically challenged by severe Doppler effect and inherent broadcast security threats. To address these issues, this paper investigates a multi-reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-assisted orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) downlink transmission system, where a LEO satellite serves multiple information receivers and potential eavesdroppers acting as energy receivers via simultaneous wireless information and power transfer (SWIPT). By jointly optimizing multi-dimensional resource variables, such as transmit beamforming and RIS reflection coefficients of the spatial domain, and the symbol scheduling matrix of the time-frequency domain, this paper aims to maximize the sum secrecy rate while satisfying constraints on satellite transmit power, the legitimate users’ quality of service, and energy-harvesting requirements. Given the high-dimensional, non-convex, and NP-hard nature of this problem, we develop an enhanced actor-critic deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework. The core innovation lies in designing an episodic return-prioritized experience selection mechanism with online mixing, which significantly improves the sampling efficiency and policy stability by intelligently selecting training data. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms existing schemes in achieving a higher sum secrecy rate, providing a practical and highly efficient resource scheduling solution for building secure and reliable next-generation SAGIN.

title: Uncertainty Quantification: Theory, Implementation, and Applications (Second Edition) [Book Review]
authors: Ming Jin
publish: IEEE Control Systems
abstract: Presents reviews for the following list of books, UNCERTAINTY QUANTIFICATION: THEORY, IMPLEMENTATION, AND APPLICATIONS (SECOND EDITION).

title: Graph Neural Networks with Augmented Lagrangian Loss for Energy Efficiency Optimization in Spectrum-Sharing Wireless Networks
authors: Xiaosen Shi, Liang Han
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: Graph neural networks (GNNs) have recently emerged as a promising approach for wireless communication problems due to their strong scalability and generalization capabilities. This letter presents a GNN-based method for optimizing energy efficiency (EE) in spectrum-sharing wireless networks. A key challenge in such problems is handling constraints, particularly the minimum rate requirements. We address this by formulating the GNN’s loss function using the augmented Lagrangian method (ALM), which effectively incorporates these constraints into the training framework. Simulation results show that the proposed approach achieves performance comparable to classical optimization algorithms and outperforms existing GNN constraint-handling methods in both optimality and feasibility.

title: Improving the Offline Dataset on Offline Reinforcement Learning
authors: Huihui Zhang, Guoyin Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Traditional online reinforcement learning (RL) systems operate by actively engaging with their environments to acquire data, with the goal of formulating an optimal policy that maximizes a predefined cumulative reward. However, in scenarios where cost and safety are paramount, the practicality of online RL is constrained. In response, offline RL emerges as a viable solution, leveraging previously amassed datasets to craft an effective policy without the need for ongoing interaction with the environment. An obstacle in offline RL lies in its tendency to overestimate the values of actions not adequately represented in the data, known as out-of-distribution (OOD) actions. While previous approaches have typically sought to enhance performance through increased algorithmic complexity, this article introduces a novel methodology that significantly improves the offline performance, with only minor additional memory cost. This study delves into the analysis of retaining high performance throughout the fully offline training. Since offline learning is unable to correct errors without interaction with the environment, it is highly dependent on the dataset. For example, a good policy can never be trained on a random dataset. On the other hand, even if an algorithm can give good performance temporarily, it will easily lead to OOD errors. Instead of resorting to complicated policy regularization, propose a simple center replacement approach that adjusts the offline dataset to suit the proposed algorithm, so that the OOD errors can be avoided, as well as improving the training performance. Our method introduces an adaptive regularization target that evolves with policy improvement, effectively relaxing the conservatism constraint over time without requiring online interaction.

title: Spatially Robust Near-Field SWIPT Using Pinching Antennas: Rate-Energy Tradeoff Bounds
authors: Zoran Hadzi-Velkov, Marija Poposka, Slavche Pejoski, Arumugam Nallanathan
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: Pinching Waveguide Antennas (PWAs) offer significant potential for simultaneous wireless information and power transfer (SWIPT) by enabling precise near-field energy focusing. However, existing optimization frameworks are largely point-based (targeting a single coordinate for maximum gain), and thus highly sensitive to positioning errors and mobility, as near-field signals fluctuate significantly even over small spatial displacements. In this paper, we propose a spatially robust design framework based on discrete antenna selection optimized for service area (SA) coverage. Unlike point-based approaches, our model guarantees quality of service within predefined SAs for both information decoding (ID) and energy harvesting (EH) receivers, thereby improving robustness to user displacements. We formulate the problem as a non-convex binary quadratic program aimed at maximizing harvested energy within the EH SA subject to robust rate constraints in the ID SA. To characterize fundamental performance limits, we develop a semidefinite relaxation (SDR) framework that provides an upper bound on the achievable rate-energy (R-E) region. For the lower bound, we employ a low-complexity swap-based local search algorithm enforcing binary hardware constraints. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed coverage-oriented design yields a robust R-E tradeoff and maintains stable performance across service regions, highlighting the advantages of discrete antenna activation over point-based near-field optimization approaches.

title: GaN Schottky Barrier Diodes with a Quasi-Depleted Layer for Ultra-Low Power Microwave Rectification
authors: Qiu-Xuan Li, Yang Li, Zheng-Ning Gu, Xu Zhang, Tao Liu, Ren-Pin Huang, Zhang-Cheng Liu, Xiao Wang, Zhi-Wei Chen, Feng-Qiu Jiang, Jin-Ping Ao
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: In this letter, a novel Gallium Nitride (GaN) Schottky barrier diode (SBD) featuring a 50-nm-thick quasi-depleted layer (QL) is proposed to mitigate the trade-off between turn-on voltage (Von) and reverse leakage (Ir). By effectively extending the zero-bias depletion region into the underlying drift layer to suppress hopping leakage, the fabricated QL-SBD simultaneously achieves an ultra-low Von of 0.2 V at 1 mA (Ion) and maintains a low Ir over the intended operating voltage range. Furthermore, the minimized zero-bias junction capacitance of 40 fF yields a cutoff frequency (fT) of 317.8 GHz, which translates into a high fT×log10(Ion/Ir)/ Von figure-of-merit for low power microwave rectification of 3320 GHz/V. A 2.6 GHz microwave rectifier is demonstrated using the QL-SBD, achieving power conversion efficiencies of 29.04% at -20 dBm, 20.82% at -25 dBm, and 14.86% at -30 dBm, the highest reported to date for GaN SBD-based rectifiers. The results underscore the immense potential of QL-SBDs for self-powered IoT sensor nodes and next-generation ambient energy harvesting applications.

title: MuCamp2: Generating Validated Cyber Campaign Variants via Constrained LLMs for Group Attribution
authors: Keunho Kim, Jimin Lee, Insup Lee, Insung Han, Jungsik Lee, Shinwoo Shim, Changhee Choi
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: As cyberattack operators have progressed to advanced persistent threat (APT) groups, accurate group attribution based on tactic, technique, and procedure (TTP) sequences has gained significant attention. However, publicly available campaign-level TTP sequences remain extremely limited, which restricts both statistical learning and generalization for cyber threat intelligence. Existing augmentation methods, such as synonym replacement (SR), often produce structurally invalid variants because they do not consider cross-technique capability chains. Although large language models (LLMs) offer a promising alternative for generating novel sequences, they alone fail to guarantee sequence integrity. Therefore, the primary limitation of current approaches is not the inability to generate diverse variants, but the tendency to produce a large volume of unusable data. To this end, we propose MuCamp2, a generation-validation architecture for campaign-level TTP augmentation that places LLMs outside the trust boundary. MuCamp2 employs deterministic candidate-pool constraints and a multi-level semantic validation based on the adversary emulation planner (AEP) to improve structural soundness and operational consistency under the available AEP mappings. Experimental results on the Lazarus and MenuPass datasets demonstrate that MuCamp2 completely eliminates tactic violations (0% compared to 26% to 32% for SR) and improves the strict AEP-based validation (L3) pass rate by 5.5 percentage points for Lazarus (10.2% to 15.7%) and 3.0 percentage points for MenuPass (7.5% to 10.5%). Furthermore, downstream attribution achieves a Macro-F1 of 0.987 to 0.990, indicating bounded but consistent usable-yield gains rather than large per-sample quality differences. We further note that L3 should be interpreted as an AEP-based operational proxy: AEP currently covers 197 of 356 ATT&CK v18 techniques (55.3%), and the high L3 pass rate observed for low-coverage sequences reflects reduced validator discrimination rather than higher intrinsic validity.

title: CondVS4: A Causal Generative Model for Online Imputation of Industrial Process Time-Series Data
authors: Shuo Li, Youqiang Hu, Yi Chai, Haoran Lu, Ke Zhang, Hongpeng Yin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: In contemporary industrial settings, the loss of collected data is a common issue, frequently caused by sensor degradation, adverse working conditions, electrical interference during motor startup, and network malfunctions. Consequently, the robust imputation of missing time-series data is a crucial component of any data-driven application. However, a significant gap exists as many state-of-the-art imputation methods are noncausal, leveraging future information to interpolate missing values. This characteristic renders them unsuitable for real-time industrial online monitoring, which demands strict adherence to temporal causality. To address this challenge, this article introduces CondVS4, a novel causal generative model specifically designed for online imputation. CondVS4 guarantees causality by performing posterior inference for missing variables conditioned solely on historical and current observations. Through extensive experiments on three real-world industrial datasets under various missingness patterns, CondVS4 demonstrates superior performance compared to a wide range of baseline methods. Furthermore, we show that our model substantially enhances the reliability of a downstream soft-sensing task, proving its effectiveness and practical value for online industrial applications.

title: XAI-Driven Input Masking to Mitigating Model Inversion Attacks
authors: Samin Dehbashi Sani, Amir Faiyaz, Tara Salman
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Federated learning (FL) enhances data privacy by sharing model updates instead of raw data; however, model inversion attacks can still reconstruct private training samples from these updates. Existing defenses apply differential privacy (DP) at the gradient level by uniformly adding noise, often resulting in significant utility loss. In this work, we propose an input-level defense guided by explainable artificial intelligence (XAI). Using integrated gradients optimized saliency (IGOS), we identify important pixels in facial images and selectively inject DP noise into these regions prior to local training. This technique prevents sensitive information from propagating into gradients. Experiments on the labeled faces in the wild (LFW) dataset demonstrate that the proposed approach substantially degrades reconstruction quality under inverting gradients attack, while preserving overall classification performance.

title: Student Feedback Sentiment Analysis on Textual Data: Comparing Machine Learning and Transfer Learning Approaches in Face-to-Face Courses
authors: Ashwin Shenoy M, Neves Binza Tunga
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Student feedback is an essential indicator for assessing teaching quality and improving the overall learning experience. However, the subjective and unstructured nature of textual feedback makes large-scale analysis a challenging task. Sentiment analysis, an important application of Natural Language Processing (NLP), provides an automated mechanism to extract opinions and emotional polarity from student comments, enabling institutions to better understand learners’ perspectives. This study conducts a comparative evaluation of sentiment classification techniques applied to authentic feedback collected from classroom-based teaching environments. The work contrasts conventional supervised machine learning models with modern transfer learning approaches. Classical classifiers, including Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machines (SVM), Random Forest, and Naïve Bayes, are evaluated alongside transformer-based pre-trained models such as BERT, DistilBERT, and RoBERTa. Prior to model training, the dataset undergoes standard preprocessing steps including tokenization, stopword elimination, and feature representation. Model effectiveness is assessed using precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy metrics. The experimental analysis reveals that SVM delivers the highest accuracy of 85% among traditional machine learning techniques, while Logistic Regression records an accuracy of 68%. In the category of transfer learning, DistilBERT achieves the best performance with an accuracy of 86%, exhibiting strong recall for both positive and negative sentiment classes. Furthermore, hybrid configurations improve classification outcomes, with the Logistic Regression–BERT model attaining an overall accuracy of 83%. These findings demonstrate that transfer learning and hybrid methodologies offer notable advantages over conventional approaches, particularly in capturing nuanced linguistic patterns present in student feedback.

title: Deepedge+Location: A Location-Based Task Orchestrator for Edge Computing
authors: Hubert Kyeremateng-Boateng, Darsana Josyula, David Casbeer
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Mobile edge computing has become an essential framework for facilitating latency-sensitive applications in wireless networks. The complexity of appropriate task placement intensifies when accounting for the mobility patterns of enduser devices and the diverse characteristics of application workloads. Conventional orchestration methods frequently neglect the spatial dynamics of mobile environments, resulting in inefficient resource allocation and heightened service interruptions. This work presents DeepEdge+Location, an intelligent task edge orchestration system that uses deep reinforcement learning to tackle these difficulties via location-aware decision-making. Our methodology significantly incorporates the geographical position of mobile devices as essential state information in the edge orchestration framework. This spatial awareness allows the system to comprehend the geographical context of offloading requests and make judicious decisions regarding server (edge or cloud) selection. Given that mobile devices demonstrate constant movement patterns in practical applications, incorporating positional data enables the orchestrator to proactively choose edge servers based on proximity, thus minimizing communication overhead and decreasing the probability of task failures caused by excessive transmission delays. Furthermore, proximity-based server selection enhances security and trustworthiness in edge computing by reducing the attack surface through shortened communication paths and minimizing data exposure during transmission between mobile devices and servers (edge or cloud). The DeepEdge+Location framework utilizes a Double Deep QNetwork architecture to derive appropriate offloading policies based on the intricate interconnections of device locations, network conditions, and application requirements. Our findings indicate that DeepEdge+Location surpasses existing deep reinforcement learning and non-deep reinforcement learning methods.

title: Maintaining SDN Controller High Availability Through Heartbeat Links: A Multi-SDN Solution
authors: Abdel-Fattah Al-Awami, Almabruk Sultan, Osama Fakron, Fatma Abdullah Alhaddad
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Static networks are used less and less each day they are being replaced with active and live networks with the help of this technology called SDN. Software Defined Networking is a technology concept that with new designs and ideas will help many people by saving time and energy in designing computer networks. Technology of SDN makes it easier for the user to make their own networks. The separation of control plane and data plane happens in the switches and routers of the technology and makes the software needed to keep the network up and running. Technology of SDN can be used on a much wider scale and it is very flexible. In this research paper we will try to prevent the problem of Single Point of Failure by installing multiple SDN Controllers which work together in an active passive configuration. We were able to have a very quick response time of 0.5 Ms. Switching time was found to be 2 seconds. This fast response time of ours made it possible for us to have very high availability in a small amount of time.

title: A Conceptual Framework for Multiplayer Mixed Reality Training with Biometric Feedback
authors: Karma Gurung, Dhiran Karki, Ashutosh Ghimire, Fathi Amsaad
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: First responder training requires exposure to highpressure, unpredictable environments that demand coordinated team decision-making and physical interaction, which are difficult to reproduce using conventional instruction or staged drills. While virtual reality (VR) enables safe and repeatable simulation of hazardous scenarios, fully immersive VR environments separate trainees from their physical surroundings, limiting realistic tool use and natural team coordination. Mixed reality (MR) presents a potential alternative by blending virtual content with the real environment, preserving physical context while enabling immersive training experiences. This paper presents a conceptual multiplayer MR training framework designed to address limitations of existing XR-based training systems. The proposed framework is designed to integrate synchronized team-based interaction with real-time physiological sensing to support adaptive scenario logic under stress. Rather than reporting experimentally validated outcomes, the paper formalizes a system architecture, interaction model, and evaluation framework intended to guide future empirical studies and real-world deployment of adaptive MR training for first responders.

title: Evaluating the Use of LLMs for Documentation to Code Traceability
authors: Ebube Alor, SayedHassan Khatoonabadi, Emad Shihab
publish: ACM Transactions on Software Engineering and Methodology
abstract: None

title: The Soft Clock: This Timepiece Merges Electronics and Millifluidics
authors: Nils Janßen
publish: IEEE Spectrum
abstract: Electrons are great. We use them to move vehicles, illuminate cities, and, of course, compute. But computation is not confined to the world of electronics. And shifting to alternative nonelectronic realms can unlock unique advantages: Photonic chips, for instance, process information with light while generating little heat. Another compelling alternative is fluidics, which uses pressurized gases or liquids to build logic circuits. Pioneered in the 1960s but sidelined by microchips, the field reemerged in the 1990s as “micro-fluidics.” This approach aims to shrink laboratories onto a single chip by creating microscopic fluid channels with integrated micropneumatic control systems.

title: Evaluation of power supply capability of microgrids with energy storage system
authors: Chang Wang, Hang Liu, Yang Yang, Shaomin Wang, Zhijie Tian
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: To address the issue that existing power supply capability evaluations do not consider energy storage system (ESS) and thus cannot characterize the complete power supply capability of microgrids under each time period, this paper proposes an evaluation method for the power supply capability of microgrids considering N-1 security constraints and ESS. First, a power supply capability model for microgrids incorporating N-1 security constraints and an ESS is developed. The real-time security service capability of the ESS is considered. As a result, the proposed model exhibits new characteristics: time-variability and uncertainty. Second, a solution algorithm for evaluating the microgrid power supply capability based on uniform sampling that accounts for temporal characteristics is presented, which can obtain the temporal power supply capability that truly reflects the actual operating conditions of microgrids. Third, the influence of time-varying and uncertain ESS on power supply capability is revealed. Only an ESS between two bottleneck components improves TSC, with its peak rising then plateauing as capacity increases. Furthermore, measures to enhance the power supply capability are put forward, and the application methods of the power supply capability in ESS planning are demonstrated. Finally, case studies are conducted to verify the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: A Method for Physical Monitoring of Wireline Communications Based on Compressed Sensing and Spread-Spectrum Signals
authors: Eulalia Balestrieri, Pasquale Daponte, Luca De Vito, Francesco Picariello, Sergio Rapuano, Ioan Tudosa
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper presents a method based on Compressed Sensing (CS) and spread-spectrum signals for estimating the frequency response of electrical communication cables, with applications in cable monitoring for diagnostics and physical-layer security. Compared with traditional systems in the literature, the use of CS relaxes the data-acquisition requirements regarding sampling frequency, thereby reducing the cost of electronic components and enabling integration of the method into the network transceivers for real-time, continuous cable monitoring. Moreover, the use of spread-spectrum signals excites the cable’s high-frequency response, allowing for the detection of defects as short as 0.5 cm. The experimental evaluation demonstrates the method’s capability to accurately estimate the cable’s frequency response in the band of interest, up to the 40<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</sup> Nyquist band with an undersampling ratio of 10, and to detect defects, specifically the lack of cable insulation.

title: Asin Phase Demodulation for High-resolution FMCW Interferometry
authors: Xiyuan Sun, Kai Zhu, Yao Zhu, Yapeng Pei, Yonggui Yuan, Fanyang Dang, Yunlong Zhu, Xiaojun Zhang, Xiaojie Bao, Guohua Wei, Cuofu Lin, Zhangjun Yu, Leiming Wu, Jun Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW) interferometry is a pivotal technique for high-resolution, non-contact distance measurement. However, enhancing its resolution typically requires a substantial laser tuning range, and a systematic framework linking signal quality to ultimate resolution remains underdeveloped. This paper introduces an Arcsine (Asin) phase demodulation method based on peak feature reconstruction to achieve high-resolution FMCW interferometry with a relatively compact tuning range. The proposed method reconstructs the phase via peak detection and an arcsine operation, significantly improving the accuracy of demodulation per unit tuning range. Experimental validation demonstrates that with a tuning range of only 26 GHz, our approach enables absolute distance measurement over 0–1 m with sub-micrometer resolution and an accuracy of 0.86 μm.

title: Multiagent Deep Reinforcement Learning With Long-Term Temporal Dependencies for Microgrid Energy Optimization and Scheduling
authors: Manqiu Huang, Zhenyu Zhang, Chao Xiang, Tao Yang, Jingang Lai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Effective energy optimization and scheduling within microgrids are essential for coordinating diverse power resources, improving system efficiency, and maximizing economic benefits. This article presents a novel multiagent deep reinforcement learning algorithm, power grid multiagent proximal policy optimization (PG-MAPPO), for microgrid energy optimization and scheduling. The proposed algorithm addresses key challenges in microgrid management by integrating temporal convolutional network (TCN) into policy networks, while utilizing separate replay buffers and a centralized value function. This design enables effective historical information processing, prevents data interference, and enhances global coordination among agents. Using real-world datasets from operational microgrids, experimental results demonstrate that PG-MAPPO achieves superior convergence and stability compared to baseline methods, converging to optimal policies more efficiently. Our hyperparameter experiments demonstrate that appropriate tuning of batch size and other hyperparameters is essential for maximizing PG-MAPPO's learning stability, convergence efficiency, and overall effectiveness in real-world industrial microgrid scenarios. The proposed algorithm demonstrates robust performance under uncertainty scenarios, offering promising potential for future applications in industrial microgrid environments.

title: Guest Editorial for Selected Papers From BIOKDD 2024
authors: Da Yan, Ahmed Abdeen Hamed, Jake Y. Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics

title: Investigation of Inter-turn Voltage Stress in Fractional Slot Concentrated Winding of Inverter-fed Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machines
authors: Xiaowei Ju, Zhiyuan Feng, Fengge Zhang, Yuan Cheng, Yi Li, Zhongshang Liu, Weiguo Yan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: Under the high-frequency and high-voltage inverter-fed conditions, fractional-slot concentrated winding (FSCW) may experience localized maximum inter-turn voltage stress (ITVS), which can easily lead to premature partial discharge and threaten the operational safety of the motor. One of the key reasons for this issue lies in the insufficient understanding the ITVS in motor winding, making it difficult to balance the conflicting demands of motor performance and reliability during the design stage. To bridge this gap, this paper focuses on studying the ITVS of FSCW. Firstly, a high-frequency equivalent circuit model for FSCW permanent magnet motors is proposed, which simultaneously considers the coupling effects of in slot- and the end-winding. Then, an experimental platform is built to measure the ITVS in FSCW under the rated load. The error between simulation and experimental results for the maximum ITVS is about 3.46%, validating the accuracy of the calculation models and methods. Finally, the ITVS of different winding connection scheme is analyzed, and a novel low ITVS FSCW structure is proposed. Compared to the original scheme, the maximum ITVS of proposed FSCW is reduced by 43.3%.

title: CONDEN-FI: Consistency and Diversity Learning-Based Multi-View Unsupervised Feature and Instance Co-Selection
authors: Yanyong Huang, Yuxin Cai, Dongjie Wang, Xiuwen Yi, Tianrui Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: The objective of multi-view unsupervised feature and instance co-selection is to simultaneously identify the most representative features and samples from multi-view unlabeled data, which aids in mitigating the curse of dimensionality and reducing instance size to improve the performance of downstream tasks. However, existing methods treat feature selection and instance selection as two separate processes, failing to leverage the potential interactions between the feature and instance spaces. Additionally, previous co-selection methods for multi-view data require concatenating different views, which overlooks the consistent information among them. In this paper, we propose a CONsistency and DivErsity learNing-based multi-view unsupervised Feature and Instance co-selection (CONDEN-FI) to address the above-mentioned issues. Specifically, CONDEN-FI reconstructs multi-view data from both the sample and feature spaces to learn representations that are consistent across views and specific to each view, enabling the simultaneous selection of the most important features and instances. Moreover, CONDEN-FI adaptively learns a view-consensus similarity graph to help select both dissimilar and similar samples in the reconstructed data space, leading to a more diverse selection of instances. An efficient algorithm is developed to solve the resultant optimization problem, and the comprehensive experimental results on real-world datasets demonstrate that CONDEN-FI is effective compared to state-of-the-art methods.

title: Flexible Passive LC Pressure Sensor With Impedance-Matched Readout for Extended Wireless Sensing Range_supp1-3697423.docx
authors: Wei Xu
publish: N/A
abstract: Flexible, wireless passive inductor-capacitor (LC) resonant pressure sensors offer a battery-free, conformal solution for biomedical and industrial monitoring. However, their deployment is hindered by bulky interrogation equipment and the limited readout range and bandwidth of portable readers. To address these challenges, we present an integrated sensing platform that combines a multilayer LC sensor fabricated on a flexible printed circuit board (FPCB), an embedded vent channel, and an impedance-matching readout circuit (IMC). The LC device features a primary sensing capacitor formed by 18 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{\mu}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>m-thick circular copper electrodes (2.3 mm radius), separated by polyimide (PI) dielectric layers and a 40 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{\mu}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>m air gap. Analytical modeling and finite element simulations show that under 0–5 kPa pressure loading, the sensor exhibits a linear resonance frequency shift from 153.8 to 137 MHz, corresponding to a theoretical sensitivity of −3.27 MHz/kPa. In addition, a voltage-driven interrogation scheme is developed and benchmarked against both a conventional wireless readout circuit (WRC) and a tunable IMC, with the sensing resonance identified by peaks in DC output. Experimental validation demonstrates a measured sensor sensitivity of −3.2 MHz/kPa with less than 2% deviation from theory, under both WRC and IMC configurations. Moreover, the IMC boosts sensor signal strength by 10<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{\times}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and extends the effective readout distance to at least 16 mm, which is 1.6<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{\times}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> longer than that of the WRC. Therefore, this compact, high-performance platform demonstrates strong potential for real-time, long-distance physiological pressure monitoring in next-generation wearable and implantable sensing systems.

title: IEEE Feedback
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Control Systems

title: Fixed-Time Optimal-Power Thrust Control in Distributed Electric Cycloidal Propulsion Using Hybrid Residual Neural Networks
authors: Subhashis Nandy, Yoonsoo Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Distributed electric propulsion enabled cycloidal rotors offer enhanced maneuverability and sustainability for aerial and marine platforms through independent blade-level actuation. However, their over-actuated and nonlinear nature offers significant challenges in achieving accurate thrust control and vectoring alongside power-optimal operation. Additionally, a purely model-based optimal controller may yield inaccurate performance predictions due to the complex cycloidal rotor geometry, modeling uncertainties, and unsteady aerodynamic effects. To mitigate modeling limitations, the cycloidal rotor performance is evaluated using a hybrid approach by integrating a physics-based aerodynamic model with a residual neural network to predict the discrepancies in thrust and power. To address the control and optimization problem, the present article develops an optimal-power fixed-time control framework that integrates thrust tracking and energy minimization within a unified architecture. By exploiting the null space of the thrust Jacobian, the proposed approach achieves power optimization without compromising thrust feasibility. Moreover, the fixed-time control law guaranties convergence of the thrust vector to its desired value and convergence of the control inputs to their optimal values within a time bound. A Lyapunov-based analysis is employed to establish fixed-time stability and to derive an explicit bound on the settling time. Numerical results reflect that the proposed hybrid model outperforms the physics-based model in predicting thrust and power. Additional numerical simulations validate the theoretical results, demonstrating uniform thrust convergence, bounded control rates, dynamic thrust tracking with blade activation–deactivation and synchronization, and convergence to a constrained power-optimal solution. Compared with existing methods, the proposed approach achieves an approximately 22% improvement in thrust tracking performance and improves efficiency by <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.42\% - 8.58 \%$</tex-math></inline-formula> with optimal power consumption.

title: Wiley
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Antennas and Propagation Magazine

title:
Field- and Temperature-Induced Drift in Multi-Level Al-Doped HfO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    RRAM
authors: Andrea Baroni, Jianan Wen, Eduardo Pérez, Keerthi Dorai Swamy Reddy, Max Uhlmann, Stefan Pechmann, Christian Wenger, Daniele Ielmini, Cristian Zambelli
publish: IEEE Transactions on Device and Materials Reliability
abstract: Drift of multi-level conductance states is a key reliability concern for resistive random access memory (RRAM) used in multi-level storage and in-memory computing applications. This work experimentally quantifies how repeated read operations and elevated-temperature storage reshape multiple conductive states in Al5%:HfO2-based RRAM cells. Systematic read-disturb and multi-temperature retention experiments are performed on one-transistor-one-resistor (1T1R) arrays, and their evolution is analyzed through intra-level transition matrices and a distance-weighted reshuffling factor Fr that jointly capture internal reshuffling dynamics beyond median and spread. Read-disturb exhibits a pronounced voltage dependence, with increasing reshuffling as VRD rises, whereas retention shows a two-phase thermal evolution dominated by an early warm-up reshuffling followed by a slower isothermal regime. Comparing incremental step pulse and verify algorithm (ISPVA) and one-shot programming (OSP) under identical targets and read-stress conditions further shows that both algorithms experience redistribution, but with different directionality and level dependence. These results clarify the distinct statistical signatures of field-and temperature-induced drift and identify the programming algorithm, read-voltage budget, and warm-up handling as key design knobs to improve the number of reliable levels in multi-level RRAM arrays.

title: Optimizing Task Scheduling in Fog Computing with Deadline Awareness
authors: Mohammad Sadegh Sirjani, Mohammad Ahmad, Amir Mousavi, Erfan Nourbakhsh, Khoa Nguyen
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: The rise of Internet of Things (IoT) devices has led to the development of numerous time-sensitive applications that require quick responses and low latency. Fog computing has emerged as a solution for processing these IoT applications, but it faces challenges such as resource allocation and job scheduling. Therefore, it is crucial to determine how to assign and schedule tasks on Fog nodes. This work aims to schedule tasks in IoT while minimizing the total energy consumption of nodes and enhancing the Quality of Service (QoS) requirements of IoT tasks, taking into account task deadlines. This paper classifies Fog nodes into two categories based on their traffic level: low and high. It schedules short-deadline tasks on lowtraffic nodes using an Improved Golden Eagle Optimization (IGEO) algorithm, an enhancement that utilizes genetic operators for discretization. Long-deadline tasks are processed on hightraffic nodes using reinforcement learning (RL). This combined approach is called the Reinforcement Improved Golden Eagle Optimization (RIGEO) algorithm. Experimental results demonstrate that RIGEO achieves up to a 29 % reduction in energy consumption, up to an 86 % improvement in response time, and up to a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 9 \%}$</tex> reduction in deadline violations compared to state-of-the-art algorithms.

title: Reconfigurable Filters [From the Editor’s Desk]
authors: Robert Caverly
publish: IEEE Microwave Magazine

title: NEITH: Networked Evidence Integrity for Telemetry Hashing
authors: Mohamed El-Hadedy
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Autonomous platforms rely on high-rate multisensor telemetry (e.g., Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) and LiDAR) for safety, accountability, and post-incident reconstruction, yet disputes often reduce to a basic question: what data was actually retained. This paper presents NEITH, a lightweight window-commitment contract that makes retention externally checkable without trusted storage or full log replication. NEITH partitions time into windows of length <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta$</tex>. Within each window, each ingested chunk is mapped to a leaf position from ingestionobservable metadata (sensor identifier and per-window index) and aggregated with standard hash functions and extendableoutput functions (XOFs) in a Merkle-style tree, yielding one constant-size root <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R_{t}$</tex> (32 bytes) per window. Later, a verifier checks retention claims from the published roots and a short inclusion proof (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R_{t}, c, \pi$</tex>); an optional order rule also makes within-window reordering detectable. NEITH runs on a small edge Kubernetes cluster and is evaluated with u-blox GNSS and Youyeetoo LiDAR streams plus bursty synthetic producers. Over 32 consecutive windows <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\Delta=10 ~\mathrm{s})$</tex>, the system emits one root per window and stays aligned to the window schedule. Microbenchmarks on 8 KiB leaves show that widely used tree-hash designs sustain <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 - 2 6 ~ M B} / \mathbf{s}$</tex> under 1/8/32 concurrent connections, whereas a reference Python ASCON-XOF baseline costs <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 760 ~\text{ms}$</tex> per 8 KiB leaf, indicating when the no-stutter timing bound cannot be met on this software stack. Overall, window commitments provide a practical integrity layer for verifiable telemetry retention on resource-constrained cyber-physical pipelines.

title: AUTOCTI: Automated Cyber Threat Detection Using AutoML
authors: Ravishankara Kulamarva, Preetham Bhandary, Sanjana A Padukone, Sakshi V Moger, Sreedevi P, Anantha Krishna Kamath
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: The increasing complexity and frequency of cyber-attacks have created a strong need for fast, intelligent, automated, and adaptive security systems. Traditional threat detection approaches rely heavily on static rules and manual configuration, which limits their ability to respond to evolving attack patterns. This paper presents the design and implementation of AutoCTI: Automated Cyber Threat Detection Using AutoML, a real-time framework that integrates modular threat detection and automated response mechanisms with automated machine learning.The proposed system’s FastAPI-based backend and React.js-powered frontend dashboard enable real-time monitoring and analysis of incoming system logs. By automatically choosing and optimizing models using AutoML techniques (Auto-sklearn/H2O), cyberthreats such as malware, phishing, and brute-force attacks can be identified. The categorization of identified threats based on severity and confidence scores initiates automated response actions, such as blocking, eliminating, or quarantining malicious entities. Real-time updates are delivered via WebSocket communication.Experimental evaluation shows that AutoCTI improves detection performance, reduces false positives, and boosts response efficiency when compared to traditional systems. The modular, database-free architecture ensures scalability, flexibility, and ease of deployment in a variety of environments. All things considered, this work offers a practical and automated cybersecurity framework that can provide prompt and reliable defense against modern cyberthreats.

title: Detection of Multiclass Pediatric Congenital Heart Defects using Deep Learning based Stacking Ensemble Method
authors: Chaithrashree M, M Hemashree, Sharanya Manohar, Diya Deepak Talekar, Vighnesh Bk
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Congenital Heart Diseases (CHDs) are the most common pediatric cardiac abnormalities and detection is crucial for successful management. However, the challenge of distinguishing between several types of CHDs from chest X-rays (CXRs) is high because of inter-class similarity and low annotated datasets. The present work introduces a deep learning-based stacking ensemble model for multiclass CHD classification of atrial septal defect (ASD), ventricular septal defect (VSD), patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) and normal cases. EfficientNetB1 and DenseNet121 are used as feature extractors in the proposed model, Vision Transformer (ViT) and ResNet50 are utilized as base classifiers. Their predictions are combined and given to XGBoost meta-learner, which serves as an effective decision layer. Experimental validation on the CHD-CXR dataset reported an overall accuracy of 94%, macro-averaged precision, recall and F1-score of 0.94, which is superior to state of the art methods in all metrics. Grad-CAM visualizes the regions of interest in CXR images, helping in understanding the model’s decisionmaking process. The proposed ensemble model offers a reliable, understandable and efficient solution for AI-based detection of pediatric CHD, ultimately improving detection and treatment outcomes.

title: Multimodal Fusion with Cross-Modal Attention for Robust MCI-to-AD Prediction
authors: Druthi K N, Chiranjeevi C
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: For prompt intervention, early prediction of the progression of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) from mild cognitive impairment (MCI) is essential. We suggest a cross-modal attention neural network that incorporates 3D structural MRI from the ADNI dataset, FreeSurfer biomarkers, and clinical evaluations. In order to learn the best feature interactions across these diverse data kinds, our architecture uses multi-head attention. Our model obtained 88.45% accuracy and an AUC of 0.874 for 24-month progression prediction by addressing important clinical data difficulties, particularly restricted MRI availability (present for only 14.8% of patients) and a severe class imbalance (18.6% progression rate). Compared to single-modality methods, this performs noticeably better (Clinical: 0.732 AUC, FreeSurfer: 0.781 AUC, MRI: 0.823 AUC). Clinical aspects (42%), FreeSurfer measurements (35%), and MRI features (23%) all contribute in a complimentary way, according to an analysis of the attention mechanisms. Cross-validation confirms robustness (0.866 ± 0.011 AUC). This comprehensible approach allows evidence-based patient classification and offers therapeutically actionable predictions, as demonstrated by a 95.65% negative predictive value.

title: FairLabeler: Achieving Fairness via Biased Label Detection and Correction
authors: Hem Chandra Joshi, Sandeep Kumar
publish: IEEE Intelligent Systems
abstract: Fairness in machine learning (ML)-based software has gained increasing attention as these systems are deployed in critical decision-making. Since ML software learns from labeled data, biased labels can substantially affect prediction outcomes, potentially resulting in discriminatory behavior. This study presents FairLabeler, a model-agnostic preprocessing approach that detects and massages biased labels in training datasets, thereby improving both individual-and group-level fairness while preserving predictive performance. We evaluate FairLabeler on nine real-world datasets using standard ML classifiers and a deep neural network. Experimental results show that FairLabeler surpasses the state-of-the-art bias mitigation method, as assessed with the fairness–performance tradeoff tool Fairea, achieving fairness with improved performance in 11% more cases. To facilitate replication and further research, we publicly release the code and datasets at <ext-link ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="https://github.com/fairlabeler/anonymous" xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/fairlabeler/anonymous</ext-link>.

title: An Age-Aware Joint Scheduling and Semantic Extraction Approach for IIoT Systems With Multi-AGVs
authors: Xin Xie, Xiaoqin Wang, Heng Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Ensuring timely and meaningful information delivery is critical in Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) applications. Semantic communication has emerged as a promising paradigm in industrial networks due to its ability to transmit task-relevant information efficiently, thereby guaranteeing real-time transmission. In this work, we consider a semantic communication-based IIoT system with multiple automated guided vehicles (AGVs), with the objective of minimizing the long-term average age of information (AoI) by jointly designing AGV scheduling and semantic extraction ratio setting policies. The key challenge lies in balancing the inherent tradeoff between reduced transmission delay through extraction and the potential risk of semantic distortion, and the strong coupling between discrete scheduling decisions and continuous extraction control further exacerbates the difficulty of solving the problem. To this end, we develop a hybrid deep reinforcement learning framework that integrates a dueling double deep Q-network for discrete vehicle selection and proximal policy optimization for continuous semantic extraction ratio control. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance in reducing the long-term average AoI compared to existing approaches.

title: Modeling and Modification of Equivalent Planar Unfolding for Post-Type Composite Insulators
authors: Ping Huang, Yongjie Liao, Li Tian, Yiyi Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: The pollution flashover discharge model is widely used to predict insulator flashover voltage; however, the complexity of existing models increases computational time and cost. In this study, the FZSMW-100/12.5 post insulator is selected as the original model, and both a true-profile unfolding model and a rectangular equivalent flat-plate model are developed.An equivalent wetted layer thickness and an arc development criterion are introduced to define the conditions for dry band formation and arc initiation. Considering the time-varying residual pollution resistance during arc propagation, a dynamic model of the surface pollution discharge process is established, solved, and validated experimentally.Simulated flashover voltages obtained under different unfolding methods are compared with experimental results. A correction approach for the rectangular flat-plate model is proposed, and its applicability is evaluated.Results show that the prediction error of the proposed model is within 10%. The true-profile unfolding model better represents the actual pollution flashover process, whereas the rectangular model prolongs flashover time due to boundary simplification. After correction, the voltage prediction accuracy of the rectangular model is improved with reduced computational cost. The true-profile model is recommended for detailed process simulation, while the rectangular model is suitable for rapid voltage prediction with subsequent correction if necessary.

title: M3T-UAV: Vision-based UAV geolocalization in multimodal, multiscene, and multitemporal scenarios
authors: Shangjie Xue, Jinhai Xu, Yuelei Xu, Zhaoxiang Zhang, Xuanbin Wang
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Magazine
abstract: Reliable unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) geolocation is essential for autonomous navigation, particularly in environments where satellite signals are vulnerable to spoofing and interference. However, current vision-based UAV positioning approaches rely heavily on visible imagery and lack large-scale multimodal datasets with strict spatial alignment, limiting their robustness across diverse scenes and temporal conditions. To bridge this gap, we introduce M3T-UAV, the first large-scale dataset specifically designed for vision-based UAV geolocation across multimodal, multiscene, and multitemporal scenarios. M3T-UAV contains more than 29,000 (29K) densely sampled and strictly registered triplets of visible, infrared (IR), and satellite images, covering seven representative natural scene attributes. Moreover, we extend the standard AUC@T metric and propose two novel evaluation measures, GeoAUC@T and Visual Positioning Score (VPS), to better assess multimodal UAV geolocation performance. Methodologically, we present LoFTDF, a semidense matching framework that achieves high-precision image matching by redesigning the soft assignment matrix learning procedure and introducing a dual-deformation field (DDF) module for fine-level geometric alignment. The proposed LoFTDF achieves state-of-the- art performance on the M3T-UAV benchmark, reaching 0.9036 VPS on the visible–satellite image matching task and 0.4421 VPS on the IR–satellite task, substantially outperforming existing matching-based geolocation methods. The code and dataset will be publicly available at: https:// github.com/JinHaiXu0322/M3T-UAV.

title: AFDM Transceiver Optimization for PAPR Reduction
authors: Hongjun Liu, Yusha Liu, Guanghui Liu, Dan Tian, Yao Sun, Qingyu Li, Fuchen Xu, Chengxiang Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Communications
abstract: In affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM) systems, the severe peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR) signals exist in the time domain due to the coherent superposition of numerous modulated symbols. Eventually, high PAPR signals require sophisticated and expensive power amplifiers with a very large linear range. To this end, a neural network (NN) aided intelligent transceiver optimization framework is proposed for suppressing PAPR based on the spreading AFDM structure. Specifically, the transceiver jointly optimizes the constellation geometry and associated bit labeling, the precoding NN, as well as the NN based detector. Moreover, the precoding NN is learned from a precoding approach which minimizes the variance of the instantaneous power of output signals at the transmitter. The joint optimization framework aims to achieve maximum PAPR reduction under the constraints of unit energy and spectral emission mask. Besides, to mitigate the potential inter-carrier interference during the offline training, a long short term memory based detector is designed within the optimization framework. Simulation results demonstrate that the conceived NN based optimization method achieves a significant enhancement on PAPR reduction compared with conventional approaches, while slightly improving the bit error ratio performance.

title: Are Drivers Tired? An Edge AI-enabled Drowsiness Detection Framework in IoV
authors: Yadi He, Sitan Chen, Jia Xu, Linfeng Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: Vehicle accidents result in a large number of fatalities and substantial economic losses annually. A significant portion of vehicle accidents are attributed to driver negligence, particularly drowsy driving (often caused by fatigue), which can seriously weaken drivers' alertness in steering control, lane keeping, and maintaining a safe distance from other vehicles. Thus, effective drowsiness detection is essential for preventing accidents. The fatigue state of drivers can be inferred from videos captured by in-vehicle cameras. To achieve accurate and rapid detection, both personal and common fatigue features should be learned from historical driving videos and jointly exploited for state recognition. In this paper, we propose an edge AI-enabled drowsiness detection framework. We design a dual-branch architecture for feature fusion that integrates the local models to capture personalized fatigue features and the regional models to extract common fatigue features, thereby improving the detection accuracy while reducing detection latency. Furthermore, to lower computational overhead, we identify key frames in videos and represent facial landmarks with relative polar coordinates, which accelerates feature extraction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach performs well in terms of accuracy, AUC, and F1 score (0.9986, 0.9999, and 0.9986, respectively). Moreover, only 85 frames (45 I-frames and 40 P-frames) are required per detection instead of the original 795 frames, resulting in a detection delay of approximately 517 ms.

title: Sparse ISAR Imaging via Frequency-Selective Atomic Norm Minimization and Accelerated ADMM
authors: Sui Wang, Shuang Yue, Zhengquan Zhou, Huagui Du, Chongyi Fan, Xiaotao Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Inverse synthetic aperture radar (ISAR) imaging technique has shown significant potential in civilian and military fields. However, radar systems encounter serious obstacles in acquiring long or full apertures. ISAR imaging targets exhibit sparsity in the spatial domain, which is beneficial for sparse recovery (SR) methods to achieve aperture reconstruction. This paper proposes the fast frequency-selective (FS) gridless sparse ISAR imaging methods to achieve high-precision aperture reconstruction, which fully considers the advantages of gridless techniques, structured model and prior knowledge. Usually, the FS atomic norm minimization (ANM) (FSANM) encodes the prior knowledge as a additional constraint into semi-positive definite programming (SDP), resulting in high computational complexity. The proposed method assumes that the selected frequency range follows the prior probability distribution, which is used to construct a weighting function for the atoms. Meanwhile, vandermonde decomposition and linear interpolation are employed to enhance the selection probability of adjacent atom. Then, a non-conveax metric has been implemented to enhance atomic sparsity. Although the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) typically provides a more efficient solution for semidefinite programming problems, the standard eigen decomposition still faces problem of high computational complexity. Finally, the Lanczos decomposition algorithm is employed to achieve faster eigen-decomposition, thereby enhancing the computational efficiency of ISAR imaging. The experimental results of the simulated and measured data show that compared with the FSANM method, the proposed method has higher computational efficiency and better imaging performance under lower signal-to-noise ratio and data rate.

title: TC-SSFN: Suppressing Seasonal Noise and Enhancing Feature Discrimination for Land Cover Time Series Change Detection
authors: Yukun Lin, Chenyin Ding, Qianwen Cheng, Shiqiang Du, Jia Yu, Chen Wu, Bo Du, Liangpei Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Change detection using multi-temporal remote sensing imagery was a critical tool for monitoring land surface dynamics. However, conventional methods often suffered from limited spatial-temporal feature integration, seasonal noise interference, and poor interpretability. To address these limitations, we proposed a Temporal-Consistent Spectral-Spatial Fusion Network (TC-SSFN) that jointly modeled local spatial-spectral structures and long-term temporal dynamics through four key modules: a Spatial Feature Extraction module (SFE) for hierarchical spatial encoding, a Temporal Series Modeling module (TSM) employing patch-based self-attention, a Quarter-based Seasonal Encoding module (QSE) for suppressing inter-annual seasonal noise, and a Deep Supervision strategy (DS) that injected label-guided constraints into intermediate layers to enhance feature discriminability and interpretability. Experimental results across five diverse scenes in China demonstrated that TC-SSFN achieved state-of-the-art performance, with an average F1 score of 93.33% and IOU of 87.69%, consistently outperforming baseline methods. Ablation and clustering analyses further validated the complementary role of each module and revealed that supervision-induced feature structuring improved semantic separability. The quarter-based temporal segmentation strategy grouped multi-temporal images by season and was combined with a differencing strategy. This combined approach effectively balanced sensitivity to both gradual and abrupt changes while preserving more meaningful change patterns. Overall, TC-SSFN provided a robust, interpretable, and generalizable framework for land cover change detection in complex, seasonally dynamic environments, offering practical value for large-scale monitoring and long-term remote sensing applications. The codes of our proposed TC-SSFN model are available at https://github.com/ChenyinD/TC-SSFN, and the datasets used in this study can be accessed at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18222086.

title: Physically Consistent Near-Field MISO Communication: Impact of Receiver-to-Transmitter Mutual Coupling on Achievable Rate
authors: Mujun Qian, Lv Ye, Chen Liu, Hong Wang, Yunchao Song, Tianbao Gao
publish: IEEE Communications Letters
abstract: Receiver-to-transmitter mutual coupling (MC) is an electromagnetic interaction phenomenon between transmit and receive antennas. This letter is the first to employ multi-port communication theory—a framework bridging information theory and electromagnetic theory—to investigate the impact of receiver-transmitter MC on near-field multiple-input-single-output (MISO) achievable rate. To this end, we derive a physically consistent MISO model based on multiport communication theory, which directly incorporates receiver-to-transmitter MC. Based on the derived physically consistent MISO model, we investigate the impact of receiver-to-transmitter MC on achievable rates. Results indicate that receiver-to-transmitter MC significantly impacts achievable rates in the reactive near-field, while its effect on far-field communication is negligible. In the radiative near-field region, the influence of the array antenna configuration must be taken into account.

title: Quantum Machine Learning for Wireless-Powered UAV Positioning in 6G Digital Twin SAGIN with Cooperative Nano-Satellite Constellations
authors: Sasinda C. Prabhashana, Minh-Hien T. Nguyen, Vishal Sharma, Thang X. Vu, Berk Canberk, Hyundong Shin, Trung Q. Duong
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications
abstract: Energy-efficient space–air–ground integrated networks (SAGINs) are vital for sustainable communications. This study presents an energy-aware SAGIN framework that utilizes a uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV)-mounted mobile edge computing (MEC) platform enhanced by digital-twin technology, UAV energy harvesting via wireless power transfer, and a nano-satellite constellation with MEC facilities. We formulate a joint optimization problem for UAV trajectory planning, task offloading, computational resource allocation, and satellite load balancing as a mixed-integer nonlinear programming (MINLP) problem that minimizes the weighted system cost while satisfying energy and latency constraints. To address this complex problem, two quantum-driven deep reinforcement learning (QD-DRL) algorithms namely quantum-driven cost-effective advantage actor–critic (QD-CE-A2C) and quantum-driven cost-effective proximal policy optimization (QD-CE-PPO) are proposed. These algorithms employ angle encoding with learnable parameters and variational quantum neural networks to enhance policy exploration and accelerate convergence. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed QD-DRL approaches achieve superior cost efficiency and ensure effective service to all access points within the defined mission duration. Moreover, QD-DRL approaches achieved higher cumulative rewards and faster convergence compared to classical DRL baselines. Consequently, the proposed frameworks provide a scalable and intelligent paradigm for cost-efficient resource management in future 6G-enabled SAGINs.

title: Guiding Light Beyond sight: AI Powered Object Detection for Blind Navigation
authors: Sowmya, Sahana, Shadwal C Rao, Sheikh Mohammed Shuhood, Shravya, Sindhura
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: This paper describes building a project which uses YOLOv5 and Faster-RCNN technologies for object detection to improve navigation for visually impaired users. The system is incorporated with a camera to detect the objects and the machine learning model processes the images to get the detected objects’ identities and distances during both daytime and nighttime and the description is then made into audio cues and sent through the earphone to provide auditory cues in real-time and enabling the users to navigate confidently and independently in their day-to-day life. The project showcases technology’s transformative potential in inclusivity and accessibility.

title: Bone Fracture Detection Using Vision Transformers: A Comparative Analysis of the Pooling-Based Vision Transformer (PiT) and the Causal Transformer (CaFormer) Models
authors: Atikul Islam Munna, Md. Ibrahim Khalil, Assaduzzaman Munna, Arnob Halder, Souvik Pramanik, Most. Aysha Siddika Sumona, Ahmed Faizul Haque Dhrubo, Mohammad Abdul Qayum
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Bone fracture detection in radiography remains a critical challenge in medical imaging, where minor fractures often elude both human experts and conventional deep learning models. This study introduces a novel comparative analysis of vision transformer architectures of Pooling-based Vision Transformer (PiT), Causal Transformer (CaFormer), ConvNeXt, XCiT, and HardCoRe-NAS to address the limitations of traditional convolutional networks in fracture diagnosis. Using a dataset of 4,083 annotated X-ray images (hand, leg, hip, shoulder), we evaluated model performance through rigorous augmentation, hyperparameter tuning, and cross-validation. Despite achieving 98.83 % training accuracy, CaFormer demonstrates a generalization gap with 97.30 % testing accuracy, while PiT outperforms all models with 97.51 % testing accuracy despite lower training performance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(97.90 \%)$</tex>. Our findings reveal a critical paradox: models excelling in training do not necessarily generalize better to unseen clinical data. Our work underscores the potential of vision transformers, particularly PiT, in automating fracture detection while highlighting the risks of overreliance on training metrics. Our study concludes with actionable insights to improve model robustness through dataset balancing and segmentation in future research.

title: Wildlife Detection and Monitoring using Single Shot Detection
authors: Roopa Nayak, Aashritha R Prasad, Bhoomika P, Aditi K Rao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: The problem of wildlife extinction, unhealthy and unbalanced ecosystem are intertwined. The healthy population of wildlife assists the sustenance of a balanced ecosystem and vice-versa. Extinction of animals in a certain region of forest can be avoided by constantly checking the presence of animals with the help of modern computer vision techniques. These techniques pose less threats to both animals and humans and offers a near to accurate results. This paper describes the work carried out to detect and monitor wild animals using single shot multi box detection with Tensor Flow API. On employing Tensor flow SSD detection for wildlife, the result was found to be satisfactory in terms of accuracy and speed. For every image detection, log report is generated that can be analyzed to detect the movement pattern of animals in a particular region.

title: A Comprehensive Review on Handwritten Descriptive Answer Script Evaluation
authors: Pradeep Rao K B, Thyagaraju G S, Sahana Kumari B, Prasad S R
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: The evaluation of handwritten descriptive answer scripts remains a complex and resource-intensive task in education. While manual assessment offers depth, it is time-consuming, prone to bias, and difficult to scale. Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI)—particularly in optical character recognition (OCR), natural language processing (NLP), and deep learning—have fueled growing interest in automated grading systems. This review synthesizes current research and proposes a structured taxonomy encompassing application stages, computational techniques, and language domains.
