authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Microwave Magazine

title: A Whole-cell Extended Gate Field Effect Transistor-Based Immunosensor for Rapid and Sensitive Detection of E. coli
authors: Salomão Moraes Da Silva, Vitaly Efremov, Mila Djisalov, Vasa Radonic, Jovana Stanojev, Ivana Gadjanski, Heman Kumar Ramiya Ramesh Babu
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: A whole bacterial cell immunosensing method based on extended-gate field-effect transistor (EGFET) is presented for the first time in this report. Herein, a commercial n-type MOSFET is modified into an EGFET by extending the gate to the biosensing layer via a simple signal line. As a biosensing layer, a gold electrode functionalized with antibodies is fabricated to confer specificity to Escherichia coli (E. coli). The transistor’s transfer and output characteristics I-V curves were acquired with a commercial parameter analyzer, and the sensor response is characterized by observing how the channel current counters to different concentrations of bacteria bound to the biosensing layer. The EGFET immunosensor demonstrates the detection limit of ≤1.5 CFU mL-1, linearity (R² > 0.98) within the range of concentrations from 1.5×101 to 1.5×10⁸ CFU mL-1. The specificity is confirmed with two non-target bacteria species (Klebsiella aerogenes and Bacillus subtilis). The developed whole-cell, label-free immunosensor has the potential to be adapted as a point-of-care diagnostic device as a practical, low-cost alternative to conventional laboratory-bound methods.

title: CTTF: Cross-Temporal Tri-Difference Fusion with Mamba for Remote Sensing Change Detection
authors: Shuying Li, Haolan Lei, Yuemei Qin, Chuang Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Mamba has recently been widely applied to remote sensing change detection tasks due to its ability to efficiently represent long-range dependencies with nearly linear computational complexity. However, existing Mamba-based change detection methods focus primarily on global modeling during the decoding stage, where insufficient attention to fine-grained details causes blurred change boundaries. In addition, most existing methods rely solely on a single type of differential feature (e.g., signed difference or absolute difference), ignoring the complementary information among different types of differential features, which makes them prone to missed or false detections under registration errors. To address these issues, this paper proposes a decoder-side improved framework named CTTF (Cross-Temporal Tri-Diff Fusion). It consists of a GLP (Global–Local–Point) Block and a SASF (Signed–Absolute–Sum Fusion) Module, enabling accurate boundary feature detection, thereby achieving more consistent and reliable change discrimination. Specifically, the GLP Block decouples and models boundary details, global semantics, and information anchors, achieving a dynamic balance between local enhancement and semantic fidelity, thereby significantly improving the boundary feature perception. The SASF constructs a tri-differential mechanism, where the sum difference serves as a stability prior to perform multi-scale calibration between the signed difference and the absolute difference. This design compensates for the loss of fine structural and consistency information caused by relying on a single differential domain, effectively reducing both missed and false detections. The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on the WHU-CD, LEVIR-CD, SYSU-CD and CLCD datasets. Ablation experiments further validate the effectiveness of the proposed SASF Module and GLP Block.

title: Saliency Characteristic Enhancement Design and Analysis of a Partitioned Rotor Pole PM Motor Considering the Flux-Approaching Current
authors: Wenjie Fan, Xiaoyong Zhu, Lei Xu, Mingzi Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: The conventional controllable leakage flux motor shows a good flux regulation capability while at heavy loads, the saturated q-axis magnetic circuit results in an insignificant saliency characteristic with similar d-q axis inductances. As a result, the reluctance torque deteriorates and hence the output torque. In this article, a new partitioned rotor poles permanent magnet motor is proposed, where the inductance regulation rotor pole (IRRP) is introduced independently from the flux regulation rotor pole (FRRP) unit. This enhances the d-q inductance difference free from adding the d-axis flux barriers and without reducing PM utilization, thus improving the reluctance torque and output torque thereby, which showing great applications in the off-road electric vehicles. Furthermore, a new concept of flux-approaching current is proposed and its relationships with motor parameters are revealed. Furthermore, considering the partitioned rotor poles with differentiated magnetic circuits, an extended equivalent magnetic circuit with a unified air gap loop is developed. On this basis, the multiple requirements of torque and speed range are converted into the requirements of flux and inductance parameters, and hence, the motor parameters can be calculated. Finally, the proposed motor and design method are verified by simulation and experimental results.

title: Optimal Control of Multidimensional Switched Systems via Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Xinbao Li, Yan Zhang, Zhengrong Xiang
publish: IEEE Systems Journal
abstract: This article investigates an optimal control problem for multidimensional switched systems with subsystems of varying state dimensions. The key challenge lies in the design of switching strategies and impulse control policies that ensure system stability and performance optimality despite dimensional mismatches. To address this challenge, the state vectors of all subsystems are mapped into a unified Euclidean space, which lays the foundation for achieving consistent switching and control policy design across modes. A deep reinforcement learning algorithm is developed to approximate the optimal value function and to derive switching sequences and impulse control inputs, without relying on the solution of Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman or Riccati equations. Under the proposed design scheme, the closed-loop system is uniformly ultimately bounded, and the state trajectories are guaranteed to converge to a compact set around the origin. Numerical simulations demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed approach.

title: Robust Text-to-Image Person Re-identification via Neighbor Consistency-based Correspondence Estimation
authors: Jinjie Liu, Hao Wu, Xueping Wang, Min Liu, Yaonan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Text-to-image person re-identification (TIReID) retrieves target individuals using textual queries, demanding precise alignment of sparse visual-textual cues. Real-world training data, however, suffers from noisy correspondence (NC), i.e., misaligned image-text pairs from annotation errors or ambiguities. While existing NC methods work for generic retrieval, they falter in fine-grained TIReID (identity-rich but pair-limited environments); crucially, their per-sample loss filtering mistakenly discards discriminative hard samples essential for subtle identity distinctions. To address these limitations, we propose Neighbor Consistency-based Correspondence Estimation (NCCE), a unified framework that integrates cross-modal context modeling and progressive noise suppression. Specifically, the Neighbor-Consistent Noise Identification (NCNI) strategy moves beyond per-sample analysis by inferring textual descriptions from the visual neighbors of an image and evaluating the consistency between the aggregated neighbor-inferred text and the original text via weighted similarity, thus avoiding the pitfalls of loss-based filtering. Moreover, Modality-Shared Projection Learning (MSPL) addresses fine-grained mismatches by projecting both modalities into a unified space, enabling adaptive alignment through mutual regularization. Extensive experiments on three public TIReID benchmarks validate the superiority of the proposed method. Notably, on the ICFG-PEDES dataset with a 50% noise ratio, our NCCE method achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance, obtaining a Rank-1 accuracy of 66.15% (+2.48% improvement) and a mAP of 40.82% (+3.51% improvement). Consistent improvements of 1-3% in both metrics are observed on the other two datasets. Furthermore, our method demonstrates strong generalizability by achieving new SOTA results on the general image retrieval benchmark MS-COCO5K.

title:
Development of the Ramsey Microwave Cavity for a Time-keeping
                    <sup>87</sup>
                    Rb Fountain Clock
authors: Hui Zhang, Shaojie Yang, Yong Guan, Sichen Fan, Dandan Liu, Yang Bai, Fan Liu, Jun Ruan, Shougang Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The time-keeping <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">87</sup>Rb fountain clock (TKRFC) is a microwave atomic clock with superior long-term frequency stability. However, as the core component, the Ramsey microwave cavity introduces key limitations for future engineering applications, particularly in system complexity, production consistency, and temperature sensitivity. This paper presents a novel cavity design, offering a new approach to overcoming these challenges. The core innovations are as follows: (1) A structural design incorporating a copper shielding tube and an extended lower end-cap with a cut-off waveguide physically suppresses microwave leakage interference on the atoms. This ensures performance while reducing or even eliminating the need for traditional external microwave interference switches. (2) A fully welded packaging process is employed, providing a viable and standardized solution for both vacuum and microwave sealing. (3) The cavity is designed with a relatively low loaded quality factor (~3120) to minimize temperature-driven cavity pulling frequency shifts. Theoretical and simulation analyses indicate that the cavity exhibits the potential to contribute to a relative frequency stability of the clock output below 1.3 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-17</sup>. This work explores a potential technical route to address key bottlenecks in the cavity, aiming to facilitate the future engineering application of the clock.

title: On Integer-Forcing Hybrid Precoding for Downlink Millimeter Wave MIMO Systems
authors: Jinsong Wang, Tao Yang, Xinzhe Qiu, Rongke Liu, Zhenyu Xiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: This paper studies a new integer-forcing (IF) hybrid precoding scheme for downlink millimeter-wave MIMO systems. First, we formulate the partially connected hybrid precoding optimization problem. Then, we decompose the problem into separate analog precoding/combining and digital precoding components. To boost the spectral efficiency, we develop an IF method for the digital precoder. Due to the unit-modulus constraint, the analog precoder is more challenging to optimize, for which we employ an element-iteration (EI) algorithm. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed IF hybrid precoding scheme, which combines analog and digital precoding, approaches the performance upper bound of partially-connected hybrid precoding architectures. It achieves substantial performance gains over existing hybrid precoding schemes in both spectral efficiency and bit error rate (BER).

title: Selecting and Distilling Cross-Label Models
authors: Su Lu, Han-Jia Ye, De-Chuan Zhan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: The practice of knowledge distillation, wherein pre-trained models – termed ”teachers” – serve to enhance the performance of subsequent ”student” models, has seen a surge in popularity. With a lot of pre-trained models available, there is a pressing need to investigate effective mechanisms for utilizing these resources optimally. This paper tackles two main challenges inherent in developing such mechanisms. The initial challenge lies in the effective selection of the most advantageous models from a vast pool, given that exhaustively testing each pre-trained model is impractical. The second challenge stems from the diversity of pre-trained models, as it is common for a pre-trained model to have a different label space than that of the current task. This necessitates the development of a universal model reuse approach capable of integrating any pre-trained model, regardless of its unknown label set. To address these issues, we introduce a dual-phase framework named “Selective Cross-Label Distillation.” The first phase, termed model assessment, evaluates the semantic similarity between a potential pre-trained model and the target model through optimal transport. By determining the transportation cost, we pinpoint candidate models with lower costs to serve as our source models. The second phase, knowledge reuse, concentrates on minimizing the transportation cost between the chosen source models and the target model. This dual-phase approach enables efficient model selection and bridges the semantic gap between pre-trained models and the target task, leading to enhanced performance. The effectiveness of our framework in model selection and knowledge reuse is validated by experimental results.

title: Statistical Shape Model for Bone-Based Cartilage Prediction: Applicability in Healthy and Pathological Knees
authors: Anna Gounot, Marion Decrouez, Baptiste Dehaine, Valérie Burdin, Guillaume Dardenne
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
abstract: Objective: Patient-specific instrumentation (PSI) and robotic systems are being developed to improve Total Knee Arthroplasty outcomes in severe knee osteoarthritis. In image-based solutions, preoperative CT scans provide accurate bone geometry for planning, but not cartilage, which can affect intraoperative registration, thus surgical accuracy, if not addressed properly. Solutions exist, such as bone probing through cartilage, but they require additional surgical steps. As an alternative, Statistical Shape Models (SSMs) can automatically predict cartilage from bone shape. The present study evaluates whether SSMs can capture pathological variability, which features complex patterns of cartilage loss and bone deformation. Methods: Segmentations from the OAI-ZIB database were classified into healthy and pathological groups based on joint space narrowing. Coupled bone-cartilage SSMs were trained separately on these groups, for femur and tibia. The performance of the SSM was assessed by comparing bone fitting and cartilage prediction accuracy. Results: Bone fitting errors (median 0.27-0.32 mm) and cartilage prediction errors (median 0.41-0.49 mm, RMSE 0.66-0.79 mm) were comparable to the literature, with cartilage errors close to the inter-observer MRI manual segmentation variability reported for similar datasets. Predictive performance was similar for healthy and pathological cases, suggesting that SSMs can capture pathological variability. However, osteophytes were not fully captured, locally affecting prediction accuracy. Conclusion: The four coupled SSMs accurately reconstructed bone and predicted cartilage in both healthy and arthritic knees, suggesting robustness to pathological variability and suitability for clinical integration. Significance: The proposed method could be integrated into CT-based PSI or robotic workflows without requiring MRI or additional steps.

title: AIoT-Based Anomaly Detection and Monitoring System for Smart Poultry Farms
authors: Chithra R, Sandhiya R, Sangamithra J
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Poultry farming faces persistent challenges in maintaining optimal environmental conditions and ensuring bird health, often leading to productivity losses due to delayed detection of abnormal situations. This paper presents an AIoT-based Anomaly Detection and Monitoring System for Smart Poultry Farms that integrates Internet of Things sensing with artificial intelligence to enable real-time monitoring and automated control. Environmental parameters including temperature, humidity, and ammonia concentration are continuously collected using IoT sensors and transmitted to a cloud platform. A lightweight 1D-CNN Autoencoder model is employed to learn normal multivariate sensor patterns and identify anomalies based on reconstruction error without requiring labeled data. The proposed system also supports automated actuation of ventilation, water spraying, and heating mechanisms, along with mobile-based alerts and visualization. Experimental validation was conducted using 30 days of real sensor data, comprising approximately 18,000 sequences, collected from a prototype poultry setup. The system achieved over 96% anomaly detection accuracy and reduced average response time from 18.2 s to 5.5 s while improving energy utilization efficiency from 72.3% to 88.5%. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of integrating AI and IoT for early anomaly detection, reduced manual intervention, and improved environmental stability, contributing toward sustainable and intelligent poultry farm management.

title: Cloud Reliability Analysis via Log-based Anomaly Detection: An End-to-End Evaluation on HDFS Logs
authors: Thomas Kidu, Vignesh R. Babu, Alex Zhu
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Modern cloud systems generate high-velocity log streams across distributed services that provide fine-grained signals for reliability monitoring and incident triage, yet manual monitoring and rule-based alerting do not scale and often miss emerging failure modes. This paper evaluates log-based anomaly detection for cloud reliability on the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) dataset using a practical, reproducible pipeline built on structured LogHub artifacts. We compare unsupervised count-based baselines (Isolation Forest and an autoencoder) with a sequence-aware DeepLog-inspired model that fuses ordered event sequences and event-count statistics using bidirectional LSTM layers under heavy class imbalance. We also present an interactive monitoring dashboard that surfaces anomaly predictions and reliability-oriented summaries. On 575,061 HDFS block traces with 29 event templates, the final model reaches Precision 0.965, Recall 0.999, and F1 0.982 on the held-out test split.

title: Early Detection of Fetal Wellness and Distress using Biomedical Signal Processing
authors: Niyan Joseph Savio Marchon
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Non-invasive fetal electrocardiography (NIFECG) is a promising technique to continuously monitor fetal’s wellness and health status early in pregnancy. This intervention plays a critical role in reducing the likelihood of fetal injury and stillbirth. Robust extraction of the fetal signal from the maternal abdominal composite mixture of strong maternal and weak fetal electrocardiograms presents the greatest challenge to effective fetal ECG (FECG) monitoring. NIFECG can extract the fetal morphological parameters which can aid in diagnosing fetal distress ahead of delivery. In this research work, we highlight the use of biomedical signal processing method for noninvasive FECG extraction and evaluate their accuracy using the available open access databases from PhysioNet. Notably the advantages and limitations of these algorithms are highlighted include the key parameters that must be set to ensure their optimal performance. In this research study, by utilizing Independent Component Analysis (ICA) on the raw abdominal recordings, the maternal ECG (MECG) component can be substantially isolated and removed from the composite signal.The proposed straightforward method effectively and reliably performed fetal QRS (FQRS) detection on the five abdfecg records. The algorithm's efficacy was confirmed by a robust performance profile, including exceptional precision (98.43-100%) and sensitivity (88-100%), which culminated in a strong F1 score (96.7-100%). Our findings underscore the method's considerable proficiency in the accurate location of fetal R-peak indices. The goal is to develop sophisticated, noninvasive signal processing methods that provide the same morphological FECG data as invasive scalp electrodes, improving both safety and diagnostic depth in fetal monitoring.

title: Intelligent Pattern Analysis and Criminal Profiling
authors: Harinakshi C, Suchetha G, Shwetha S Shetty, Ananya R Shetty, Sparsh Rai, Dhananjaya B
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: The increasing complexity of modern crimes requires intelligent, data-driven solutions that enable effective analysis and criminal activity profiling. This paper describes a support system for criminal investigation based on AI, which aims at centralizing, analyzing, and visualizing criminal information for effective decision support. The support system integrates MongoDB for storing structured information and Neo4j for relationship-based analysis using graphs, enabling criminal networks, gang hierarchies, and behavioral trends identification. Crime hotspot, time-driven trends, and outliers are detected using advanced analytics modules, while a Flask-based backend makes module integration effortless. A chatbot interface based on natural language processing (NLP) allows investigators to submit queries to the support system using simple, natural-language questions, enabling effortless information access on vital information. Overall, the designed support system automates investigative activity, reduces manual efforts, and supports decision-taking based on data for law enforcing agencies.

title: A Comprehensive Survey of Deep Learning-Based PCB Defect Detection Techniques
authors: Darshan M R, N Muhammad Navmaan, Manoj Kumar V, Nagendra Prasad C N, Rajini S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Deep learning has become the most popular for Automated optical inspection (AOI) paradigm of printed circuit boards, offering more accuracy and scalability than conventional rule-based or manually designed feature methods. As summary of fourteen recent research that were published in the year between 2019 and 2025, which tackles the problem of detecting PCB defects utilizing both transformer-based and convolutional architectures. We classify methodologies into hybrid CNN–Transformer models, including GAN-based synthesis and semi-supervised learning, lightweight designs for edge deploy management and augmentation strategies, and one-stage and two-stage detectors. The survey in many instances, performs better than state-of-the-art solutions, highlighting other enduring difficulties like detecting minute flaws, interpretability, class imbalance, and generalization across datasets. We wrap up by suggesting a few areas for further study, such as explainable AI frameworks for dependable industrial adoption, effective transformer adjustments, standardized evaluation procedures, and physics-informed synthetic data generation.

title: An Enhanced Approach for Early Prediction of Data Anomalies in IoT
authors: Dina ElMenshawy
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 12th International Conference on Computing and Data Engineering
abstract: None

title: Application and Implementation of Adaptive Railway Signaling Technology
authors: Cuiying Yi
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 International Symposium on Robotics, Automation, and Mechatronics
abstract: None

title: Semantic NeRF Oriented 3D Object Counting for Industrial Fruit Harvesting
authors: Yimo Wang, Bin Kang, Jian Liu, Changyin Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Object counting is the core of perception components in the industry system. Nevertheless, real-world occlusion and dense clustering pose significant challenges to fruit harvesting. Existing methods closely rely on physics-agnostic 2D clustering with rigid thresholds, neglecting critical 3D geometric cues in complex scenes. Thus, they suffer from the multi-view double-counting issue. In this paper, we propose a 3D object counting framework that integrates semantic Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) with a physics-guided adaptive clustering algorithm. In particular, we employ a semantic NeRF to achieve implicit 3D scene reconstruction, which effectively isolates target objects from the background. Based on the semantic NeRF, we introduce a physics-guided adaptive clustering algorithm that exploits physically interpretable features, including surface normals and elevation gradients, for accurate unsupervised point cloud segmentation. Subsequently, an energy function optimization mechanism is utilized to autonomously aggregate multi-dimensional features for dynamically adjusting clustering thresholds, which enables the 3D counting to adapt to diverse fruit morphologies without manual parameter tuning. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed framework, which improves counting accuracy by an average of 9.0 percentage points (6.9 pp on synthetic, 7.2 pp on real-world, and 12.9 pp on Fuji) over the state-of-the-art 3-D baseline.

title: Multi-UAV Tracking under Partially Overlapping and Gapped Fields of View in Distributed Multi-sensor Networks
authors: Chengping Ma, Yunxiao Peng, Ci Wang, Xiaoxiao Zhuo, Wen Wu, Liang Tang, Yu Zhao
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: In distributed multi-sensor multi-target tracking networks with limited fields of view (FoVs), unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) targets may traverse uncovered regions, causing missing measurements, label inconsistency, and fragmented trajectories. To address this problem, we propose a cross-FoV association-based global track management (GTM) method for consistent and continuous global trajectory recovery. The proposed method classifies fused global tracks into active, pending, and dead sets according to their latest detection times and a gaptolerance threshold, enabling explicit identification of candidate fragmented track pairs. In the association stage, temporal order and predictive Mahalanobis gating are used to link fragmented tracks across FoV gaps while accounting for uncertainty growth during missing-observation intervals. In the reconstruction stage, inconsistent labels are unified and missing states are restored via retrodiction-based interpolation, which exploits both pre-gap and post-gap information for smoother trajectory reconstruction. Simulation results show that the proposed method mitigates FoV-gap-induced degradation and achieves more consistent and continuous tracking performance.

title: Careers: Finding Success in Industry as a Chip Designer: Academe and Industry Require Different Approaches
authors: Maysam Ghovanloo
publish: IEEE Spectrum
abstract: I have been an application-specific IC (ASIC) designer for almost three decades. Over that time, I've moved through the full academic trajectory, from graduate student to full professor; later, I transitioned to industry after an unsuccessful stint at entrepreneurship. When I made the switch to the private sector in 2019, I began focusing on a critically important aspect of the electronic industry: silicon intellectual property.

title: YOWOFormer: Bridging Video Transformers and CNN Detectors for One-Stage Spatio-Temporal Action Detection
authors: Vasu Thakaew, Sitapa Watcharapinchai, Punnarai Siricharoen
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The YOWO family provides one-stage dual-stream detectors for spatio-temporal action detection (STAD) by decoupling spatial (2D CNN) and temporal (3D CNN) backbones, enabling modality-specific pre-training and independent optimization. All existing variants, however, rely on 3D CNNs whose temporal receptive field is limited. Video transformers such as VideoMAE capture richer temporal representations, yet their token outputs are not directly compatible with CNN-based detectors. We present YOWOFormer, which replaces the 3D CNN stream with VideoMAE and introduces two bridging modules. Specifically, a Spatial Query Adapter (SQA) converts transformer tokens into spatial feature maps via learnable queries and cross-attention, and a Bidirectional Spatio-Temporal Fusion (BSTF) enables spatial and temporal features to mutually reinforce through bidirectional cross-attention.We further conduct systematic comparisons across four adapter designs, three fusion strategies, and the scaling of backbone capacity and clip length. On UCF101-24, YOWOFormer-L achieves 93.35% frame-level mAP, a new state of the art surpassing the previous best of 88.64%, and YOWOFormer-B attains 91.47% at 26.81 frames per second (FPS). On AVA v2.2, YOWOFormer-B and YOWOFormer-L improve by 2.34 and 5.39 percentage points over prior dual-stream 3D-CNN-based methods, respectively. Ablation studies demonstrate that temporal representation is the primary bottleneck in dual-stream STAD and that scaling video transformers yields more consistent gains than scaling 3D CNNs.

title: Restricted Target Wake Time Mechanism for Industrial Internet of Things Scenarios: Worst-Case Analysis
authors: Maria Shlapak, Ekaterina Stepanova, Andrey Lyakhov
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) involves deploying heterogeneous networks consisting of a large number of low-powered sensors that quickly report critical events and other devices serving saturated traffic. To combine power saving with fast and reliable transmissions, Wi-Fi 7 introduces the Restricted Target Wake Time (R-TWT) mechanism that aims to provide a strict quality of service (QoS) for real-time applications and thus can be used by IIoT sensors. To investigate the usability of R-TWT in IIoT networks, we develop an analytical model for a scenario of low-intensity critical events using worst-case analysis. This model allows finding settings of TWT-like mechanisms and channel access parameters that maximize the network throughput under QoS provision for sensors. Comparing throughputs for each mechanism, we find that under strict energy consumption requirements, R-TWT can be effectively used only with additional protection. For this protection, we propose the PR-TWT mechanism and prove that it significantly increases the throughput.

title: PI-FD: Property Inference Attacks on Federated Learning with Distillation via Synthetic Data Generation
authors: Yungi Cho, Miseon Yu, Jaejin Kim, Younghan Lee, Yunheung Paek
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Federated Learning with Distillation (FD) has clients upload output logits evaluated on a shared public dataset, instead of gradients computed on private data. Although FD is often believed to offer stronger privacy protection than traditional federated learning, recent studies have shown that a malicious server can still extract sensitive information, such as reconstructing private samples or inferring label distributions in a private dataset [1], [2]. This suggests that FD is not immune to sensitive information leakages, raising concerns of other significant privacy threats such as property inference attacks. Motivated by these concerns, we explore property inference attacks (PIA) which aim to infer property population information (e.g., demographics or disease prevalence) of a private dataset. However, most existing PIA methods rely on the same underlying distribution as the victim’s private data, which is often unavailable in FD because the server only observes the public dataset distribution. This distribution shift can significantly degrade the effectiveness of existing PIA methods. To overcome this limitation, we introduce PI-FD, a new PIA method that employs synthetic data generation in combination with novel loss functions. Experiments on four datasets with 14 properties show PI-FD reaches 94.07% attack accuracy, highlighting the need to mitigate PIA threats.

title: IEEE Connect
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Microwave Magazine

title: Toward Green Communication: Power-Efficient Beamforming for STAR-RIS-Aided BackCom
authors: Fan Yang, Yiyang Ni, Zhaoying Mo, Minxin Peng, Hui Zhang, Hongbo Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: This paper investigates a simultaneously transmitting and reflecting reconfigurable intelligent surface (STAR-RIS) assisted backscatter communication system. Our objective is to minimize the transmit power by jointly optimizing the transmit beamforming vector, the tags' reflection coefficients, the STAR-RIS coefficient matrices and energy splitting coefficients at the STAR-RIS, subject to the signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio constraints for decoding backscatter signals and the power constraints of the tags. We propose an alternating optimization algorithm to find near-optimal solution. Numerical results show that our proposed scheme can significantly reduce the transmit power even under the assumption that all reflection/transmission elements have the same amplitude coefficients.

title: MonoHPE: Monocular 3D Object Detection with Height Positional Encoding
authors: Chenxi Zhu, Zhi Li, Zhenyu Dai, Long Zheng, Weidong Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Monocular 3D object detection in autonomous driving has achieved revolutionary breakthroughs, primarily attributed to integrating perspective projection and geometric priors. Despite the continuous surge in overall performance, we argue that the subtle yet critical challenge of insufficient geometric constraints in the height dimension has not been sufficiently dissected, resulting in a systematic deviation between the bounding box height and the actual projected height, thereby nonlinearly amplifying the error in depth estimation. To uncover the profound essence of this error amplification mechanism, this paper employs a differentiable analytical framework to dissect the ratio dynamics of depth perception in perspective projection, providing a principled explanation of the sensitivity of depth perception to height deviations. Inspired by these insights, we propose MonoHPE, a method based on a hierarchical task learning paradigm. This method explicitly strengthens the geometric associations in the height dimension through an ingenious position modeling mechanism, achieving a breakthrough leap in the precision of depth estimation. Furthermore, to enhance the model’s adaptability in complex environments, we innovatively introduce an adaptive activation-guided loss, which directs the network to focus on semantically dominant regions, effectively suppressing foreground interference and noise contamination. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the KITTI dataset.

title: A Universal Framework for Programmable Multiattractor Chaotic Maps: Dynamics Analysis, DSP Realization, and Secure Application
authors: Yue Deng, Fang Yuan, Yuxia Li, Herbert Ho-Ching Iu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: This article proposes a universal framework transforming a broad class of bounded one-dimensional chaotic maps into a hyperchaotic three-dimensional map with programmable multiattractor topologies. A desired count of coexisting multiattractor can be extracted by a parametric piecewise-linear controller via integer parameters m and k. Theoretical analysis confirms the attractor layering mechanism and equilibrium instability. Moreover, feasibility and statistically satisfactory randomness are validated via digital signal processor implementation and National Institute of Standards and Technology tests. Finally, a chaos-based timed one-time password scheme demonstrates its application potential in secure communications.

title: Iterative Feedforward Tuning With Multiplicative Nonlinearity: Application to Linear-Motor-Driven Wafer Stage With Multiplicative Force Ripple
authors: Shuo Song, Li Li, Yue Dong, Yang Liu, Jiubin Tan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Iterative feedforward tuning (IFFT) plays important roles in precision motion control systems to ensure high motion performance and high task flexibility simultaneously. In this article, IFFT subject to the unknown multiplicative nonlinearity is further investigated. The proposed method adopts the inverse compensation mechanism for the multiplicative nonlinearity. Thus, a rational feedforward controller is developed with the numerator compensating for the reference trajectory and the additive disturbance and with the denominator compensating for the multiplicative nonlinearity. After feedforward parametrization, the IFFT algorithm is proposed to realize the simultaneous tuning for the numerator and denominator polynomial parameters. To enhance the convergence speed, an inner-loop iteration procedure is further utilized in each trial to obtain a more accurate data-based model required by IFFT by more fully utilizing the servo error. The superiorities of the proposed method in achieving faster, more accurate and more flexible tuning compared to relevant methods are finally verified through experimental results on a linear-motor-driven wafer stage with multiplicative force ripple.

title: Protection Efficiency-Based FIPP p -Cycle Design with Topology Partitioning in Mixed-Grid Optical Networks
authors: Chengzhi Song, Rongxi He, Xiaojing Chen
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: The migration from wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM) to elastic optical networks (EONs) leads to mixed-grid optical networks (MGONs), where fixed-grid and flex-grid nodes coexist. This hybrid infrastructure introduces critical survivability challenges: spectrum granularity mismatches increase protection resource overhead, while frequent optical-electrical-optical (O-E-O) conversions prolong recovery time. To address these issues, we propose a protection efficiency-based failure-independent path protecting (FIPP) p-cycle algorithm with topology partitioning (PE-TP-FIPP). The framework partitions the network into homogeneous domains based on node type and geographical proximity, thereby isolating capacity mismatches and avoiding unnecessary O-E-O conversions. Furthermore, this partitioning strategy balances protection efficiency and computational complexity by preventing excessive partitioning. Within this partitioned architecture, FIPP p-cycles are established for each connection request. A protection efficiency (PE) metric guides the selection of optimal p-cycles to minimize backup resources, while a cost of path (CoP) metric co-optimizes working path and protection allocation. To validate the proposed framework, performance baselines are first established in homogeneous environments, confirming the fundamental sharing efficiency of conventional shared backup path protection (SBPP) and FIPP p-cycles. Building upon these baselines, simulation results confirm that in MGONs, PE-TP-FIPP effectively mitigates the forced capacity expansion inherent in global routing, achieving highly competitive spare capacity redundancy (SCR) and shorter backup paths compared to global schemes. Consequently, the algorithm reduces the bandwidth blocking ratio by up to 55.31% and improves spectrum utilization by up to 26.2% versus benchmark schemes. Finally, extending the framework to accommodate differentiated services demonstrates its flexibility in fulfilling diverse service level agreements (SLAs).

title: Stealthy Merker Memory Attacks on Siemens S7 Programmable Logic Controllers
authors: Anup Suwal, Weiqing Sun, William Evans
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Industrial control systems (ICS) now routinely connect field devices, programmable logic controllers (PLCs), and supervisory networks, leaving safety-critical operations vulnerable to remote attacks. We examine a particular threat to Siemens S7 PLCs: runtime memory manipulation that targets process values and operator displays without modifying the control logic. Attackers can alter Data Blocks and Merker memory to skew the process behavior and falsify HMI readings while the user program remains unchanged. We implemented and tested three memory attacks on a lab water-tank controller to assess their effects and detection challenges. The findings reveal that carefully crafted memory-only attacks, especially those focused on Merker regions, can evade standard program-integrity checks and basic write-pattern monitoring.

title: PQ-MQTT: A Hybrid Post-Quantum Secure Communication Protocol for MQTT-Based IoT Networks
authors: Sohaib Saleem, Samir M. Umran, Zaid Ameen Abduljabbar, Pericle Perazzo, Uzair Saleem
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: The proliferation of Internet of Things (IoT) devices that use the Message Queuing Telemetry Transport (MQTT) protocol poses significant security challenges in the emerging quantum computing era. Current Transport Layer Security (TLS) implementations rely on classical cryptographic algorithms vulnerable to Shor's algorithm. This paper proposes PQ-MQTT, a hybrid post-quantum secure communication protocol that integrates ML-KEM-768 with X25519 for key encapsulation and a lightweight session-key derivation mechanism optimized for resource-constrained IoT publishers. We present a formal security analysis under the Quantum Random Oracle Model (QROM), demonstrating resilience against store-now-decryptlater attacks. Experimental evaluation on the Raspberry Pi 4, ESP32, and Arduino Nano 33 platforms shows that PQ-MQTT achieves handshake latencies of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$127.3 ~\text{ms}, 412.7 ~\text{ms}$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1,847.2 ~\text{ms}$</tex>, respectively, with 18.2 KB of additional memory overhead. A comparative analysis of pure post-quantum and classical TLS implementations shows energy reductions of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$23.5 \%-40.5 \%$</tex> relative to pure PQC, achieved through our hybrid optimization approach, while maintaining 94.2 % throughput efficiency.

title: EdgeStress: Real-Time Stress Detection Using IoT and Machine Learning
authors: Elwin Gonsalves, Farhan Bijapur, Sheela S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Stress detection has been one of the sectors of research that has grown very rapidly due to the fact that it has a direct influence on human-being, productivity, and decision-making. This paper introduces EdgeStress which is a real-time IoT-based system that monitors stress levels continuously in a non-invasive way through physiological signals from cheap sensors. In the presented system, an ESP32 microcontroller is used for the collection of Galvanic Skin Response (GSR) and heart rate (BPM) data. These data are then sent to a pre-trained Random Forest model which identifies stress as Low, Normal, or High. A dashboard built on Flask shows real-time parameters, previous trends, and interactive relaxation activities like guided breathing and humor-based interventions. The point of time here are the accurate classification of the real-time data and the responsive feedback which make the EdgeStress an efficient lightweight wellness monitoring and biofeedback tool.

title: Zero Trust IoT Security Platform
authors: Kanduri Aryaprasadh, Deepu M, B Devendra, Sheela S, Basavaraj Patil
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: The proposed Zero Trust IoT Security Platform with a hardware–SaaS hybrid security framework is presented, utilizing Zero Trust architecture, encryption, and monitoring. Every sensor connected to the proposed platform, such as PIR and DHT22, will operate as an independent node carrying out TinyML-based anomaly detection and sending encrypted data via AES/ECC to the cloud. The proposed hardware component is made up of modular IoT nodes driven by ESP32 microcontrollers. The cloud management layer is a SaaS platform built on Firebase, functioning as the centralized management layer providing real-time dashboards, device management, compliance tracking, and role-based access control. Extensive testing achieved over 95% intrusion detection accuracy, rapid deployment under one hour, and scalability beyond 100 nodes.

title: BEST: Blockchain And AI-Enabled EHR System For Ambulatory Care
authors: K. N. Rajanikanth, Dhanya Iyer, Abhiram Kasyap, Satish Doreswamy
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: The increasing demand for secure and efficient healthcare data management necessitates innovative solutions beyond traditional Electronic Health Record (EHR) systems. This work presents BEST, a blockchain-based EHR management platform enhanced with AI-powered speech transcription to address challenges in data security, accessibility, interoperability, and compliance brought forth by modern clinic-based care. By integrating the InterPlanetary File System (IPFS) for decentralized data storage and smart contracts on a private Ethereum blockchain for secure metadata management, BEST ensures tamper-proof and transparent medical data handling. The system employs Angular framework for the frontend user interface and Django backend framework for FHIR-compliant interoperability to ensure seamless interaction and functionality. BEST offers a secure, patient-centered platform that simplifies clinical documentation, is compliant with such regulations as HIPAA, GDPR, DPDP and is architecturally designed for frictionless data exchange based on FHIR standards, breaking down data silos between ambulatory settings and the broader healthcare ecosystem. The system design followed by an implementation with a Raspberry Pi platform and a general computing platform is demonstrated.

title: Machine Learning for Usable Security: A Survey of User-Centered Approaches
authors: Aney Rani Paul, Farjana Eishita, Mostafa Fouda
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Usable security is where Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) meets cybersecurity. At this intersection, the design of a security system considers how the user thinks, acts, and makes decisions within it. The use of Machine Learning (ML) in usable security has become an effective approach to meeting the needs of both the security system and the user, providing stronger security and a more engaging experience. This survey provides a high-level overview of how recent developments in ML applied to usable security have advanced in four main areas: Authentication, Phishing Detection, Biometric Systems, and Privacy Configurations. We classify the various ML approaches developed in these areas, as well as identify several usability metrics, such as cognitive load, user acceptance, and task efficiency, and the impact of the various ML-based approaches on those metrics. Additionally, we discuss the trade-offs between model precision and system efficiency, with emphasis on the risks associated with false positives and false negatives in security contexts. The study outlines existing challenges, including the need for Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI), resilience against adversarial attacks, and ethical data management. Finally, we conclude with proposed future research directions toward the design of deployable, human-centered ML-based security systems.

title: Human-Centered AI in Banking: Designing Adaptive Systems for Secure and Personalized Financial Engagements
authors: Muthu Selvam
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: This article presents the Human-Centered Adaptive AI Framework for intelligent financial systems that prioritise users' agency, privacy, transparency, and customisation. Behavioural profiling, adaptive personalisation, explainable artificial intelligence (XAI), and trust and privacy assurance create a responsive and ethical user experience tailored to each person's financial behaviours and goals. Federated learning and consent-driven data usage protect sensitive data while adapting. The system uses SHAP, LIME, and dynamic user interfaces depending on the knowledge and goal to make AI decisions understandable. An integrated examination of user surveys, prototype testing, and quantitative metrics showed improvements in trust perception, target completion rates, data-sharing compliance, and support efficiency. These studies show that human-centered design inside AI systems may make financial technology safer, more transparent, and more user-friendly.

title: AI-Augmented Debugging: Using LLMs and Execution Traces to Explain and Fix Software Failures
authors: Vishnupriya S Devarajulu
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Fault localization and debugging are major bottlenecks in the current software development that requires massive developer time and resources. Conventional methods of debugging require the use of manual inspection of execution logs and stack traces, which leads to long detectability and higher cognitive load. The current paper outlines a new framework that combines large language models with the analysis of execution traces and symbolic executing methods to automatically detect, analyze, and provide solutions to the failures in software. The system processes logs, stack traces, and source code to generate probable root cause locations, natural language explanations, and fix recommendations. A Comprehensive evaluation using realworld crash datasets demonstrates that this approach achieves superior fault localization accuracy while reducing mean time to resolution by over 41.2 % compared to conventional debugging tools.

title: Performance Evaluation of Machine Learning Models for Real-Time Anomaly Detection in MQTT-Based IoT Networks
authors: Shival Vyas, Anastasia Sukhanova, Yousef Fazea
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: The swift growth of Internet of Things (IoT) devices and the adoption of the Message Queuing Telemetry Transport (MQTT) protocol have allowed for very efficient, lightweight communication in IoT networks, even in resource-constrained environments. Yet, as the number of devices grows and, with it, the amount of data traffic, we encounter increased risks of cyberattacks, data corruption, and system failures. This work presents a performance evaluation of four machine learning models, Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and a feedforward Neural Network (FNN), for real-time anomaly detection in IoT networks that use MQTT as a communication protocol. The models were assessed along several dimensions: latency, detection accuracy, resource utilization, scalability, and robustness to noisy data. The RF model achieved the highest accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(95.8 \%)$</tex>, with low latency <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(35 ~\text{ms})$</tex> and moderate resource utilization (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 5 \%} \boldsymbol{\%}$</tex>). In contrast, SVM, while still achieving high accuracy (93.2 %), had much poorer latency <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(180 ~\text{ms})$</tex> and was very resource-intensive, making it unsuitable for deployment on real-time resource-limited IoT devices. KNN was even worse on all counts <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(89.8 \%, 250 ~\text{ms})$</tex>. FNN was better in accuracy (94 %) but was still too resource-intensive (95 % utilization) to be feasible for real-time effectuation on resourcelimited IoT devices.

title: Automated Oral Technical Evaluation Using Speech Recognition and Semantic Scoring
authors: Adithi Shetty, Mamatha Balipa, Aishwarya A Poojary
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Manual oral examination evaluation is often subjective and inconsistent, with a very high time demand from examiners. This paper addresses the challenge of automatically evaluating spoken technical answers through an automated assessment framework by combining ASR with semantic similarity techniques. In this proposed system, the speech of a learner first undergoes speech-to-text conversion by ASR. Then, NLP models, such as BERT and Sentence-BERT (SBERT), compute cosine similarity between the meaning of a transcribed answer and a reference response. In the final score, both content relevance and fluency contribute to a more objective and balanced evaluation. Experiments show that this design decreases evaluator bias, improves scoring consistency, and presents a scalable method applicable both within educational institutions and during technical interviews.

title: Sensorless Control of PMSMs at Ultralow Speed: An Adaptive Position Estimation Strategy Based on Reconstructed Flux Linkage
authors: Pengcheng Du, Bo Wang, Dianguo Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Model-based sensorless control of permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSMs) faces challenges at ultralow speeds and during smooth zero-speed crossing. This is primarily attributed to insufficient utilization of model information and inherent stability defect. To address these issues, a position error estimation method is proposed based on gradient descent adaptation. First, a hybrid current–voltage stator flux observer is designed as a reference model under a convergence assumption. To fully utilize the model information, the estimated position error is incorporated into both the permanent magnet and the saliency terms via a reconstructed flux linkage. A quadratic flux-error cost function is then minimized by an Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)-rule-based adaptive law to yield the estimated position error, while speed and position are recovered by a phase-locked loop. The coupled observer–adaptation dynamics are analyzed, and input-to-state stability is established in the presence of nonlinearities and disturbances. Practical enhancements, including integral leakage and virtual signal injection, are incorporated, and concise tuning guidelines are provided to enhance the reproducibility. Experiments on a 2.2-kW PMSM test rig include step transients, ultralow-speed operation, and motoring-generating transition. The proposed method maintains dynamic characteristics and delivers improved ultralow-speed performance over two representative model-based methods.

title: Deposition of Titanium Nitride on Inner Surface of Grade B API 5L Steel Pipes by Cathodic Cage Plasma Nitriding Technique
authors: I. M. Sousa, F. A. Sousa, R. R. M. de Sousa, J. C. P. Barbosa, F. O. de Araújo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: Grade B American Petroleum Institute (API) 5L tubes are those most used in the construction of pipelines and production lines for oil transportation. These pipes may have internal corrosion due to several factors, for example, the reaction of components such as O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>, H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>S, and CO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> with the metal surface. In this work, we used the cathodic cage plasma nitriding (CCPN) technique to coat samples of API 5L Grade B steel tubes with titanium nitride (TiN) films, as an alternative method for the uniform deposition of this film. The cathodic cage was manufactured using pure grade 1 commercial titanium, and treatments were performed at 1.5 mbar, with temperatures of 400 and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$450~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C and treatment times of 3, 4, and 5 h. A gas mixture composed of 40% <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\mathrm {H}}_{2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, 40% Ar, and 20% N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> was used. The treated samples were characterized by grazing incidence X-ray diffraction (GIXRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and Raman spectroscopy. It was found that increasing the treatment temperature with constant treatment time increases the thickness of the deposited layer, and these results indicate the efficiency of this process on formation of a uniform layer composed mainly of TiN.

title: Egress Accommodation for Factory-Assembled Structures: CODE REQUIREMENTS AND STRATEGIES TO MEET THEM
authors: Alan Clemenson, Alex Bresnan, Luca Magenes, Joe Coder, Mitchell Roegele
publish: IEEE Industry Applications Magazine
abstract: THE NATIONAL ELECTRICAL CODE (NEC) IS PERIODICALLY revised to enhance efficiency, account for technology development, and improve standard safety practices. Code changes made in the 2023 edition significantly impact various industries, particularly regarding the use of prefabricated structures in brownfield applications. One key amendment is the egress requirement added to sections 110.26 and 110.33 for equipment under and over 1,000 V. This code requirement is to be treated independently from working clearance and specifically necessitates that 24 in of egress are made available in all aisles while equipment doors are concurrently open. The implication is that buildings will be designed with larger footprints and additional shipping splits and will likely become more expensive to construct. Engineers must adapt to provide innovative design methodologies to meet these demands. This article reviews the egress code requirements, impacts on industries, and some of the devised strategies to meet standards and promote a safer electrical infrastructure.

title: Spatiotemporal Modulation-Based Nonreciprocal Filter for Full-Duplex Wireless Systems with UAV Communication Applications
authors: Jiaxing Hao, Bin Wang, Feng Ding, Hong Xu, Yuewei Liu, Jingbo Duan, Xuesong Bai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Nonreciprocal filters in this current era with advanced suppression and directional transmission capabilities offer remarkable advantages for wireless systems with particular relevance to RF front-end applications. Full-duplex wireless communication systems demand RF front-end components capable of simultaneous transmission and reception at the same frequency while maintaining strong isolation between the two signal paths. By allowing asymmetric signal flow, these filters can mitigate self-interference between co-located transmitters and receivers for full-duplex operations without degradation in link quality. This article presents a spatiotemporal modulation-based nonreciprocal bandpass filter designed for full-duplex wireless systems, with particular relevance to UAV communication applications. The proposed technology comprises a third-order inter-digital filtering segment, with robust nonreciprocal electromagnetic wave propagation achieved through time-modulation mechanism. Initially, a prototype has been developed and rigorously tested at 2.4 GHz frequency before deployment, which demonstrates an insertion loss of merely -2.58 dB. Simultaneously, arbitrary switching of the operating frequency across 2.3-2.5 GHz range is achieved by regulating the DC bias voltage imposed on each modulated resonator within the filter. The designed nonreciprocal filter is capable to perform the functionalities of both filter as well as an isolator, to facilitate efficient directional transmission of in-band useful signals while mitigating interference from out-of-band spurious signals within a compact planar configuration. The proposed design offers a practically viable, low-complexity RF solution for interference mitigation in full-duplex wireless platforms including UAV networks, IoT devices, and advanced communication systems.

title: Robust Single-Packet Physical Layer Authentication for UAVs: A Spatial Challenge-Response Approach
authors: Yonggu Lee, Jinyoung Lee
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: This letter proposes a spatial challenge-response physical layer authentication scheme for UAVs under a stringent single-packet scenario. To ensure reliability from a single observation, we develop a rational detection framework leveraging micro-scale spatial channel statistics to resolve the likelihood singularity and prove that the resulting test is uniformly most powerful invariant. Furthermore, it is analytically shown that even under an optimal spoofing strategy, the adversarial signal inherently suffers from a variance deficit, ensuring robust detectability even under perfect power matching. Finally, we derive an optimal altitude for authentication by balancing scattering-based discriminability against path loss, providing robust protection for latency-critical 3D aerial networks.

title: Your Virtual Twin Could One Day Save Your Life: Replicas of the Human Heart and Other Organs are Enabling Virtual Surgery and Clinical Trials
authors: Steve Levine
publish: IEEE Spectrum
abstract: One morning in May 2019, a cardiac surgeon stepped into the operating room at Boston Children's Hospital more prepared than ever before to perform a high-risk procedure to rebuild a child's heart. The surgeon was experienced, but he had an additional advantage: He had already performed the procedure on this child dozens of times—virtually. He knew exactly what to do before the first cut was made. Even more important, he knew which strategies would provide the best possible outcome for the child whose life was in his hands.

title: 5 Questions: Leontien Talboom: What it Takes to Preserve Floppy Disk Data
authors: Gwendolyn Rak
publish: IEEE Spectrum
abstract: Floppy disks are several decades old—many of the disks are degrading, and the data stored on them is at risk of being lost. In response, Leontien Talboom, a technical analyst at Cambridge University Libraries and Archives, led a project preserving floppy disks and their data called “Future Nostalgia,” which concluded in January.

title: Dual Prompt Learning for Unsupervised Degradation-Resistant Image Fusion
authors: Borui Li, Yong Ma, Fan Fan, Xiaoguang Mei, Jiayi Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Mining source information and resisting degradation in multi-modal image fusion is of great challenge. Current approaches suffer from inaccurate mining prior and poor scene generalization, limiting their ability to handle the extreme condition image fusion. In this paper, we propose a dual degradation-resistant prompt learning fusion framework, termed as DRFusion, to address these issues. Firstly, we design the Dual Prompt Degradation-Resistant Network (DPDNet), which leverages large language-vision model to perform multi-level mining perception. To be detailed, it adopts the large-scale Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP) to assist in extracting degraded features through dual guidance of semantics and perception. The prior knowledge in CLIP serves as a bridge between high-quality open-world images and learnable prompts, solving the problem of lacking paired data in multi-modal image fusion tasks with degradation. Subsequently, we develop the Efficient Information Aggregation Network (EIANet) to integrate the extracted multi-level degradation features and source images in a hierarchical manner, generating the fused image. Additionally, we designed the smooth illumination loss and infrared contrast retention loss, with the consideration of source images and degradation features to reduce the discrepancy between open-world semantic information and source images. Finally, our method produces fused image that exhibit enhanced clarity, brightness, and distinctly highlighted infrared targets. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of effectiveness, robustness, and reliability.

title: Prediction-Assisted Multi-UAV Online Service Migration and Trajectory Control for MEC-Empowered Vehicular Networks
authors: Wei Feng, Wenyang Gao, Jianping Yao, Longyu Zhou, Chenggang Yan, Tony Q. S. Quek
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: The rapid proliferation of intelligent vehicular networks and mobile edge computing (MEC) demands substantial improvements in computation efficiency, latency reduction, and service continuity, especially under high mobility and stochastic task generation. Conventional single-uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) solutions with static trajectory design lack the adaptability required in such dynamic environments. To address these challenges, we develop a collaborative multi-UAV optimization framework that jointly coordinates continuous UAV movement and service migration decisions. The framework incorporates a stacked long short-term memory (LSTM) model to predict short-term vehicle trajectories from historical motion data, which enables mobility-aware planning and reduces unnecessary cross-UAV migrations. To balance long-term migration cost and instantaneous delay, a Lyapunov-based technique is integrated into the per-slot optimization. Furthermore, a multiagent deep deterministic policy gradient (MADDPG) algorithm is adopted to facilitate cooperative policy learning across UAV agents in stochastic and partially observable environments. By unifying prediction, trajectory design, and adaptive migration control, the proposed method effectively adapts to time-varying network conditions. Extensive simulations demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms baseline schemes in terms of delay, reward stability, and scalability under dynamic network loads.

title: Composite Error Learning Estimator for Wiener–Hammerstein Systems With Time-Delay Under Binary-Valued Measurements
authors: Linwei Li, Jie Zhang, Xuemei Ren, Xin Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: System modeling and parameter estimation of quantized systems are crucial for economizing communication resources and reducing the cost of sensors in the current information age of general communication and digital technology. In the presence of noise, binary-valued sensor, and nonlinearity, such an estimation is even more challenging. To address this challenge, this study introduces an alternative composite error learning estimator for nonlinear Wiener–Hammerstein systems with a time delay that is subject to binary-valued observations. Unlike existing techniques that use conventional single-error learning pattern, the proposed scheme leverages composite error data, including both pure estimation error and initial value error data, to develop a new parameter estimator, which achieves a high estimation performance compared with some available algorithms. In particular, the proposed compensation variable is applied to obtain the pure estimation error data by compensating the quantized data matrix on the effect of the estimator, thus avoiding the inverse problem of the data matrix. In addition, the provided online validation mechanism for the persistent excitation condition not only ensures the convergence of the proposed method, but can also select the type of input signal. Moreover, the system parameters and time delay can be estimated simultaneously, and the parameter error convergence is rigorously proven. The introduced method is illustrated and validated on a binary-valued Wiener–Hammerstein system with time delay and a permanent magnet synchronous motor drive plant.

title: Securing IoT Devices in the Quantum Era: AI-Based Detection with Hybrid Post-Quantum Encryption
authors: Zina Balani, Maryam Sarmad Mohammed Ali
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: With the advancement of quantum computing, the long-term security of IoT systems is increasingly challenged, particularly due to the vulnerability of traditional public-key cryptography. This study integrates artificial intelligence (AI) with a hybrid strategy that involves Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) and Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) to address this challenge. Incoming traffic from the TON-IoT dataset is processed by AI using XGBoost, Random Forest, and One-Class SVM as anomaly-detection filters. Only traffic classified as normal is forwarded to the encryption phase. Postquantum key establishment is performed using Kyber-512, and the resulting shared secret is combined with simulated BB84based QKD key material via a key-derivation function (KDF) to derive a 256-bit session key, which is then used with AES-256GCM for authenticated IoT data encryption. Experimental results demonstrate that the hybrid algorithm, along with the AI, can effectively improve IoT security. Upon optimization of the parameter <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta(=1.0)$</tex>, the Delivery Purity is 0.92, the Data Delivery rate is 0.80, and the Secure Delivery <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{F} \beta$</tex> score is 0.94 compared to Delivery Purity of 0.77, Delivery Rate of 1.0, and Secure Delivery Fβ score of 0.87, respectively, without AI.

title: Theoretical Analysis of Fidelity and Interpretability Trade-Offs in Explainable Artificial Intelligence
authors: KavyaSri Koppanati, Akshitha Jakkula, Muhammad Zubair Khan, Belinda Copus
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) aims to make machine learning models more transparent and understandable to human users. However, increasing interpretability often comes at the cost of reduced fidelity, where explanations no longer fully reflect a model's internal decision-making process. This paper presents a conceptual analysis of the trade-off between interpretability and fidelity through a comparative examination of two widely used XAI methods, LIME and SHAP. Rather than proposing a new explanation algorithm, the study synthesizes existing theoretical perspectives and uses illustrative experiments to ground the discussion. The analysis highlights how cognitive and computational constraints limit the simultaneous maximization of interpretability and fidelity, offering insights to guide the design and evaluation of explainable models.

title: BEHANA : Enhancing Women’s Safety through AI-Powered Solutions
authors: Ankitha Shetty, Ayush Chaudhary, Navaneeth Shetty, Aditya Patil T, Mahammad Shahil
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Women’s safety is one of the raising concerns in the contemporary era. Several cases of harassment, exploitation, and even threat to human life. Rising concerns on such activities lead to multiple solutions have been proposed so far; this paper aims to propose a solution of a mobile application that is powered by modern Machine Learning techniques that empowers safety by providing several safety and precautionary features. The primary intention of the proposal is to equip women with the best solution to such quotidian complications. Using the Machine Learning feature of the proposed system, the application helps minimize the risks by predicting risk prone scenarios by leveraging the community knowledge and analyzing behavioral patterns. Such pro-active features enable women to navigate safely through places, suggest safer routes, and also initiate emergency protocols such as SOS and SMS. Adding on to these features with the integration of natural language processing (NLP) for analyzing the women community confidence of particular area or location based on their posts on the application indirectly helps rest of the female community from facing potential dangers. Combining the merits of these features, the proposed system provides a strong, robust, and reliable framework that not only detects potential risks but also prevents those risks. Furthermore, the proposed mobile application emphasizes usability for all spectrum of users, making it simple and in a user-centric design, making it accessible, intuitive, and reliable regardless of their technical expertise.

title: EdgeBlockAI - An Offline, AI-Assisted Blockchain Framework for Supply Chain Anomaly Detection
authors: Bhumika G, Kambhampati Aasrika, Fathimathul Zenha KP, Kanika Chauda, Sheela S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: All over the world, counterfeits are causing losses of billions annually. Standard blockchain infrastructures can create immutable audit trails; however, they are cloud-dependent, have high latency, do not pre-validate data, and require complex consensus mechanisms, which makes deployment on edge devices difficult. EdgeBlockAI is an offline, AI-assisted blockchain framework that uses edge-deployed anomaly detection and a lightweight DAG-based local ledger for supply chain authentication on resource constrained hardware. The data fusion is done using RFID identification, environmental DHT22 sensor and USB camera. The visual inspection is done using quantised AI models (Tiny CNN model for image data, LSTM model for sensor data) implemented on Raspberry Pi Zero 2W (512MB RAM). Unlike traditional blockchains that rely on network connectivity at all times, EdgeBlockAI features a local directed acyclic graph (DAG) ledger. Therefore, this ledger operates independently when the network crashes, especially in a localised network. Moreover, when the connection is restored, EdgeBlockAI leverages smart batching and conflict-free merging. Using smart filtering helps consume 60-80% less bandwidth through confidence thresholding and duplicate detection while ensuring that critical anomalies get uploaded right away. Our experimental results showed 2-3 times faster transaction execution than conventional blockchain (3-4 seconds end-to-end) and minimal data loss in successful offline operation. The model achieves 93% confidence in detecting anomalies while consuming 7.5-10W power, thus validating its practical feasibility for large-scale deployment in remote warehouses and resource-limited environments.

title: Prediction of Cancer Drug Response Based on Hypergraph Convolutional Network and Contrastive Learning
authors: Haitao Ma, Zhihao Wang, Yukai Jia, Ze Li, Yuhai Zhao, Changyong Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: Objective: Accurate prediction of cancer drug responses is essential for advancing precision oncology. This work aims to improve generalization and robustness in drug response prediction by modeling complex drug–cell line interactions. Methods: We propose HypergraphCDR, a Hypergraph Convolutional Network model with Hypergraph Contrastive Learning for cancer drug response prediction. Multiomics features of cancer cell lines are first compressed using an autoencoder. A Hypergraph is then constructed to capture high-order relationships between drugs and cancer cell lines, with Hypergraph Convolutional Networks generating drug embeddings. In parallel, cell line embeddings are generated via a neural network. Drug and cell line embeddings are jointly optimized and integrated into a regression framework using a combination of supervised regression loss and contrastive loss. Results: Extensive experiments demonstrate that HypergraphCDR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of Pearson Correlation Coefficient (${PCC}$), Spearman Correlation Coefficient (${SCC}$), and Coefficient of Determination (${R}^{2}$). Moreover, in independent experiments involving unseen drugs and unseen cell lines, as well as tissue-specific evaluations, HypergraphCDR exhibits superior generalization performance compared with recent baselines. Conclusion: By explicitly modeling higher-order drug–cell line relationships, HypergraphCDR substantially enhances the accuracy and robustness of cancer drug response prediction. Significance: This study provides an effective and generalizable computational framework for drug response prediction, supporting reliable drug screening and treatment strategy development in precision medicine.

title: Interpretable Multispectral Deep Learning for Non-Destructive Fruit Quality Monitoring in Agriculture 5.0
authors: Peicyuan Su, Ho-feng Peng, Chengen Wu, Shenghsien Hsieh, Mingyi Lin
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 12th International Conference on Computing and Data Engineering
abstract: None
